Dance Hall (right) and Worship Hall (left background) }} {{osm box|w|48434848}} [[File:Muko-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Mukō Shrine''' (向日神社, Mukō Jinja), also known as '''Mukō Myōjin''', is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in Mukōchō Kitayama, [[Mukō]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]]. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myōjin Taisha and small shrine) and a former prefectural shrine. == Deities == * Main deities: {{ill|Toshigami|ja|年神}}, {{ill|Yakusanoikazuchi|ja|火雷大神}}, {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命}}, {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}} == History == Originally, Mukō Shrine consisted of two separate shrines on Mukō Mountain: {{ill|Muka Shrine|ja|向神社}} (Uqqer Shrine) and {{ill|Honoikazuchi Shrine|ja|火雷神社}} (Lower Shrine). Both are ancient shrines mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳}}, with Honoikazuchi Shrine being one of the candidates for the {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社}} "Otokuni-zazuchi Shrine" (other candidates include {{ill|Sumiyagashira Shrine|ja|角宮神社}} in {{ill|Nagaokakyō|ja|長岡京市}}). Muka Shrine is believed to have originated when Toshigami (Mukō Kami) stayed on Mukō Mountain and promoted rice cultivation. Initially, the shrine was located atop the {{ill|Gotsukahara Burial Mound|ja|五塚原古墳}}, 500 meters north of its current location. In 718 (2nd year of {{ill|Yōrō|ja|養老}}), the shrine was relocated to its current position south of the {{ill|Moto-Inari Burial Mound|ja|元稲荷古墳}}. Honoikazuchi Shrine is said to have been established when Emperor Jimmu enshrined the deity Honoikazuchi during his journey from {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}} to {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|山背国}}. It is also believed to have been founded around 518 (12th year of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|継体天皇}}), when Keitai built the {{ill|Otokuni Palace|ja|乙訓宮}}. In 718, Honoikazuchi Shrine incorporated Tamayorihime and Emperor Jimmu as deities. In 784 (3rd year of {{ill|Enryaku|ja|延暦}}), the establishment of {{ill|Nagaoka-kyō|ja|長岡京}} brought the imperial palace, Nagaoka-no-miya, to the eastern foot of Muka Shrine. The chief priest of Honoikazuchi Shrine joined Emperor Go-Toba's forces during the {{ill|Jōkyū Rebellion|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221 but later fled to the {{ill|Rokuto-no-shō|ja|六人部荘}} estate in present-day {{ill|Fukuchiyama|ja|福知山市}}. After returning, the shrine's influence waned, and in 1275 (1st year of {{ill|Kenji|ja|建治}}), Muka Shrine merged with Honoikazuchi Shrine, forming Mukō Shrine and becoming the area's guardian shrine. The current main hall was rebuilt in 1422 (29th year of {{ill|Ōei|ja|応永}}) with the cooperation of seven surrounding villages. During the Muromachi period, Mukō Shrine became a rallying point for local {{ill|Tsuchi Ikki|ja|土一揆}} peasant uprisings before they marched on Kyoto. In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine. In 1961 (36th year of {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}}), the surrounding hill was partially excavated for the construction of the {{ill|Meishin Expressway|ja|名神高速道路}}. Today, the shrine and Moto-Inari Burial Mound remain as a small hill. In recent years, the shrine has been surrounded by a cherry blossom park, partially restoring the area’s former beauty. The main hall of Tokyo's {{ill|Meiji Shrine|ja|明治神宮}} is modeled after Mukō Shrine's main hall, scaled up by 1.5 times. == Grounds == [[File:Mukō-jinja-Cherry Blossoms of aqqroach to a shrine.jpg|thumb|Path lined with cherry blossoms leading to the shrine]] From {{ill|Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|ja|京都府道・大阪府道67号西京高槻線}} (Astro Street), visitors enter a long, stone-paved path lined with cherry, azalea, and maple trees. The incline is scenic but shared with vehicles, requiring caution. * Main Hall (Important Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1422 during the Muromachi period. Representative of nagare-zukuri architecture. The model for the Meiji Shrine. ''' Main Hall Shelter (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842 (13th year of {{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保}}). * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842. * North and South Gates (Registered Tangible Cultural Properties) – Built in 1842. * Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1842. * Kagura Hall – Rebuilt in 2003 after the original structure (built in 1625) was lost in a 2002 fire. * Guest Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Relocated from a youth training facility in Osaka around 1965. * Purification Pavilion (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Late 18th-century reconstruction. === Auxiliary Shrines === * Ancestral Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1688 (1st year of {{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}}). * Five Shrines (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Dedicated to various deities. * Other shrines include {{ill|Goryō Shrine|ja|御霊神社}}, {{ill|Tenmangū|ja|天満宮}}, and Katsuyama Inari Shrine. == Festivals == * festival – Second Sunday in May * Sōmen Festival == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall and five ridge plaques. * Paper manuscript of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} (Lower Chapter, Age of the Gods) – A 14th-century transcription. === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Multiple structures, including the Main Hall Shelter, North and South Gates, and various auxiliary shrines. == Access == * Nearest station: [[Hankyu Kyoto Line]] [[Nishimukō Station]], 500 meters northwest. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Mutsume Koreka|ja|六人部是香}} – Late Edo-period scholar and former shrine priest. == External Links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20190101000000/http://www.geocities.jp/mukojinjahp/index.htm Mukō Shrine Official Website (archived)] * {{Commonscat-inline}} {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Tangible Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Historic sites in Mukō]] [[Category:Cherry blossom spots in Japan]] Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Muko Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Muko Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Muko Shrine}}[[Category:qq]] [[category:Mukō Shrine]][[Category:Mukō Shrine]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Muko Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Muko Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Muko Shrine]][[Category:qq]] {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Mukō Shrine | location = Mukōchō Kitayama 65, [[Mukō]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]], Japan | Latitude degrees = 34 | Latitude minutes = 56 | Latitude seconds = 38.8 | N/S = N | Longitude degrees = 135 | Longitude minutes = 41 | Longitude seconds = 49.6 | E/W = E | Map country code = JP | Map = Japan Kyoto | Main deity = {{ill|Toshigami|ja|年神}}, {{ill|Honoikazuchi no Ōkami|ja|火雷大神}}, {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命}}, {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}} | Shrine rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社}} and small shrine), former {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|府社}} | Main hall = Three-bay nagare-zukuri (Important Cultural Property) | festival = Second Sunday in May | Religious rituals = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Muko-jinja, shaden.jpg|280px]]
Dance Hall (right) and Worship Hall (left background) }} {{osm box|w|48434848}} [[File:Muko-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Mukō Shrine''' (向日神社, Mukō Jinja), also known as '''Mukō Myōjin''', is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in Mukōchō Kitayama, [[Mukō]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]]. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myōjin Taisha and small shrine) and a former prefectural shrine. == Deities == * Main deities: {{ill|Toshigami|ja|年神}}, {{ill|Yakusanoikazuchi|ja|火雷大神}}, {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命}}, {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}} == History == Originally, Mukō Shrine consisted of two separate shrines on Mukō Mountain: {{ill|Muka Shrine|ja|向神社}} (Uqqer Shrine) and {{ill|Honoikazuchi Shrine|ja|火雷神社}} (Lower Shrine). Both are ancient shrines mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳}}, with Honoikazuchi Shrine being one of the candidates for the {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社}} "Otokuni-zazuchi Shrine" (other candidates include {{ill|Sumiyagashira Shrine|ja|角宮神社}} in {{ill|Nagaokakyō|ja|長岡京市}}). Muka Shrine is believed to have originated when Toshigami (Mukō Kami) stayed on Mukō Mountain and promoted rice cultivation. Initially, the shrine was located atop the {{ill|Gotsukahara Kofun|ja|五塚原古墳}}, 500 meters north of its current location. In 718 (2nd year of {{ill|Yōrō|ja|養老}}), the shrine was relocated to its current position south of the {{ill|Moto-Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳}}. Honoikazuchi Shrine is said to have been established when Emperor Jimmu enshrined the deity Honoikazuchi during his journey from {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province }} to {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|山背国}}. It is also believed to have been founded around 518 (12th year of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|継体天皇}}), when Keitai built the {{ill|Otokuni Palace|ja|乙訓宮}}. In 718, Honoikazuchi Shrine incorporated Tamayorihime and Emperor Jimmu as deities. In 784 (3rd year of {{ill|Enryaku|ja|延暦}}), the establishment of {{ill|Nagaoka-kyō|ja|長岡京}} brought the imperial palace, Nagaoka-no-miya, to the eastern foot of Muka Shrine. The chief priest of Honoikazuchi Shrine joined Emperor Go-Toba's forces during the {{ill|Jōkyū Rebellion|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221 but later fled to the {{ill|Rokuto-no-shō|ja|六人部荘}} estate in present-day {{ill|Fukuchiyama|ja|福知山市}}. After returning, the shrine's influence waned, and in 1275 (1st year of {{ill|Kenji|ja|建治}}), Muka Shrine merged with Honoikazuchi Shrine, forming Mukō Shrine and becoming the area's guardian shrine. The current main hall was rebuilt in 1422 (29th year of {{ill|Ōei|ja|応永}}) with the cooperation of seven surrounding villages. During the Muromachi period, Mukō Shrine became a rallying point for local {{ill|Tsuchi Ikki|ja|土一揆}} peasant uprisings before they marched on Kyoto. In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine. In 1961 (36th year of {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}}), the surrounding hill was partially excavated for the construction of the {{ill|Meishin Expressway|ja|名神高速道路}}. Today, the shrine and Moto-Inari Kofun remain as a small hill. In recent years, the shrine has been surrounded by a cherry blossom park, partially restoring the area’s former beauty. The main hall of Tokyo's {{ill|Meiji Shrine|ja|明治神宮}} is modeled after Mukō Shrine's main hall, scaled up by 1.5 times. == Grounds == [[File:Mukō-jinja-Cherry Blossoms of aqqroach to a shrine.jpg|thumb|Path lined with cherry blossoms leading to the shrine]] From {{ill|Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|ja|京都府道・大阪府道67号西京高槻線}} (Astro Street), visitors enter a long, stone-paved path lined with cherry, azalea, and maple trees. The incline is scenic but shared with vehicles, requiring caution. * Main Hall (Important Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1422 during the Muromachi period. Representative of nagare-zukuri architecture. The model for the Meiji Shrine. ''' Main Hall Shelter (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842 (13th year of {{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保}}). * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842. * North and South Gates (Registered Tangible Cultural Properties) – Built in 1842. * Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1842. * Kagura Hall – Rebuilt in 2003 after the original structure (built in 1625) was lost in a 2002 fire. * Guest Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Relocated from a youth training facility in Osaka around 1965. * Purification Pavilion (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Late 18th-century reconstruction. === Auxiliary Shrines === * Ancestral Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1688 (1st year of {{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}}). * Five Shrines (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Dedicated to various deities. * Other shrines include {{ill|Goryō Shrine|ja|御霊神社}}, {{ill|Tenmangū|ja|天満宮}}, and Katsuyama Inari Shrine. == Festivals == * festival – Second Sunday in May * Sōmen Festival == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall and five ridge plaques. * Paper manuscript of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} (Lower Chapter, Age of the Gods) – A 14th-century transcription. === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Multiple structures, including the Main Hall Shelter, North and South Gates, and various auxiliary shrines. == Access == * Nearest station: [[Hankyu Kyoto Line]] [[Nishimukō Station]], 500 meters northwest. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Mutsume Koreka|ja|六人部是香}} – Late Edo-period scholar and former shrine priest. == External Links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20190101000000/http://www.geocities.jp/mukojinjahp/index.htm Mukō Shrine Official Website (archived)] * {{Commonscat-inline}} {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Tangible Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Historic sites in Mukō]] [[Category:Cherry blossom spots in Japan]] Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Muko Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Muko Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Muko Shrine}}[[Category:qq]] [[category:Mukō Shrine]][[Category:Mukō Shrine]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Muko Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Muko Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Muko Shrine]][[Category:qq]] {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Mukō Shrine | location = Mukōchō Kitayama 65, [[Mukō]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]], Japan | Latitude degrees = 34 | Latitude minutes = 56 | Latitude seconds = 38.8 | N/S = N | Longitude degrees = 135 | Longitude minutes = 41 | Longitude seconds = 49.6 | E/W = E | Map country code = JP | Map = Japan Kyoto | Main deity = {{ill|Toshigami|ja|年神}}, {{ill|Honoikazuchi no Ōkami|ja|火雷大神}}, {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命}}, {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}} | Shrine rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社}} and small shrine), former {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|府社}} | Main hall = Three-bay nagare-zukuri (Important Cultural Property) | festival = Second Sunday in May | Religious rituals = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Muko-jinja, shaden.jpg|280px]]
Dance Hall (right) and Worship Hall (left background) }} {{osm box|w|48434848}} [[File:Muko-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Mukō Shrine''' (向日神社, Mukō Jinja), also known as '''Mukō Myōjin''', is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in Mukōchō Kitayama, [[Mukō]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]]. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myōjin Taisha and small shrine) and a former prefectural shrine. == Deities == * Main deities: {{ill|Toshigami|ja|年神}}, {{ill|Yakusanoikazuchi|ja|火雷大神}}, {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命}}, {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}} == History == Originally, Mukō Shrine consisted of two separate shrines on Mukō Mountain: {{ill|Muka Shrine|ja|向神社}} (U11er Shrine) and {{ill|Honoikazuchi Shrine|ja|火雷神社}} (Lower Shrine). Both are ancient shrines mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳}}, with Honoikazuchi Shrine being one of the candidates for the {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社}} "Otokuni-zazuchi Shrine" (other candidates include {{ill|Sumiyagashira Shrine|ja|角宮神社}} in {{ill|Nagaokakyō|ja|長岡京市}}). Muka Shrine is believed to have originated when Toshigami (Mukō Kami) stayed on Mukō Mountain and promoted rice cultivation. Initially, the shrine was located atop the {{ill|Gotsukahara Kofun|ja|五塚原古墳}}, 500 meters north of its current location. In 718 (2nd year of {{ill|Yōrō|ja|養老}}), the shrine was relocated to its current position south of the {{ill|Moto-Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳}}. Honoikazuchi Shrine is said to have been established when Emperor Jimmu enshrined the deity Honoikazuchi during his journey from {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province }} to {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|山背国}}. It is also believed to have been founded around 518 (12th year of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|継体天皇}}), when Keitai built the {{ill|Otokuni Palace|ja|乙訓宮}}. In 718, Honoikazuchi Shrine incorporated Tamayorihime and Emperor Jimmu as deities. In 784 (3rd year of {{ill|Enryaku|ja|延暦}}), the establishment of {{ill|Nagaoka-kyō|ja|長岡京}} brought the imperial palace, Nagaoka-no-miya, to the eastern foot of Muka Shrine. The chief priest of Honoikazuchi Shrine joined Emperor Go-Toba's forces during the {{ill|Jōkyū Rebellion|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221 but later fled to the {{ill|Rokuto-no-shō|ja|六人部荘}} estate in present-day {{ill|Fukuchiyama|ja|福知山市}}. After returning, the shrine's influence waned, and in 1275 (1st year of {{ill|Kenji|ja|建治}}), Muka Shrine merged with Honoikazuchi Shrine, forming Mukō Shrine and becoming the area's guardian shrine. The current main hall was rebuilt in 1422 (29th year of {{ill|Ōei|ja|応永}}) with the cooperation of seven surrounding villages. During the Muromachi period, Mukō Shrine became a rallying point for local {{ill|Tsuchi Ikki|ja|土一揆}} peasant uprisings before they marched on Kyoto. In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine. In 1961 (36th year of {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}}), the surrounding hill was partially excavated for the construction of the {{ill|Meishin Expressway|ja|名神高速道路}}. Today, the shrine and Moto-Inari Kofun remain as a small hill. In recent years, the shrine has been surrounded by a cherry blossom park, partially restoring the area’s former beauty. The main hall of Tokyo's {{ill|Meiji Shrine|ja|明治神宮}} is modeled after Mukō Shrine's main hall, scaled up by 1.5 times. == Grounds == [[File:Mukō-jinja-Cherry Blossoms of a11roach to a shrine.jpg|thumb|Path lined with cherry blossoms leading to the shrine]] From {{ill|Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|ja|京都府道・大阪府道67号西京高槻線}} (Astro Street), visitors enter a long, stone-paved path lined with cherry, azalea, and maple trees. The incline is scenic but shared with vehicles, requiring caution. * Main Hall (Important Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1422 during the Muromachi period. Representative of nagare-zukuri architecture. The model for the Meiji Shrine. ''' Main Hall Shelter (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842 (13th year of {{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保}}). * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842. * North and South Gates (Registered Tangible Cultural Properties) – Built in 1842. * Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1842. * Kagura Hall – Rebuilt in 2003 after the original structure (built in 1625) was lost in a 2002 fire. * Guest Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Relocated from a youth training facility in Osaka around 1965. * Purification Pavilion (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Late 18th-century reconstruction. === Auxiliary Shrines === * Ancestral Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1688 (1st year of {{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}}). * Five Shrines (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Dedicated to various deities. * Other shrines include {{ill|Goryō Shrine|ja|御霊神社}}, {{ill|Tenmangū|ja|天満宮}}, and Katsuyama Inari Shrine. == Festivals == * festival – Second Sunday in May * Sōmen Festival == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall and five ridge plaques. * Paper manuscript of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} (Lower Chapter, Age of the Gods) – A 14th-century transcription. === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Multiple structures, including the Main Hall Shelter, North and South Gates, and various auxiliary shrines. == Access == * Nearest station: [[Hankyu Kyoto Line]] [[Nishimukō Station]], 500 meters northwest. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Mutsume Koreka|ja|六人部是香}} – Late Edo-period scholar and former shrine priest. == External Links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20190101000000/http://www.geocities.jp/mukojinjahp/index.htm Mukō Shrine Official Website (archived)] * {{Commonscat-inline}} {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Tangible Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Historic sites in Mukō]] [[Category:Cherry blossom spots in Japan]] Shrine]][[Category:Mukō Shrine]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Muko Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Muko Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Muko Shrine]][[Category:11]] {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Mukō Shrine | location = Mukōchō Kitayama 65, [[Mukō]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]], Japan | Latitude degrees = 34 | Latitude minutes = 56 | Latitude seconds = 38.8 | N/S = N | Longitude degrees = 135 | Longitude minutes = 41 | Longitude seconds = 49.6 | E/W = E | Map country code = JP | Map = Japan Kyoto | Main deity = {{ill|Toshigami|ja|年神}}, {{ill|Honoikazuchi no Ōkami|ja|火雷大神}}, {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命}}, {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}} | Shrine rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社}} and small shrine), former {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|府社}} | Main hall = Three-bay nagare-zukuri (Important Cultural Property) | festival = Second Sunday in May | Religious rituals = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Muko-jinja, shaden.jpg|280px]]
Dance Hall (right) and Worship Hall (left background) }} {{osm box|w|48434848}} [[File:Muko-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Mukō Shrine''' (向日神社, Mukō Jinja), also known as '''Mukō Myōjin''', is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in Mukōchō Kitayama, [[Mukō]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]]. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myōjin Taisha and small shrine) and a former prefectural shrine. == Deities == * Main deities: {{ill|Toshigami|ja|年神}}, {{ill|Yakusanoikazuchi|ja|火雷大神}}, {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命}}, {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}} == History == Originally, Mukō Shrine consisted of two separate shrines on Mukō Mountain: {{ill|Muka Shrine|ja|向神社}} (U11er Shrine) and {{ill|Honoikazuchi Shrine|ja|火雷神社}} (Lower Shrine). Both are ancient shrines mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳}}, with Honoikazuchi Shrine being one of the candidates for the {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社}} "Otokuni-zazuchi Shrine" (other candidates include {{ill|Sumiyagashira Shrine|ja|角宮神社}} in {{ill|Nagaokakyō|ja|長岡京市}}). Muka Shrine is believed to have originated when Toshigami (Mukō Kami) stayed on Mukō Mountain and promoted rice cultivation. Initially, the shrine was located atop the {{ill|Gotsukahara Kofun|ja|五塚原古墳}}, 500 meters north of its current location. In 718 (2nd year of {{ill|Yōrō|ja|養老}}), the shrine was relocated to its current position south of the {{ill|Moto-Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳}}. Honoikazuchi Shrine is said to have been established when Emperor Jimmu enshrined the deity Honoikazuchi during his journey from {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province }} to {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|山背国}}. It is also believed to have been founded around 518 (12th year of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|継体天皇}}), when Keitai built the {{ill|Otokuni Palace|ja|乙訓宮}}. In 718, Honoikazuchi Shrine incorporated Tamayorihime and Emperor Jimmu as deities. In 784 (3rd year of {{ill|Enryaku|ja|延暦}}), the establishment of {{ill|Nagaoka-kyō|ja|長岡京}} brought the imperial palace, Nagaoka-no-miya, to the eastern foot of Muka Shrine. The chief priest of Honoikazuchi Shrine joined Emperor Go-Toba's forces during the {{ill|Jōkyū Rebellion|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221 but later fled to the {{ill|Rokuto-no-shō|ja|六人部荘}} estate in present-day {{ill|Fukuchiyama|ja|福知山市}}. After returning, the shrine's influence waned, and in 1275 (1st year of {{ill|Kenji|ja|建治}}), Muka Shrine merged with Honoikazuchi Shrine, forming Mukō Shrine and becoming the area's guardian shrine. The current main hall was rebuilt in 1422 (29th year of {{ill|Ōei|ja|応永}}) with the cooperation of seven surrounding villages. During the Muromachi period, Mukō Shrine became a rallying point for local {{ill|Tsuchi Ikki|ja|土一揆}} peasant uprisings before they marched on Kyoto. In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine. In 1961 (36th year of {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}}), the surrounding hill was partially excavated for the construction of the {{ill|Meishin Expressway|ja|名神高速道路}}. Today, the shrine and Moto-Inari Kofun remain as a small hill. In recent years, the shrine has been surrounded by a cherry blossom park, partially restoring the area’s former beauty. The main hall of Tokyo's {{ill|Meiji Shrine|ja|明治神宮}} is modeled after Mukō Shrine's main hall, scaled up by 1.5 times. == Grounds == [[File:Mukō-jinja-Cherry Blossoms of a11roach to a shrine.jpg|thumb|Path lined with cherry blossoms leading to the shrine]] From {{ill|Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|ja|京都府道・大阪府道67号西京高槻線}} (Astro Street), visitors enter a long, stone-paved path lined with cherry, azalea, and maple trees. The incline is scenic but shared with vehicles, requiring caution. * Main Hall (Important Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1422 during the Muromachi period. Representative of nagare-zukuri architecture. The model for the Meiji Shrine. ''' Main Hall Shelter (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842 (13th year of {{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保}}). * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842. * North and South Gates (Registered Tangible Cultural Properties) – Built in 1842. * Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1842. * Kagura Hall – Rebuilt in 2003 after the original structure (built in 1625) was lost in a 2002 fire. * Guest Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Relocated from a youth training facility in Osaka around 1965. * Purification Pavilion (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Late 18th-century reconstruction. === Auxiliary Shrines === * Ancestral Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1688 (1st year of {{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}}). * Five Shrines (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Dedicated to various deities. * Other shrines include {{ill|Goryō Shrine|ja|御霊神社}}, {{ill|Tenmangū|ja|天満宮}}, and Katsuyama Inari Shrine. == Festivals == * festival – Second Sunday in May * Sōmen Festival == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall and five ridge plaques. * Paper manuscript of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} (Lower Chapter, Age of the Gods) – A 14th-century transcription. === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Multiple structures, including the Main Hall Shelter, North and South Gates, and various auxiliary shrines. == Access == * Nearest station: [[Hankyu Kyoto Line]] [[Nishimukō Station]], 500 meters northwest. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Mutsume Koreka|ja|六人部是香}} – Late Edo-period scholar and former shrine priest. == External Links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20190101000000/http://www.geocities.jp/mukojinjahp/index.htm Mukō Shrine Official Website (archived)] * {{Commonscat-inline}} {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Tangible Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Historic sites in Mukō]] [[Category:Cherry blossom spots in Japan]] tier 6]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Muko Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Muko Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Muko Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Muko revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Mukō Shrine | location = Mukōchō Kitayama 65, [[Mukō]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]], Japan | Latitude degrees = 34 | Latitude minutes = 56 | Latitude seconds = 38.8 | N/S = N | Longitude degrees = 135 | Longitude minutes = 41 | Longitude seconds = 49.6 | E/W = E | Map country code = JP | Map = Japan Kyoto | Main deity = {{ill|Toshigami|ja|年神|1=Toshigami}}, {{ill|Honoikazuchi no Ōkami|ja|火雷大神}}, {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命}}, {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}} | Shrine rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社}} and small shrine), former {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|府社}} | Main hall = Three-bay nagare-zukuri (Important Cultural Property) | festival = Second Sunday in May | Religious rituals = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Muko-jinja, shaden.jpg|280px]]
Dance Hall (right) and Worship Hall (left background) }} {{osm box|w|48434848}} [[File:Muko-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Mukō Shrine''' (向日神社, Mukō Jinja), also known as '''Mukō Myōjin''', is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in Mukōchō Kitayama, [[Mukō]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]]. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myōjin Taisha and small shrine) and a former prefectural shrine. == Deities == * Main deities: {{ill|Toshigami|ja|年神|1=Toshigami}}, {{ill|Yakusanoikazuchi|ja|火雷大神}}, {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命}}, {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}} == History == Originally, Mukō Shrine consisted of two separate shrines on Mukō Mountain: {{ill|Muka Shrine|ja|向神社}} (U11er Shrine) and {{ill|Honoikazuchi Shrine|ja|火雷神社}} (Lower Shrine). Both are ancient shrines mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳}}, with Honoikazuchi Shrine being one of the candidates for the {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社}} "Otokuni-zazuchi Shrine" (other candidates include {{ill|Sumiyagashira Shrine|ja|角宮神社}} in {{ill|Nagaokakyō|ja|長岡京市}}). Muka Shrine is believed to have originated when Toshigami (Mukō Kami) stayed on Mukō Mountain and promoted rice cultivation. Initially, the shrine was located atop the {{ill|Gotsukahara Kofun|ja|五塚原古墳}}, 500 meters north of its current location. In 718 (2nd year of {{ill|Yōrō|ja|養老}}), the shrine was relocated to its current position south of the {{ill|Moto-Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳}}. Honoikazuchi Shrine is said to have been established when Emperor Jimmu enshrined the deity Honoikazuchi during his journey from {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province }} to {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|山背国}}. It is also believed to have been founded around 518 (12th year of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|継体天皇}}), when Keitai built the {{ill|Otokuni Palace|ja|乙訓宮}}. In 718, Honoikazuchi Shrine incorporated Tamayorihime and Emperor Jimmu as deities. In 784 (3rd year of {{ill|Enryaku|ja|延暦}}), the establishment of {{ill|Nagaoka-kyō|ja|長岡京}} brought the imperial palace, Nagaoka-no-miya, to the eastern foot of Muka Shrine. The chief priest of Honoikazuchi Shrine joined Emperor Go-Toba's forces during the {{ill|Jōkyū Rebellion|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221 but later fled to the {{ill|Rokuto-no-shō|ja|六人部荘}} estate in present-day {{ill|Fukuchiyama|ja|福知山市}}. After returning, the shrine's influence waned, and in 1275 (1st year of {{ill|Kenji|ja|建治}}), Muka Shrine merged with Honoikazuchi Shrine, forming Mukō Shrine and becoming the area's guardian shrine. The current main hall was rebuilt in 1422 (29th year of {{ill|Ōei|ja|応永}}) with the cooperation of seven surrounding villages. During the Muromachi period, Mukō Shrine became a rallying point for local {{ill|Tsuchi Ikki|ja|土一揆}} peasant uprisings before they marched on Kyoto. In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine. In 1961 (36th year of {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}}), the surrounding hill was partially excavated for the construction of the {{ill|Meishin Expressway|ja|名神高速道路}}. Today, the shrine and Moto-Inari Kofun remain as a small hill. In recent years, the shrine has been surrounded by a cherry blossom park, partially restoring the area’s former beauty. The main hall of Tokyo's {{ill|Meiji Shrine|ja|明治神宮}} is modeled after Mukō Shrine's main hall, scaled up by 1.5 times. == Grounds == [[File:Mukō-jinja-Cherry Blossoms of a11roach to a shrine.jpg|thumb|Path lined with cherry blossoms leading to the shrine]] From {{ill|Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|ja|京都府道・大阪府道67号西京高槻線}} (Astro Street), visitors enter a long, stone-paved path lined with cherry, azalea, and maple trees. The incline is scenic but shared with vehicles, requiring caution. * Main Hall (Important Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1422 during the Muromachi period. Representative of nagare-zukuri architecture. The model for the Meiji Shrine. ''' Main Hall Shelter (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842 (13th year of {{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保}}). * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842. * North and South Gates (Registered Tangible Cultural Properties) – Built in 1842. * Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1842. * Kagura Hall – Rebuilt in 2003 after the original structure (built in 1625) was lost in a 2002 fire. * Guest Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Relocated from a youth training facility in Osaka around 1965. * Purification Pavilion (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Late 18th-century reconstruction. === Auxiliary Shrines === * Ancestral Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1688 (1st year of {{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}}). * Five Shrines (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Dedicated to various deities. * Other shrines include {{ill|Goryō Shrine|ja|御霊神社}}, {{ill|Tenmangū|ja|天満宮}}, and Katsuyama Inari Shrine. == Festivals == * festival – Second Sunday in May * Sōmen Festival == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall and five ridge plaques. * Paper manuscript of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} (Lower Chapter, Age of the Gods) – A 14th-century transcription. === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Multiple structures, including the Main Hall Shelter, North and South Gates, and various auxiliary shrines. == Access == * Nearest station: [[Hankyu Kyoto Line]] [[Nishimukō Station]], 500 meters northwest. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Mutsume Koreka|ja|六人部是香}} – Late Edo-period scholar and former shrine priest. == External Links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20190101000000/http://www.geocities.jp/mukojinjahp/index.htm Mukō Shrine Official Website (archived)] * {{Commonscat-inline}} {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Tangible Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Historic sites in Mukō]] [[Category:Cherry blossom spots in Japan]] revision.comment: Bot: Replaced ill templates with appended |12=draft and created corresponding redirect pages.
revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Mukō Shrine | location = Mukōchō Kitayama 65, [[Mukō]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]], Japan | Latitude degrees = 34 | Latitude minutes = 56 | Latitude seconds = 38.8 | N/S = N | Longitude degrees = 135 | Longitude minutes = 41 | Longitude seconds = 49.6 | E/W = E | Map country code = JP | Map = Japan Kyoto | Main deity = {{ill|Toshigami|ja|年神|1=Toshigami|12=draft}}, {{ill|Honoikazuchi no Ōkami|ja|火雷大神|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇|12=draft}} | Shrine rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=draft}} and small shrine), former {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|府社|12=draft}} | Main hall = Three-bay nagare-zukuri (Important Cultural Property) | festival = Second Sunday in May | Religious rituals = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Muko-jinja, shaden.jpg|280px]]
Dance Hall (right) and Worship Hall (left background) }} {{osm box|w|48434848}} [[File:Muko-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Mukō Shrine''' (向日神社, Mukō Jinja), also known as '''Mukō Myōjin''', is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} located in Mukōchō Kitayama, [[Mukō]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]]. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|12=draft}} (Myōjin Taisha and small shrine) and a former prefectural shrine. == Deities == * Main deities: {{ill|Toshigami|ja|年神|1=Toshigami|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yakusanoikazuchi|ja|火雷大神|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇|12=draft}} == History == Originally, Mukō Shrine consisted of two separate shrines on Mukō Mountain: {{ill|Muka Shrine|ja|向神社|12=draft}} (U11er Shrine) and {{ill|Honoikazuchi Shrine|ja|火雷神社|12=draft}} (Lower Shrine). Both are ancient shrines mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|12=draft}}, with Honoikazuchi Shrine being one of the candidates for the {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=draft}} "Otokuni-zazuchi Shrine" (other candidates include {{ill|Sumiyagashira Shrine|ja|角宮神社|12=draft}} in {{ill|Nagaokakyō|ja|長岡京市|12=draft}}). Muka Shrine is believed to have originated when Toshigami (Mukō Kami) stayed on Mukō Mountain and promoted rice cultivation. Initially, the shrine was located atop the {{ill|Gotsukahara Kofun|ja|五塚原古墳|12=draft}}, 500 meters north of its current location. In 718 (2nd year of {{ill|Yōrō|ja|養老|12=draft}}), the shrine was relocated to its current position south of the {{ill|Moto-Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳|12=draft}}. Honoikazuchi Shrine is said to have been established when Emperor Jimmu enshrined the deity Honoikazuchi during his journey from {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|12=draft}} to {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|山背国|12=draft}}. It is also believed to have been founded around 518 (12th year of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|継体天皇|12=draft}}), when Keitai built the {{ill|Otokuni Palace|ja|乙訓宮|12=draft}}. In 718, Honoikazuchi Shrine incorporated Tamayorihime and Emperor Jimmu as deities. In 784 (3rd year of {{ill|Enryaku|ja|延暦|12=draft}}), the establishment of {{ill|Nagaoka-kyō|ja|長岡京|12=draft}} brought the imperial palace, Nagaoka-no-miya, to the eastern foot of Muka Shrine. The chief priest of Honoikazuchi Shrine joined Emperor Go-Toba's forces during the {{ill|Jōkyū Rebellion|ja|承久の乱|12=draft}} in 1221 but later fled to the {{ill|Rokuto-no-shō|ja|六人部荘|12=draft}} estate in present-day {{ill|Fukuchiyama|ja|福知山市|12=draft}}. After returning, the shrine's influence waned, and in 1275 (1st year of {{ill|Kenji|ja|建治|12=draft}}), Muka Shrine merged with Honoikazuchi Shrine, forming Mukō Shrine and becoming the area's guardian shrine. The current main hall was rebuilt in 1422 (29th year of {{ill|Ōei|ja|応永|12=draft}}) with the cooperation of seven surrounding villages. During the Muromachi period, Mukō Shrine became a rallying point for local {{ill|Tsuchi Ikki|ja|土一揆|12=draft}} peasant uprisings before they marched on Kyoto. In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine. In 1961 (36th year of {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和|12=draft}}), the surrounding hill was partially excavated for the construction of the {{ill|Meishin Expressway|ja|名神高速道路|12=draft}}. Today, the shrine and Moto-Inari Kofun remain as a small hill. In recent years, the shrine has been surrounded by a cherry blossom park, partially restoring the area’s former beauty. The main hall of Tokyo's {{ill|Meiji Shrine|ja|明治神宮|12=draft}} is modeled after Mukō Shrine's main hall, scaled up by 1.5 times. == Grounds == [[File:Mukō-jinja-Cherry Blossoms of a11roach to a shrine.jpg|thumb|Path lined with cherry blossoms leading to the shrine]] From {{ill|Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|ja|京都府道・大阪府道67号西京高槻線|12=draft}} (Astro Street), visitors enter a long, stone-paved path lined with cherry, azalea, and maple trees. The incline is scenic but shared with vehicles, requiring caution. * Main Hall (Important Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1422 during the Muromachi period. Representative of nagare-zukuri architecture. The model for the Meiji Shrine. ''' Main Hall Shelter (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842 (13th year of {{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保|12=draft}}). * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842. * North and South Gates (Registered Tangible Cultural Properties) – Built in 1842. * Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1842. * Kagura Hall – Rebuilt in 2003 after the original structure (built in 1625) was lost in a 2002 fire. * Guest Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Relocated from a youth training facility in Osaka around 1965. * Purification Pavilion (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Late 18th-century reconstruction. === Auxiliary Shrines === * Ancestral Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1688 (1st year of {{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄|12=draft}}). * Five Shrines (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Dedicated to various deities. * Other shrines include {{ill|Goryō Shrine|ja|御霊神社|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tenmangū|ja|天満宮|12=draft}}, and Katsuyama Inari Shrine. == Festivals == * festival – Second Sunday in May * Sōmen Festival == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall and five ridge plaques. * Paper manuscript of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}} (Lower Chapter, Age of the Gods) – A 14th-century transcription. === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Multiple structures, including the Main Hall Shelter, North and South Gates, and various auxiliary shrines. == Access == * Nearest station: [[Hankyu Kyoto Line]] [[Nishimukō Station]], 500 meters northwest. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Mutsume Koreka|ja|六人部是香|12=draft}} – Late Edo-period scholar and former shrine priest. == External Links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20190101000000/http://www.geocities.jp/mukojinjahp/index.htm Mukō Shrine Official Website (archived)] * {{Commonscat-inline}} {{shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Tangible Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Historic sites in Mukō]] [[Category:Cherry blossom spots in Japan]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Mukō Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category: tier 6]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[sn:Muko Shrine]] [[ja:向日神社]] [[en:Muko Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Muko Shrine}}

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Mukō Shrine | location = Mukōchō Kitayama 65, [[Mukō]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]], Japan | Latitude degrees = 34 | Latitude minutes = 56 | Latitude seconds = 38.8 | N/S = N | Longitude degrees = 135 | Longitude minutes = 41 | Longitude seconds = 49.6 | E/W = E | Map country code = JP | Map = Japan Kyoto | Main deity = {{ill|Toshigami|ja|年神|1=Toshigami|12=draft}}, {{ill|Honoikazuchi no Ōkami|ja|火雷大神|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇|12=draft}} | Shrine rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=draft}} and small shrine), former {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|府社|12=draft}} | Main hall = Three-bay nagare-zukuri (Important Cultural Property) | festival = Second Sunday in May | Religious rituals = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Muko-jinja, shaden.jpg|280px]]
Dance Hall (right) and Worship Hall (left background) }} {{osm box|w|48434848}} [[File:Muko-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Mukō Shrine''' (向日神社, Mukō Jinja), also known as '''Mukō Myōjin''', is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} located in Mukōchō Kitayama, [[Mukō]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]]. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|12=draft}} (Myōjin Taisha and small shrine) and a former prefectural shrine. == Deities == * Main deities: {{ill|Toshigami|ja|年神|1=Toshigami|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yakusanoikazuchi|ja|火雷大神|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇|12=draft}} == History == Originally, Mukō Shrine consisted of two separate shrines on Mukō Mountain: {{ill|Muka Shrine|ja|向神社|12=draft}} (U11er Shrine) and {{ill|Honoikazuchi Shrine|ja|火雷神社|12=draft}} (Lower Shrine). Both are ancient shrines mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|12=draft}}, with Honoikazuchi Shrine being one of the candidates for the {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=draft}} "Otokuni-zazuchi Shrine" (other candidates include {{ill|Sumiyagashira Shrine|ja|角宮神社|12=draft}} in {{ill|Nagaokakyō|ja|長岡京市|12=draft}}). Muka Shrine is believed to have originated when Toshigami (Mukō Kami) stayed on Mukō Mountain and promoted rice cultivation. Initially, the shrine was located atop the {{ill|Gotsukahara Kofun|ja|五塚原古墳|12=draft}}, 500 meters north of its current location. In 718 (2nd year of {{ill|Yōrō|ja|養老|12=draft}}), the shrine was relocated to its current position south of the {{ill|Moto-Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳|12=draft}}. Honoikazuchi Shrine is said to have been established when Emperor Jimmu enshrined the deity Honoikazuchi during his journey from {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|12=draft}} to {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|山背国|12=draft}}. It is also believed to have been founded around 518 (12th year of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|継体天皇|12=draft}}), when Keitai built the {{ill|Otokuni Palace|ja|乙訓宮|12=draft}}. In 718, Honoikazuchi Shrine incorporated Tamayorihime and Emperor Jimmu as deities. In 784 (3rd year of {{ill|Enryaku|ja|延暦|12=draft}}), the establishment of {{ill|Nagaoka-kyō|ja|長岡京|12=draft}} brought the imperial palace, Nagaoka-no-miya, to the eastern foot of Muka Shrine. The chief priest of Honoikazuchi Shrine joined Emperor Go-Toba's forces during the {{ill|Jōkyū Rebellion|ja|承久の乱|12=draft}} in 1221 but later fled to the {{ill|Rokuto-no-shō|ja|六人部荘|12=draft}} estate in present-day {{ill|Fukuchiyama|ja|福知山市|12=draft}}. After returning, the shrine's influence waned, and in 1275 (1st year of {{ill|Kenji|ja|建治|12=draft}}), Muka Shrine merged with Honoikazuchi Shrine, forming Mukō Shrine and becoming the area's guardian shrine. The current main hall was rebuilt in 1422 (29th year of {{ill|Ōei|ja|応永|12=draft}}) with the cooperation of seven surrounding villages. During the Muromachi period, Mukō Shrine became a rallying point for local {{ill|Tsuchi Ikki|ja|土一揆|12=draft}} peasant uprisings before they marched on Kyoto. In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine. In 1961 (36th year of {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和|12=draft}}), the surrounding hill was partially excavated for the construction of the {{ill|Meishin Expressway|ja|名神高速道路|12=draft}}. Today, the shrine and Moto-Inari Kofun remain as a small hill. In recent years, the shrine has been surrounded by a cherry blossom park, partially restoring the area’s former beauty. The main hall of Tokyo's {{ill|Meiji Shrine|ja|明治神宮|12=draft}} is modeled after Mukō Shrine's main hall, scaled up by 1.5 times. == Grounds == [[File:Mukō-jinja-Cherry Blossoms of a11roach to a shrine.jpg|thumb|Path lined with cherry blossoms leading to the shrine]] From {{ill|Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|ja|京都府道・大阪府道67号西京高槻線|12=draft}} (Astro Street), visitors enter a long, stone-paved path lined with cherry, azalea, and maple trees. The incline is scenic but shared with vehicles, requiring caution. * Main Hall (Important Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1422 during the Muromachi period. Representative of nagare-zukuri architecture. The model for the Meiji Shrine. ''' Main Hall Shelter (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842 (13th year of {{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保|12=draft}}). * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842. * North and South Gates (Registered Tangible Cultural Properties) – Built in 1842. * Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1842. * Kagura Hall – Rebuilt in 2003 after the original structure (built in 1625) was lost in a 2002 fire. * Guest Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Relocated from a youth training facility in Osaka around 1965. * Purification Pavilion (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Late 18th-century reconstruction. === Auxiliary Shrines === * Ancestral Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1688 (1st year of {{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄|12=draft}}). * Five Shrines (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Dedicated to various deities. * Other shrines include {{ill|Goryō Shrine|ja|御霊神社|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tenmangū|ja|天満宮|12=draft}}, and Katsuyama Inari Shrine. == Festivals == * festival – Second Sunday in May * Sōmen Festival == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall and five ridge plaques. * Paper manuscript of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}} (Lower Chapter, Age of the Gods) – A 14th-century transcription. === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Multiple structures, including the Main Hall Shelter, North and South Gates, and various auxiliary shrines. == Access == * Nearest station: [[Hankyu Kyoto Line]] [[Nishimukō Station]], 500 meters northwest. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Mutsume Koreka|ja|六人部是香|12=draft}} – Late Edo-period scholar and former shrine priest. == External Links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20190101000000/http://www.geocities.jp/mukojinjahp/index.htm Mukō Shrine Official Website (archived)] * {{Commonscat-inline}} {{shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Tangible Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Historic sites in Mukō]] [[Category:Cherry blossom spots in Japan]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Mukō Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[sn:Muko Shrine]] [[ja:向日神社]] [[en:Muko Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Muko Shrine}}

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Mukō Shrine | location = Mukōchō Kitayama 65, [[Mukō]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]], Japan | Latitude degrees = 34 | Latitude minutes = 56 | Latitude seconds = 38.8 | N/S = N | Longitude degrees = 135 | Longitude minutes = 41 | Longitude seconds = 49.6 | E/W = E | Map country code = JP | Map = Japan Kyoto | Main deity = {{ill|Toshigami|ja|年神|1=Toshigami|12=draft}}, {{ill|Honoikazuchi no Ōkami|ja|火雷大神|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇|12=draft}} | Shrine rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=draft}} and small shrine), former {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|府社|12=draft}} | Main hall = Three-bay nagare-zukuri (Important Cultural Property) | festival = Second Sunday in May | Religious rituals = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Muko-jinja, shaden.jpg|280px]]
Dance Hall (right) and Worship Hall (left background) }} {{osm box|w|48434848}} [[File:Muko-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Mukō Shrine''' (向日神社, Mukō Jinja), also known as '''Mukō Myōjin''', is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} located in Mukōchō Kitayama, [[Mukō]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]]. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|12=draft}} (Myōjin Taisha and small shrine) and a former prefectural shrine. == Deities == * Main deities: {{ill|Toshigami|ja|年神|1=Toshigami|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yakusanoikazuchi|ja|火雷大神|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇|12=draft}} == History == Originally, Mukō Shrine consisted of two separate shrines on Mukō Mountain: {{ill|Muka Shrine|ja|向神社|12=draft}} (U11er Shrine) and {{ill|Honoikazuchi Shrine|ja|火雷神社|12=draft}} (Lower Shrine). Both are ancient shrines mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|12=draft}}, with Honoikazuchi Shrine being one of the candidates for the {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=draft}} "Otokuni-zazuchi Shrine" (other candidates include {{ill|Sumiyagashira Shrine|ja|角宮神社|12=draft}} in {{ill|Nagaokakyō|ja|長岡京市|12=draft}}). Muka Shrine is believed to have originated when Toshigami (Mukō Kami) stayed on Mukō Mountain and promoted rice cultivation. Initially, the shrine was located atop the {{ill|Gotsukahara Kofun|ja|五塚原古墳|12=draft}}, 500 meters north of its current location. In 718 (2nd year of {{ill|Yōrō|ja|養老|12=draft}}), the shrine was relocated to its current position south of the {{ill|Moto-Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳|12=draft}}. Honoikazuchi Shrine is said to have been established when Emperor Jimmu enshrined the deity Honoikazuchi during his journey from {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|12=draft}} to {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|山背国|12=draft}}. It is also believed to have been founded around 518 (12th year of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|継体天皇|12=draft}}), when Keitai built the {{ill|Otokuni Palace|ja|乙訓宮|12=draft}}. In 718, Honoikazuchi Shrine incorporated Tamayorihime and Emperor Jimmu as deities. In 784 (3rd year of {{ill|Enryaku|ja|延暦|12=draft}}), the establishment of {{ill|Nagaoka-kyō|ja|長岡京|12=draft}} brought the imperial palace, Nagaoka-no-miya, to the eastern foot of Muka Shrine. The chief priest of Honoikazuchi Shrine joined Emperor Go-Toba's forces during the {{ill|Jōkyū Rebellion|ja|承久の乱|12=draft}} in 1221 but later fled to the {{ill|Rokuto-no-shō|ja|六人部荘|12=draft}} estate in present-day {{ill|Fukuchiyama|ja|福知山市|12=draft}}. After returning, the shrine's influence waned, and in 1275 (1st year of {{ill|Kenji|ja|建治|12=draft}}), Muka Shrine merged with Honoikazuchi Shrine, forming Mukō Shrine and becoming the area's guardian shrine. The current main hall was rebuilt in 1422 (29th year of {{ill|Ōei|ja|応永|12=draft}}) with the cooperation of seven surrounding villages. During the Muromachi period, Mukō Shrine became a rallying point for local {{ill|Tsuchi Ikki|ja|土一揆|12=draft}} peasant uprisings before they marched on Kyoto. In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine. In 1961 (36th year of {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和|12=draft}}), the surrounding hill was partially excavated for the construction of the {{ill|Meishin Expressway|ja|名神高速道路|12=draft}}. Today, the shrine and Moto-Inari Kofun remain as a small hill. In recent years, the shrine has been surrounded by a cherry blossom park, partially restoring the area’s former beauty. The main hall of Tokyo's {{ill|Meiji Shrine|ja|明治神宮|12=draft}} is modeled after Mukō Shrine's main hall, scaled up by 1.5 times. == Grounds == [[File:Mukō-jinja-Cherry Blossoms of a11roach to a shrine.jpg|thumb|Path lined with cherry blossoms leading to the shrine]] From {{ill|Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|ja|京都府道・大阪府道67号西京高槻線|12=draft}} (Astro Street), visitors enter a long, stone-paved path lined with cherry, azalea, and maple trees. The incline is scenic but shared with vehicles, requiring caution. * Main Hall (Important Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1422 during the Muromachi period. Representative of nagare-zukuri architecture. The model for the Meiji Shrine. ''' Main Hall Shelter (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842 (13th year of {{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保|12=draft}}). * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842. * North and South Gates (Registered Tangible Cultural Properties) – Built in 1842. * Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1842. * Kagura Hall – Rebuilt in 2003 after the original structure (built in 1625) was lost in a 2002 fire. * Guest Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Relocated from a youth training facility in Osaka around 1965. * Purification Pavilion (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Late 18th-century reconstruction. === Auxiliary Shrines === * Ancestral Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1688 (1st year of {{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄|12=draft}}). * Five Shrines (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Dedicated to various deities. * Other shrines include {{ill|Goryō Shrine|ja|御霊神社|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tenmangū|ja|天満宮|12=draft}}, and Katsuyama Inari Shrine. == Festivals == * festival – Second Sunday in May * Sōmen Festival == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall and five ridge plaques. * Paper manuscript of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}} (Lower Chapter, Age of the Gods) – A 14th-century transcription. === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Multiple structures, including the Main Hall Shelter, North and South Gates, and various auxiliary shrines. == Access == * Nearest station: [[Hankyu Kyoto Line]] [[Nishimukō Station]], 500 meters northwest. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Mutsume Koreka|ja|六人部是香|12=draft}} – Late Edo-period scholar and former shrine priest. == External Links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20190101000000/http://www.geocities.jp/mukojinjahp/index.htm Mukō Shrine Official Website (archived)] * {{Commonscat-inline}} {{shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Tangible Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Historic sites in Mukō]] [[Category:Cherry blossom spots in Japan]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Mukō Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Muko Shrine]] [[ja:向日神社]] [[en:Muko Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Muko Shrine}}

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Mukō Shrine | location = Mukōchō Kitayama 65, [[Mukō]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]], Japan | Latitude degrees = 34 | Latitude minutes = 56 | Latitude seconds = 38.8 | N/S = N | Longitude degrees = 135 | Longitude minutes = 41 | Longitude seconds = 49.6 | E/W = E | Map country code = JP | Map = Japan Kyoto | Main deity = {{ill|Toshigami|ja|年神|1=Toshigami|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Honoikazuchi no Ōkami|ja|火雷大神|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇|12=draft|12=draft}} | Shrine Rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=draft|12=draft}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=draft|12=draft}} and small shrine), former {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|府社|12=draft|12=draft}} | Main hall = Three-bay nagare-zukuri (Important Cultural Property) | festival = Second Sunday in May | Religious rituals = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Muko-jinja, shaden.jpg|280px]]
Dance Hall (right) and Worship Hall (left background) }} {{osm box|w|48434848}} [[File:Muko-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Mukō Shrine''' (向日神社, Mukō Jinja), also known as '''Mukō Myōjin''', is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft|12=draft}} located in Mukōchō Kitayama, [[Mukō]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]]. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=draft|12=draft}} (Myōjin Taisha and small shrine) and a former prefectural shrine. == Deities == * Main deities: {{ill|Toshigami|ja|年神|1=Toshigami|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yakusanoikazuchi|ja|火雷大神|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇|12=draft|12=draft}} == History == Originally, Mukō Shrine consisted of two separate shrines on Mukō Mountain: {{ill|Muka Shrine|ja|向神社|12=draft|12=draft}} (U11er Shrine) and {{ill|Honoikazuchi Shrine|ja|火雷神社|12=draft|12=draft}} (Lower Shrine). Both are ancient shrines mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|12=draft|12=draft}}, with Honoikazuchi Shrine being one of the candidates for the {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=draft|12=draft}} "Otokuni-zazuchi Shrine" (other candidates include {{ill|Sumiyagashira Shrine|ja|角宮神社|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Nagaokakyō|ja|長岡京市|12=draft|12=draft}}). Muka Shrine is believed to have originated when Toshigami (Mukō Kami) stayed on Mukō Mountain and promoted rice cultivation. Initially, the shrine was located atop the {{ill|Gotsukahara Kofun|ja|五塚原古墳|12=draft|12=draft}}, 500 meters north of its current location. In 718 (2nd year of {{ill|Yōrō|ja|養老|12=draft|12=draft}}), the shrine was relocated to its current position south of the {{ill|Moto-Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳|12=draft|12=draft}}. Honoikazuchi Shrine is said to have been established when Emperor Jimmu enshrined the deity Honoikazuchi during his journey from {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|12=draft|12=draft}} to {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|山背国|12=draft|12=draft}}. It is also believed to have been founded around 518 (12th year of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|継体天皇|12=draft|12=draft}}), when Keitai built the {{ill|Otokuni Palace|ja|乙訓宮|12=draft|12=draft}}. In 718, Honoikazuchi Shrine incorporated Tamayorihime and Emperor Jimmu as deities. In 784 (3rd year of {{ill|Enryaku|ja|延暦|12=draft|12=draft}}), the establishment of {{ill|Nagaoka-kyō|ja|長岡京|12=draft|12=draft}} brought the imperial palace, Nagaoka-no-miya, to the eastern foot of Muka Shrine. The chief priest of Honoikazuchi Shrine joined Emperor Go-Toba's forces during the {{ill|Jōkyū Rebellion|ja|承久の乱|12=draft|12=draft}} in 1221 but later fled to the {{ill|Rokuto-no-shō|ja|六人部荘|12=draft|12=draft}} estate in present-day {{ill|Fukuchiyama|ja|福知山市|12=draft|12=draft}}. After returning, the shrine's influence waned, and in 1275 (1st year of {{ill|Kenji|ja|建治|12=draft|12=draft}}), Muka Shrine merged with Honoikazuchi Shrine, forming Mukō Shrine and becoming the area's guardian shrine. The current main hall was rebuilt in 1422 (29th year of {{ill|Ōei|ja|応永|12=draft|12=draft}}) with the cooperation of seven surrounding villages. During the Muromachi period, Mukō Shrine became a rallying point for local {{ill|Tsuchi Ikki|ja|土一揆|12=draft|12=draft}} peasant uprisings before they marched on Kyoto. In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine. In 1961 (36th year of {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和|12=draft|12=draft}}), the surrounding hill was partially excavated for the construction of the {{ill|Meishin Expressway|ja|名神高速道路|12=draft|12=draft}}. Today, the shrine and Moto-Inari Kofun remain as a small hill. In recent years, the shrine has been surrounded by a cherry blossom park, partially restoring the area’s former beauty. The main hall of Tokyo's {{ill|Meiji Shrine|ja|明治神宮|12=draft|12=draft}} is modeled after Mukō Shrine's main hall, scaled up by 1.5 times. == Grounds == [[File:Mukō-jinja-Cherry Blossoms of a11roach to a shrine.jpg|thumb|Path lined with cherry blossoms leading to the shrine]] From {{ill|Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|ja|京都府道・大阪府道67号西京高槻線|12=draft|12=draft}} (Astro Street), visitors enter a long, stone-paved path lined with cherry, azalea, and maple trees. The incline is scenic but shared with vehicles, requiring caution. * Main Hall (Important Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1422 during the Muromachi period. Representative of nagare-zukuri architecture. The model for the Meiji Shrine. ''' Main Hall Shelter (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842 (13th year of {{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保|12=draft|12=draft}}). * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842. * North and South Gates (Registered Tangible Cultural Properties) – Built in 1842. * Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1842. * Kagura Hall – Rebuilt in 2003 after the original structure (built in 1625) was lost in a 2002 fire. * Guest Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Relocated from a youth training facility in Osaka around 1965. * Purification Pavilion (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Late 18th-century reconstruction. === Auxiliary Shrines === * Ancestral Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1688 (1st year of {{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄|12=draft|12=draft}}). * Five Shrines (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Dedicated to various deities. * Other shrines include {{ill|Goryō Shrine|ja|御霊神社|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tenmangū|ja|天満宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, and Katsuyama Inari Shrine. == Festivals == * festival – Second Sunday in May * Sōmen Festival == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall and five ridge plaques. * Paper manuscript of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft|12=draft}} (Lower Chapter, Age of the Gods) – A 14th-century transcription. === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Multiple structures, including the Main Hall Shelter, North and South Gates, and various auxiliary shrines. == Access == * Nearest station: [[Hankyu Kyoto Line]] [[Nishimukō Station]], 500 meters northwest. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Mutsume Koreka|ja|六人部是香|12=draft|12=draft}} – Late Edo-period scholar and former shrine priest. == External Links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20190101000000/http://www.geocities.jp/mukojinjahp/index.htm Mukō Shrine Official Website (archived)] * {{Commonscat-inline}} {{shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Tangible Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Historic sites in Mukō]] [[Category:Cherry blossom spots in Japan]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Mukō Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Muko Shrine]] [[ja:向日神社]] [[en:Muko Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Muko Shrine}}

{{ill|Tenmangū|ja|天満宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, and Katsuyama Inari Shrine. == Festivals == * festival – Second Sunday in May * Sōmen Festival == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall and five ridge plaques. * Paper manuscript of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft|12=draft}} (Lower Chapter, Age of the Gods) – A 14th-century transcription. === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Multiple structures, including the Main Hall Shelter, North and South Gates, and various auxiliary shrines. == Access == * Nearest station: [[Hankyu Kyoto Line]] [[Nishimukō Station]], 500 meters northwest. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Mutsume Koreka|ja|六人部是香|12=draft|12=draft}} – Late Edo-period scholar and former shrine priest. == External Links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20190101000000/http://www.geocities.jp/mukojinjahp/index.htm Mukō Shrine Official Website (archived)] * {{Commonscat-inline}} {{shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Tangible Cultural Properties of Japan]] revision.comment: Text replacement - "Engishiki Jinmyocho|" to "Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|"
revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Mukō Shrine | location = Mukōchō Kitayama 65, [[Mukō]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]], Japan | Latitude degrees = 34 | Latitude minutes = 56 | Latitude seconds = 38.8 | N/S = N | Longitude degrees = 135 | Longitude minutes = 41 | Longitude seconds = 49.6 | E/W = E | Map country code = JP | Map = Japan Kyoto | Main deity = {{ill|Toshigami|ja|年神|1=Toshigami|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Honoikazuchi no Ōkami|ja|火雷大神|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇|12=draft|12=draft}} | Shrine Rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=draft|12=draft}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=draft|12=draft}} and small shrine), former {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|府社|12=draft|12=draft}} | Main hall = Three-bay nagare-zukuri (Important Cultural Property) | festival = Second Sunday in May | Religious rituals = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Muko-jinja, shaden.jpg|280px]]
Dance Hall (right) and Worship Hall (left background) }} {{osm box|w|48434848}} [[File:Muko-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Mukō Shrine''' (向日神社, Mukō Jinja), also known as '''Mukō Myōjin''', is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft|12=draft}} located in Mukōchō Kitayama, [[Mukō]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]]. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=draft|12=draft}} (Myōjin Taisha and small shrine) and a former prefectural shrine. == Deities == * Main deities: {{ill|Toshigami|ja|年神|1=Toshigami|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yakusanoikazuchi|ja|火雷大神|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇|12=draft|12=draft}} == History == Originally, Mukō Shrine consisted of two separate shrines on Mukō Mountain: {{ill|Muka Shrine|ja|向神社|12=draft|12=draft}} (U11er Shrine) and {{ill|Honoikazuchi Shrine|ja|火雷神社|12=draft|12=draft}} (Lower Shrine). Both are ancient shrines mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|12=draft|12=draft}}, with Honoikazuchi Shrine being one of the candidates for the {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=draft|12=draft}} "Otokuni-zazuchi Shrine" (other candidates include {{ill|Sumiyagashira Shrine|ja|角宮神社|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Nagaokakyō|ja|長岡京市|12=draft|12=draft}}). Muka Shrine is believed to have originated when Toshigami (Mukō Kami) stayed on Mukō Mountain and promoted rice cultivation. Initially, the shrine was located atop the {{ill|Gotsukahara Kofun|ja|五塚原古墳|12=draft|12=draft}}, 500 meters north of its current location. In 718 (2nd year of {{ill|Yōrō|ja|養老|12=draft|12=draft}}), the shrine was relocated to its current position south of the {{ill|Moto-Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳|12=draft|12=draft}}. Honoikazuchi Shrine is said to have been established when Emperor Jimmu enshrined the deity Honoikazuchi during his journey from {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|12=draft|12=draft}} to {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|山背国|12=draft|12=draft}}. It is also believed to have been founded around 518 (12th year of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|継体天皇|12=draft|12=draft}}), when Keitai built the {{ill|Otokuni Palace|ja|乙訓宮|12=draft|12=draft}}. In 718, Honoikazuchi Shrine incorporated Tamayorihime and Emperor Jimmu as deities. In 784 (3rd year of {{ill|Enryaku|ja|延暦|12=draft|12=draft}}), the establishment of {{ill|Nagaoka-kyō|ja|長岡京|12=draft|12=draft}} brought the imperial palace, Nagaoka-no-miya, to the eastern foot of Muka Shrine. The chief priest of Honoikazuchi Shrine joined Emperor Go-Toba's forces during the {{ill|Jōkyū Rebellion|ja|承久の乱|12=draft|12=draft}} in 1221 but later fled to the {{ill|Rokuto-no-shō|ja|六人部荘|12=draft|12=draft}} estate in present-day {{ill|Fukuchiyama|ja|福知山市|12=draft|12=draft}}. After returning, the shrine's influence waned, and in 1275 (1st year of {{ill|Kenji|ja|建治|12=draft|12=draft}}), Muka Shrine merged with Honoikazuchi Shrine, forming Mukō Shrine and becoming the area's guardian shrine. The current main hall was rebuilt in 1422 (29th year of {{ill|Ōei|ja|応永|12=draft|12=draft}}) with the cooperation of seven surrounding villages. During the Muromachi period, Mukō Shrine became a rallying point for local {{ill|Tsuchi Ikki|ja|土一揆|12=draft|12=draft}} peasant uprisings before they marched on Kyoto. In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine. In 1961 (36th year of {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和|12=draft|12=draft}}), the surrounding hill was partially excavated for the construction of the {{ill|Meishin Expressway|ja|名神高速道路|12=draft|12=draft}}. Today, the shrine and Moto-Inari Kofun remain as a small hill. In recent years, the shrine has been surrounded by a cherry blossom park, partially restoring the area’s former beauty. The main hall of Tokyo's {{ill|Meiji Shrine|ja|明治神宮|12=draft|12=draft}} is modeled after Mukō Shrine's main hall, scaled up by 1.5 times. == Grounds == [[File:Mukō-jinja-Cherry Blossoms of a11roach to a shrine.jpg|thumb|Path lined with cherry blossoms leading to the shrine]] From {{ill|Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|ja|京都府道・大阪府道67号西京高槻線|12=draft|12=draft}} (Astro Street), visitors enter a long, stone-paved path lined with cherry, azalea, and maple trees. The incline is scenic but shared with vehicles, requiring caution. * Main Hall (Important Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1422 during the Muromachi period. Representative of nagare-zukuri architecture. The model for the Meiji Shrine. ''' Main Hall Shelter (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842 (13th year of {{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保|12=draft|12=draft}}). * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842. * North and South Gates (Registered Tangible Cultural Properties) – Built in 1842. * Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1842. * Kagura Hall – Rebuilt in 2003 after the original structure (built in 1625) was lost in a 2002 fire. * Guest Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Relocated from a youth training facility in Osaka around 1965. * Purification Pavilion (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Late 18th-century reconstruction. === Auxiliary Shrines === * Ancestral Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1688 (1st year of {{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄|12=draft|12=draft}}). * Five Shrines (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Dedicated to various deities. * Other shrines include {{ill|Goryō Shrine|ja|御霊神社|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tenmangū|ja|天満宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, and Katsuyama Inari Shrine. == Festivals == * festival – Second Sunday in May * Sōmen Festival == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall and five ridge plaques. * Paper manuscript of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft|12=draft}} (Lower Chapter, Age of the Gods) – A 14th-century transcription. === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Multiple structures, including the Main Hall Shelter, North and South Gates, and various auxiliary shrines. == Access == * Nearest station: [[Hankyu Kyoto Line]] [[Nishimukō Station]], 500 meters northwest. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Mutsume Koreka|ja|六人部是香|12=draft|12=draft}} – Late Edo-period scholar and former shrine priest. == External Links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20190101000000/http://www.geocities.jp/mukojinjahp/index.htm Mukō Shrine Official Website (archived)] * {{Commonscat-inline}} {{shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Tangible Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Historic sites in Mukō]] [[Category:Cherry blossom spots in Japan]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Mukō Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Muko Shrine]] [[ja:向日神社]] [[en:Muko Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Muko Shrine}}

revision.comment: 53 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:向日神社 up to rev 99695516
revision.text:
{{神社 |名称 = 向日神社 |所在地 = [[京都府]][[向日市]]向日町北山65 | 緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 56 | 緯度秒 = 38.8 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N | 経度度 = 135 |経度分 = 41 | 経度秒 = 49.6 | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E | 地図国コード = JP | 地図 = Japan Kyoto |祭神 = [[年神|向日神]]
火雷大神
[[玉櫛媛|玉依姫命]]
[[神武天皇]] |社格 = [[式内社]]（[[名神大社|名神大]]・小）
旧[[府社]] |本殿 = 三間社[[神社建築|流造]]（重要文化財） |例祭 = 5月第二日曜日 |神事= |画像 = [[File:Muko-jinja, shaden.jpg|280px]]
舞楽殿（右）・拝殿（左奥） }} {{osm box|w|48434848}} [[File:Muko-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|鳥居}}]] '''向日神社'''（むこうじんじゃ）は、[[京都府]][[向日市]]向日町北山にある[[神社]]。'''向日明神'''とも呼ばれる。[[式内社]]（[[名神大社]]・小社）で、旧[[社格]]は[[府社]]。 == 祭神 == * 主祭神 - [[年神|向日神]]、火雷大神、[[玉櫛媛|玉依姫命]]、[[神武天皇]] == 歴史 == 元々は、同じ向日山に鎮座する「向神社」（上ノ社）、「火雷神社」（下ノ社）という別の神社だった。いずれも[[延喜式神名帳]]に現れる古社で、「火雷神社」については[[名神大社]]「乙訓坐火雷神社（乙訓神社）」の[[論社]]である（他の論社は[[長岡京市]]の[[角宮神社]]）。 向神社は御歳神（[[年神|向日神]]）が向日山にとどまり、稲作を奨励したことに始まるというが、そもそもは現在地から北に500メートルの所にある五塚原古墳（いつかはらこふん）の上に祀られていたようである。[[養老]]2年（[[718年]]）になって元稲荷古墳の南側にある現在地に遷座した。 火雷神社は[[神武天皇]]が[[大和国]][[橿原市|橿原]]から[[山城国|山背国]]へ遷った際、当地に火雷神を祀ったことに始まると伝えるが、[[継体天皇]]が乙訓宮を造った継体天皇12年（[[518年]]）頃の創建ともされる。また、火雷神社は養老2年（718年）の社殿新築にあたり[[玉櫛媛|玉依姫命]]と[[神武天皇]]を合祀している。 [[延暦]]3年（[[784年]]）に[[長岡京]]が作られて遷都されたが、その中心となる長岡宮は向神社の東の麓に作られた。 火雷神社宮司は[[承久]]3年（[[1221年]]）の[[承久の乱]]の際に[[後鳥羽天皇|後鳥羽上皇]]側について敗れると、現在の京都府[[福知山市]]にあった[[荘園 (日本)|荘園]]「六人部荘（むとべのしょう）」に隠れ、許されて戻った時には衰微していた。こうして[[建治]]元年（[[1275年]]）には向神社が火雷神社を併祭することとなり、向日神社と社名を改めて付近一帯の総鎮守となった。 [[応永]]29年（[[1422年]]）に7つの村が協力して本殿（[[重要文化財]]）を再建している。当地はかつて西岡（にしのおか）と呼ばれていたが、[[土一揆]]が行われる際には当社で集結してから京都に向かっている。 [[明治]]時代になると、当社は[[府社]]に列せられた。 当社が建っている丘陵は、[[1961年]]（[[昭和]]36年）に[[名神高速道路]]建設のための土を取ったため、かなり削られ、向日神社と元稲荷古墳がある部分だけが小山になって残った。近隣に[[笹部新太郎]]の桜苗園がありこれも取り壊されたが、近年になり神社周辺に桜の園として復元されている。 [[東京]]の[[明治神宮]]の本殿は当社の本殿がモデルとなっており、当社本殿の1.5倍のスケールに拡大した設計となっている。 境内の南には向日市天文館がある。 == 境内 == [[ファイル:Mukō-jinja-Cherry Blossoms of approach to a shrine.jpg|代替文=参道|サムネイル|長い石畳の参道脇に桜が咲く]] [[京都府道・大阪府道67号西京高槻線|京都府道67号西京高槻線]]（アストロ通り）から、長い石畳の参道に入る。参道は[[サクラ|桜]]・[[ツツジ]]・[[カエデ]]等の並木が連なる緩やかな登り坂で情緒があるが、自動車も走るので注意が必要である。 * 本殿（[[重要文化財]]） - [[応永]]29年（[[1422年]]）再建。[[室町時代]]の[[流造]]を代表するもの。[[明治神宮]]本殿のモデルである。 ** 本殿覆屋（国[[登録有形文化財]]） - [[天保]]13年（[[1842年]]）再建。 * 幣殿（国登録有形文化財） - 天保13年（1842年）再建。 * 拝殿（国登録有形文化財） - 天保13年（1842年）再建。 * 北門（国登録有形文化財） - 天保13年（1842年）建立。 * 南門（国登録有形文化財） - 天保13年（1842年）建立。 * 瑞垣（国登録有形文化財） - 天保13年（1842年）建立。 * 神変大菩薩 - [[役行者]]を祀る。そのほかにも[[神仏習合]]の名残がある。 * 勝山身代[[不動明王]] * 白雲龍王 * 白玉[[弁財天]]女 * 舞楽殿 - [[2003年]]（[[平成]]15年）再建。もともとは[[寛永]]2年（[[1625年]]）建立の建物であったが、[[2002年]]（平成14年）に火災で焼失した。 * 社務所・参集殿 * 客殿（国登録有形文化財） - [[1965年]]（[[昭和]]40年）前後に[[大阪府]][[島本町]]にあった青年研修施設を移築したものとされる。 * 手水舎（国登録有形文化財） - 18世紀後期の再建。 === 摂末社 === [[ファイル:Mukō-jinja, Auxiliary Katsuyama-Inari-jinja 001.jpg|代替文=境内社　勝山稲荷社|サムネイル|境内社　勝山稲荷社]] * 祖霊社（国登録有形文化財） - [[元禄]]元年（[[1688年]]）建立。本社の拝殿とは[[江戸時代]]末期建立の渡廊（国登録有形文化財）で繋がっている。 * 五社神社（国登録有形文化財） - 祭神：[[大国主|大己貴神]]、[[タケミカヅチ|武雷神]]、[[賀茂別雷命|別雷神]]、[[イワサク・ネサク|磐裂神]]、[[事代主|事代主神]]。江戸時代末期の建立。 * 春日神社（国登録有形文化財） - 祭神：武甕槌神、[[経津主神|斎主神]]、[[天児屋命|天津児屋根尊]]、[[比売神|姫大神]]。もとは森本村にあった社で、[[1878年]]（[[明治]]11年）に移築したもの。 * 増井神社 - 祭神：火雷神荒魂。火雷神の荒魂神を祀る井戸が御神体となっている。 * 向日大明神 * 秀九大明神 * 御霊神社（国登録有形文化財） - 祭神：[[イザナギ|伊邪那岐命]]、[[イザナミ|伊邪那美命]]。もとは[[物集女]]村にあった社で、[[1877年]]（明治10年）に移築したもの。 * 天満宮社（国登録有形文化財） - 祭神：[[菅原道真]]、大歳神、屋船神。天保4年（[[1833年]]）建立。瑞垣（国登録有形文化財）は江戸時代の築造である。 * 勝山稲荷神社（国登録有形文化財） - 祭神：[[ウカノミタマ|宇迦之御魂大神]]。[[1967年]]（昭和42年）に[[大阪]]から移築したもの。拝殿（国登録有形文化財）もある。 * 元稲荷社 == 祭礼 == * 年頭祭 * 索餅祭 == 文化財 == === 重要文化財 === * 本殿 附：棟札5枚 * 紙本墨書 [[日本書紀]] 神代巻下巻 - [[延喜]]4年（[[904年]]）の奥書があるが、[[南北朝時代 (日本)|南北朝時代]]の写本である[https://www.city.muko.kyoto.jp/rekimachi/rekishi_bunkazai/bunkazai/bunkazai/kuni/kunisiteitou/1441755502103.html 紙本墨書日本書紀神代紀下巻（向日市サイト）]。 === 国登録有形文化財 === * 幣拝殿及び本殿覆屋平成27年8月4日文部科学省告示第125号（本件以下「手水舎」まで、同告示による登録） * 南門・北門及び瑞垣 * 末社祖霊社本殿 * 末社祖霊社渡廊 * 末社五社神社本殿 * 末社春日神社本殿 * 末社御霊神社本殿 * 末社天満宮社本殿 * 末社天満宮社瑞垣 * 末社勝山稲荷神社本殿 * 末社勝山稲荷神社拝殿 * 客殿 * 手水舎 === その他 === * 飾太刀 == 交通 == ; 最寄駅 : [[阪急京都本線]][[西向日駅]]から北西へ500m == 脚注 == {{Reflist}} == 関連項目 == * [[六人部是香]]　幕末の国学者。当社の神官。 == 外部リンク == *{{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/mukojinjahp/index.htm |title=向日神社 |date=20190101000000}}公式ホームページ *{{Commonscat-inline}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:むこうしんしや}} [[Category:京都府の神社]] [[Category:山城国の式内社]] [[Category:京都府の重要文化財]] [[Category:重要文化財 (建造物)]] [[Category:京都府の登録有形文化財]] [[Category:向日市の建築物]] [[Category:向日市の歴史]] [[Category:桜に関する場所]]
