revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Mukō Shrine | location = Mukōchō Kitayama 65, [[Mukō]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]], Japan | Latitude degrees = 34 | Latitude minutes = 56 | Latitude seconds = 38.8 | N/S = N | Longitude degrees = 135 | Longitude minutes = 41 | Longitude seconds = 49.6 | E/W = E | Map country code = JP | Map = Japan Kyoto | Main deity = {{ill|Toshigami|ja|年神|1=Toshigami}}, {{ill|Honoikazuchi no Ōkami|ja|火雷大神}}, {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命}}, {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}} | Shrine Rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社}} and small shrine), former {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|府社}} | Main hall = Three-bay nagare-zukuri (Important Cultural Property) | festival = Second Sunday in May | Religious rituals = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Muko-jinja, shaden.jpg|280px]]
Dance Hall (right) and Worship Hall (left background) }} {{osm box|w|48434848}} [[File:Muko-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Mukō Shrine''' (向日神社, Mukō Jinja), also known as '''Mukō Myōjin''', is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in Mukōchō Kitayama, [[Mukō]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]]. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha}} (Myōjin Taisha and small shrine) and a former prefectural shrine. == Deities == * Main deities: {{ill|Toshigami|ja|年神|1=Toshigami}}, {{ill|Yakusanoikazuchi|ja|火雷大神}}, {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命}}, {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}} == History == Originally, Mukō Shrine consisted of two separate shrines on Mukō Mountain: {{ill|Muka Shrine|ja|向神社}} (U11er Shrine) and {{ill|Honoikazuchi Shrine|ja|火雷神社}} (Lower Shrine). Both are ancient shrines mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho}}, with Honoikazuchi Shrine being one of the candidates for the {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社}} "Otokuni-zazuchi Shrine" (other candidates include {{ill|Sumiyagashira Shrine|ja|角宮神社}} in {{ill|Nagaokakyō|ja|長岡京市}}). Muka Shrine is believed to have originated when Toshigami (Mukō Kami) stayed on Mukō Mountain and promoted rice cultivation. Initially, the shrine was located atop the {{ill|Gotsukahara Kofun|ja|五塚原古墳}}, 500 meters north of its current location. In 718 (2nd year of {{ill|Yōrō|ja|養老}}), the shrine was relocated to its current position south of the {{ill|Moto-Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳}}. Honoikazuchi Shrine is said to have been established when Emperor Jimmu enshrined the deity Honoikazuchi during his journey from {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province}} to {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|山背国}}. It is also believed to have been founded around 518 (12th year of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|継体天皇}}), when Keitai built the {{ill|Otokuni Palace|ja|乙訓宮}}. In 718, Honoikazuchi Shrine incorporated Tamayorihime and Emperor Jimmu as deities. In 784 (3rd year of {{ill|Enryaku|ja|延暦}}), the establishment of {{ill|Nagaoka-kyō|ja|長岡京}} brought the imperial palace, Nagaoka-no-miya, to the eastern foot of Muka Shrine. The chief priest of Honoikazuchi Shrine joined Emperor Go-Toba's forces during the {{ill|Jōkyū Rebellion|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221 but later fled to the {{ill|Rokuto-no-shō|ja|六人部荘}} estate in present-day {{ill|Fukuchiyama|ja|福知山市}}. After returning, the shrine's influence waned, and in 1275 (1st year of {{ill|Kenji|ja|建治}}), Muka Shrine merged with Honoikazuchi Shrine, forming Mukō Shrine and becoming the area's guardian shrine. The current main hall was rebuilt in 1422 (29th year of {{ill|Ōei|ja|応永}}) with the cooperation of seven surrounding villages. During the Muromachi period, Mukō Shrine became a rallying point for local {{ill|Tsuchi Ikki|ja|土一揆}} peasant uprisings before they marched on Kyoto. In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine. In 1961 (36th year of {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}}), the surrounding hill was partially excavated for the construction of the {{ill|Meishin Expressway|ja|名神高速道路}}. Today, the shrine and Moto-Inari Kofun remain as a small hill. In recent years, the shrine has been surrounded by a cherry blossom park, partially restoring the area’s former beauty. The main hall of Tokyo's {{ill|Meiji Shrine|ja|明治神宮}} is modeled after Mukō Shrine's main hall, scaled up by 1.5 times. == Grounds == [[File:Mukō-jinja-Cherry Blossoms of a11roach to a shrine.jpg|thumb|Path lined with cherry blossoms leading to the shrine]] From {{ill|Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|ja|京都府道・大阪府道67号西京高槻線}} (Astro Street), visitors enter a long, stone-paved path lined with cherry, azalea, and maple trees. The incline is scenic but shared with vehicles, requiring caution. * Main Hall (Important Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1422 during the Muromachi period. Representative of nagare-zukuri architecture. The model for the Meiji Shrine. ''' Main Hall Shelter (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842 (13th year of {{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保}}). * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842. * North and South Gates (Registered Tangible Cultural Properties) – Built in 1842. * Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1842. * Kagura Hall – Rebuilt in 2003 after the original structure (built in 1625) was lost in a 2002 fire. * Guest Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Relocated from a youth training facility in Osaka around 1965. * Purification Pavilion (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Late 18th-century reconstruction. === Auxiliary Shrines === * Ancestral Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1688 (1st year of {{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}}). * Five Shrines (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Dedicated to various deities. * Other shrines include {{ill|Goryō Shrine|ja|御霊神社}}, {{ill|Tenmangū|ja|天満宮}}, and Katsuyama Inari Shrine. == Festivals == * festival – Second Sunday in May * Sōmen Festival == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall and five ridge plaques. * Paper manuscript of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} (Lower Chapter, Age of the Gods) – A 14th-century transcription. === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Multiple structures, including the Main Hall Shelter, North and South Gates, and various auxiliary shrines. == Access == * Nearest station: [[Hankyu Kyoto Line]] [[Nishimukō Station]], 500 meters northwest. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Mutsume Koreka|ja|六人部是香}} – Late Edo-period scholar and former shrine priest. == External Links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20190101000000/http://www.geocities.jp/mukojinjahp/index.htm Mukō Shrine Official Website (archived)] * {{Commonscat-inline}} {{shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Tangible Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Historic sites in Mukō]] [[Category:Cherry blossom spots in Japan]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Mukō Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Muko Shrine]] [[ja:向日神社]] [[en:Muko Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Muko Shrine}} {{translated page|ja|向日神社|version=99695516|comment=Imported full ja history}}

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Mukō Shrine | location = Mukōchō Kitayama 65, [[Mukō]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]], Japan | Latitude degrees = 34 | Latitude minutes = 56 | Latitude seconds = 38.8 | N/S = N | Longitude degrees = 135 | Longitude minutes = 41 | Longitude seconds = 49.6 | E/W = E | Map country code = JP | Map = Japan Kyoto | Main deity = {{ill|Toshigami|ja|年神|1=Toshigami|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Honoikazuchi no Ōkami|ja|火雷大神|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇|qq=draft}} | Shrine Rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|qq=draft}} and small shrine), former {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|府社|qq=draft}} | Main hall = Three-bay nagare-zukuri (Important Cultural Property) | festival = Second Sunday in May | Religious rituals = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Muko-jinja, shaden.jpg|280px]]
Dance Hall (right) and Worship Hall (left background) }} {{osm box|w|48434848}} [[File:Muko-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Mukō Shrine''' (向日神社, Mukō Jinja), also known as '''Mukō Myōjin''', is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft}} located in Mukōchō Kitayama, [[Mukō]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]]. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft}} (Myōjin Taisha and small shrine) and a former prefectural shrine. == Deities == * Main deities: {{ill|Toshigami|ja|年神|1=Toshigami|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Yakusanoikazuchi|ja|火雷大神|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇|qq=draft}} == History == Originally, Mukō Shrine consisted of two separate shrines on Mukō Mountain: {{ill|Muka Shrine|ja|向神社|qq=draft}} (U11er Shrine) and {{ill|Honoikazuchi Shrine|ja|火雷神社|qq=draft}} (Lower Shrine). Both are ancient shrines mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|qq=draft}}, with Honoikazuchi Shrine being one of the candidates for the {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|qq=draft}} "Otokuni-zazuchi Shrine" (other candidates include {{ill|Sumiyagashira Shrine|ja|角宮神社|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Nagaokakyō|ja|長岡京市|qq=draft}}). Muka Shrine is believed to have originated when Toshigami (Mukō Kami) stayed on Mukō Mountain and promoted rice cultivation. Initially, the shrine was located atop the {{ill|Gotsukahara Kofun|ja|五塚原古墳|qq=draft}}, 500 meters north of its current location. In 718 (2nd year of {{ill|Yōrō|ja|養老|qq=draft}}), the shrine was relocated to its current position south of the {{ill|Moto-Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳|qq=draft}}. Honoikazuchi Shrine is said to have been established when Emperor Jimmu enshrined the deity Honoikazuchi during his journey from {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|qq=draft}} to {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|山背国|qq=draft}}. It is also believed to have been founded around 518 (12th year of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|継体天皇|qq=draft}}), when Keitai built the {{ill|Otokuni Palace|ja|乙訓宮|qq=draft}}. In 718, Honoikazuchi Shrine incorporated Tamayorihime and Emperor Jimmu as deities. In 784 (3rd year of {{ill|Enryaku|ja|延暦|qq=draft}}), the establishment of {{ill|Nagaoka-kyō|ja|長岡京|qq=draft}} brought the imperial palace, Nagaoka-no-miya, to the eastern foot of Muka Shrine. The chief priest of Honoikazuchi Shrine joined Emperor Go-Toba's forces during the {{ill|Jōkyū Rebellion|ja|承久の乱|qq=draft}} in 1221 but later fled to the {{ill|Rokuto-no-shō|ja|六人部荘|qq=draft}} estate in present-day {{ill|Fukuchiyama|ja|福知山市|qq=draft}}. After returning, the shrine's influence waned, and in 1275 (1st year of {{ill|Kenji|ja|建治|qq=draft}}), Muka Shrine merged with Honoikazuchi Shrine, forming Mukō Shrine and becoming the area's guardian shrine. The current main hall was rebuilt in 1422 (29th year of {{ill|Ōei|ja|応永|qq=draft}}) with the cooperation of seven surrounding villages. During the Muromachi period, Mukō Shrine became a rallying point for local {{ill|Tsuchi Ikki|ja|土一揆|qq=draft}} peasant uprisings before they marched on Kyoto. In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine. In 1961 (36th year of {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和|qq=draft}}), the surrounding hill was partially excavated for the construction of the {{ill|Meishin Expressway|ja|名神高速道路|qq=draft}}. Today, the shrine and Moto-Inari Kofun remain as a small hill. In recent years, the shrine has been surrounded by a cherry blossom park, partially restoring the area’s former beauty. The main hall of Tokyo's {{ill|Meiji Shrine|ja|明治神宮|qq=draft}} is modeled after Mukō Shrine's main hall, scaled up by 1.5 times. == Grounds == [[File:Mukō-jinja-Cherry Blossoms of a11roach to a shrine.jpg|thumb|Path lined with cherry blossoms leading to the shrine]] From {{ill|Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|ja|京都府道・大阪府道67号西京高槻線|qq=draft}} (Astro Street), visitors enter a long, stone-paved path lined with cherry, azalea, and maple trees. The incline is scenic but shared with vehicles, requiring caution. * Main Hall (Important Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1422 during the Muromachi period. Representative of nagare-zukuri architecture. The model for the Meiji Shrine. ''' Main Hall Shelter (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842 (13th year of {{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保|qq=draft}}). * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842. * North and South Gates (Registered Tangible Cultural Properties) – Built in 1842. * Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1842. * Kagura Hall – Rebuilt in 2003 after the original structure (built in 1625) was lost in a 2002 fire. * Guest Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Relocated from a youth training facility in Osaka around 1965. * Purification Pavilion (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Late 18th-century reconstruction. === Auxiliary Shrines === * Ancestral Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1688 (1st year of {{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄|qq=draft}}). * Five Shrines (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Dedicated to various deities. * Other shrines include {{ill|Goryō Shrine|ja|御霊神社|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Tenmangū|ja|天満宮|qq=draft}}, and Katsuyama Inari Shrine. == Festivals == * festival – Second Sunday in May * Sōmen Festival == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall and five ridge plaques. * Paper manuscript of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft}} (Lower Chapter, Age of the Gods) – A 14th-century transcription. === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Multiple structures, including the Main Hall Shelter, North and South Gates, and various auxiliary shrines. == Access == * Nearest station: [[Hankyu Kyoto Line]] [[Nishimukō Station]], 500 meters northwest. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Mutsume Koreka|ja|六人部是香|qq=draft}} – Late Edo-period scholar and former shrine priest. == External Links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20190101000000/http://www.geocities.jp/mukojinjahp/index.htm Mukō Shrine Official Website (archived)] * {{Commonscat-inline}} {{shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|向日神社|version=99695516|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Muko Shrine}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Tangible Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Historic sites in Mukō]] [[Category:Cherry blossom spots in Japan]] [[Category:Mukō Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] }} [[sn:Muko Shrine]] [[ja:向日神社]] [[en:Muko Shrine]]

meters northwest. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Mutsume Koreka|ja|六人部是香|qq=draft}} – Late Edo-period scholar and former shrine priest. == External Links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20190101000000/http://www.geocities.jp/mukojinjahp/index.htm Mukō Shrine Official Website (archived)] * {{Commonscat-inline}} {{shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|向日神社|version=99695516|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Mukō Shrine | location = Mukōchō Kitayama 65, [[Mukō]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]], Japan | Latitude degrees = 34 | Latitude minutes = 56 | Latitude seconds = 38.8 | N/S = N | Longitude degrees = 135 | Longitude minutes = 41 | Longitude seconds = 49.6 | E/W = E | Map country code = JP | Map = Japan Kyoto | Main deity = {{ill|Toshigami|ja|年神|1=Toshigami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshigami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshigami|qq=}}, {{ill|Honoikazuchi no Ōkami|ja|火雷大神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honoikazuchi no Ōkami|qq=}}, {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamayori-hime|qq=}}, {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Jimmu|qq=}} | Shrine Rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|qq=}} and small shrine), former {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|府社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|qq=}} | Main hall = Three-bay nagare-zukuri (Important Cultural Property) | festival = Second Sunday in May | Religious rituals = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Muko-jinja, shaden.jpg|280px]]
Dance Hall (right) and Worship Hall (left background) }} {{osm box|w|48434848}} [[File:Muko-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Mukō Shrine''' (向日神社, Mukō Jinja), also known as '''Mukō Myōjin''', is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto shrine|qq=}} located in Mukōchō Kitayama, [[Mukō]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]]. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}} (Myōjin Taisha and small shrine) and a former prefectural shrine. == Deities == * Main deities: {{ill|Toshigami|ja|年神|1=Toshigami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshigami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshigami|qq=}}, {{ill|Yakusanoikazuchi|ja|火雷大神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakusanoikazuchi|qq=}}, {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamayori-hime|qq=}}, {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Jimmu|qq=}} == History == Originally, Mukō Shrine consisted of two separate shrines on Mukō Mountain: {{ill|Muka Shrine|ja|向神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muka Shrine|qq=}} (U11er Shrine) and {{ill|Honoikazuchi Shrine|ja|火雷神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honoikazuchi Shrine|qq=}} (Lower Shrine). Both are ancient shrines mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, with Honoikazuchi Shrine being one of the candidates for the {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|qq=}} "Otokuni-zazuchi Shrine" (other candidates include {{ill|Sumiyagashira Shrine|ja|角宮神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyagashira Shrine|qq=}} in {{ill|Nagaokakyō|ja|長岡京市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagaokakyō|qq=}}). Muka Shrine is believed to have originated when Toshigami (Mukō Kami) stayed on Mukō Mountain and promoted rice cultivation. Initially, the shrine was located atop the {{ill|Gotsukahara Kofun|ja|五塚原古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gotsukahara Kofun|qq=}}, 500 meters north of its current location. In 718 (2nd year of {{ill|Yōrō|ja|養老|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō|qq=}}), the shrine was relocated to its current position south of the {{ill|Moto-Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Inari Kofun|qq=}}. Honoikazuchi Shrine is said to have been established when Emperor Jimmu enshrined the deity Honoikazuchi during his journey from {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Province|qq=}} to {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|山背国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashiro Province|qq=}}. It is also believed to have been founded around 518 (12th year of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|継体天皇|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Keitai|qq=}}), when Keitai built the {{ill|Otokuni Palace|ja|乙訓宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otokuni Palace|qq=}}. In 718, Honoikazuchi Shrine incorporated Tamayorihime and Emperor Jimmu as deities. In 784 (3rd year of {{ill|Enryaku|ja|延暦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enryaku|qq=}}), the establishment of {{ill|Nagaoka-kyō|ja|長岡京|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagaoka-kyō|qq=}} brought the imperial palace, Nagaoka-no-miya, to the eastern foot of Muka Shrine. The chief priest of Honoikazuchi Shrine joined Emperor Go-Toba's forces during the {{ill|Jōkyū Rebellion|ja|承久の乱|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōkyū Rebellion|qq=}} in 1221 but later fled to the {{ill|Rokuto-no-shō|ja|六人部荘|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokuto-no-shō|qq=}} estate in present-day {{ill|Fukuchiyama|ja|福知山市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukuchiyama|qq=}}. After returning, the shrine's influence waned, and in 1275 (1st year of {{ill|Kenji|ja|建治|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenji|qq=}}), Muka Shrine merged with Honoikazuchi Shrine, forming Mukō Shrine and becoming the area's guardian shrine. The current main hall was rebuilt in 1422 (29th year of {{ill|Ōei|ja|応永|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōei|qq=}}) with the cooperation of seven surrounding villages. During the Muromachi period, Mukō Shrine became a rallying point for local {{ill|Tsuchi Ikki|ja|土一揆|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchi Ikki|qq=}} peasant uprisings before they marched on Kyoto. In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine. In 1961 (36th year of {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa|qq=}}), the surrounding hill was partially excavated for the construction of the {{ill|Meishin Expressway|ja|名神高速道路|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meishin Expressway|qq=}}. Today, the shrine and Moto-Inari Kofun remain as a small hill. In recent years, the shrine has been surrounded by a cherry blossom park, partially restoring the area’s former beauty. The main hall of Tokyo's {{ill|Meiji Shrine|ja|明治神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Shrine|qq=}} is modeled after Mukō Shrine's main hall, scaled up by 1.5 times. == Grounds == [[File:Mukō-jinja-Cherry Blossoms of a11roach to a shrine.jpg|thumb|Path lined with cherry blossoms leading to the shrine]] From {{ill|Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|ja|京都府道・大阪府道67号西京高槻線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|qq=}} (Astro Street), visitors enter a long, stone-paved path lined with cherry, azalea, and maple trees. The incline is scenic but shared with vehicles, requiring caution. * Main Hall (Important Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1422 during the Muromachi period. Representative of nagare-zukuri architecture. The model for the Meiji Shrine. ''' Main Hall Shelter (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842 (13th year of {{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpō|qq=}}). * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842. * North and South Gates (Registered Tangible Cultural Properties) – Built in 1842. * Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1842. * Kagura Hall – Rebuilt in 2003 after the original structure (built in 1625) was lost in a 2002 fire. * Guest Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Relocated from a youth training facility in Osaka around 1965. * Purification Pavilion (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Late 18th-century reconstruction. === Auxiliary Shrines === * Ancestral Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1688 (1st year of {{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genroku|qq=}}). * Five Shrines (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Dedicated to various deities. * Other shrines include {{ill|Goryō Shrine|ja|御霊神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goryō Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Tenmangū|ja|天満宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmangū|qq=}}, and Katsuyama Inari Shrine. == Festivals == * festival – Second Sunday in May * Sōmen Festival == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall and five ridge plaques. * Paper manuscript of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Shoki|qq=}} (Lower Chapter, Age of the Gods) – A 14th-century transcription. === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Multiple structures, including the Main Hall Shelter, North and South Gates, and various auxiliary shrines. == Access == * Nearest station: [[Hankyu Kyoto Line]] [[Nishimukō Station]], 500 meters northwest. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Mutsume Koreka|ja|六人部是香|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mutsume Koreka|qq=}} – Late Edo-period scholar and former shrine priest. == External Links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20190101000000/http://www.geocities.jp/mukojinjahp/index.htm Mukō Shrine Official Website (archived)] * {{Commonscat-inline}} {{shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|向日神社|version=99695516|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Tangible Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Historic sites in Mukō]] [[Category:Cherry blossom spots in Japan]] [[Category:Mukō Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] }} [[sn:Muko Shrine]] [[ja:向日神社]] [[en:Muko Shrine]]

revision.comment: Text replacement - "Engishiki Jinmyocho" to "Engishiki Jinmyōchō"
revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Mukō Shrine | location = Mukōchō Kitayama 65, [[Mukō]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]], Japan | Latitude degrees = 34 | Latitude minutes = 56 | Latitude seconds = 38.8 | N/S = N | Longitude degrees = 135 | Longitude minutes = 41 | Longitude seconds = 49.6 | E/W = E | Map country code = JP | Map = Japan Kyoto | Main deity = {{ill|Toshigami|ja|年神|1=Toshigami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshigami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshigami|qq=}}, {{ill|Honoikazuchi no Ōkami|ja|火雷大神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honoikazuchi no Ōkami|qq=}}, {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamayori-hime|qq=}}, {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Jimmu|qq=}} | Shrine Rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|qq=}} and small shrine), former {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|府社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|qq=}} | Main hall = Three-bay nagare-zukuri (Important Cultural Property) | festival = Second Sunday in May | Religious rituals = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Muko-jinja, shaden.jpg|280px]]
Dance Hall (right) and Worship Hall (left background) }} {{osm box|w|48434848}} [[File:Muko-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Mukō Shrine''' (向日神社, Mukō Jinja), also known as '''Mukō Myōjin''', is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto shrine|qq=}} located in Mukōchō Kitayama, [[Mukō]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]]. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}} (Myōjin Taisha and small shrine) and a former prefectural shrine. == Deities == * Main deities: {{ill|Toshigami|ja|年神|1=Toshigami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshigami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshigami|qq=}}, {{ill|Yakusanoikazuchi|ja|火雷大神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakusanoikazuchi|qq=}}, {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamayori-hime|qq=}}, {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Jimmu|qq=}} == History == Originally, Mukō Shrine consisted of two separate shrines on Mukō Mountain: {{ill|Muka Shrine|ja|向神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muka Shrine|qq=}} (U11er Shrine) and {{ill|Honoikazuchi Shrine|ja|火雷神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honoikazuchi Shrine|qq=}} (Lower Shrine). Both are ancient shrines mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, with Honoikazuchi Shrine being one of the candidates for the {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|qq=}} "Otokuni-zazuchi Shrine" (other candidates include {{ill|Sumiyagashira Shrine|ja|角宮神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyagashira Shrine|qq=}} in {{ill|Nagaokakyō|ja|長岡京市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagaokakyō|qq=}}). Muka Shrine is believed to have originated when Toshigami (Mukō Kami) stayed on Mukō Mountain and promoted rice cultivation. Initially, the shrine was located atop the {{ill|Gotsukahara Kofun|ja|五塚原古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gotsukahara Kofun|qq=}}, 500 meters north of its current location. In 718 (2nd year of {{ill|Yōrō|ja|養老|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō|qq=}}), the shrine was relocated to its current position south of the {{ill|Moto-Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Inari Kofun|qq=}}. Honoikazuchi Shrine is said to have been established when Emperor Jimmu enshrined the deity Honoikazuchi during his journey from {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国|qq=Yamato Province|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Province|qq=}} to {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|山背国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashiro Province|qq=}}. It is also believed to have been founded around 518 (12th year of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|継体天皇|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Keitai|qq=}}), when Keitai built the {{ill|Otokuni Palace|ja|乙訓宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otokuni Palace|qq=}}. In 718, Honoikazuchi Shrine incorporated Tamayorihime and Emperor Jimmu as deities. In 784 (3rd year of {{ill|Enryaku|ja|延暦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enryaku|qq=}}), the establishment of {{ill|Nagaoka-kyō|ja|長岡京|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagaoka-kyō|qq=}} brought the imperial palace, Nagaoka-no-miya, to the eastern foot of Muka Shrine. The chief priest of Honoikazuchi Shrine joined Emperor Go-Toba's forces during the {{ill|Jōkyū Rebellion|ja|承久の乱|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōkyū Rebellion|qq=}} in 1221 but later fled to the {{ill|Rokuto-no-shō|ja|六人部荘|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokuto-no-shō|qq=}} estate in present-day {{ill|Fukuchiyama|ja|福知山市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukuchiyama|qq=}}. After returning, the shrine's influence waned, and in 1275 (1st year of {{ill|Kenji|ja|建治|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenji|qq=}}), Muka Shrine merged with Honoikazuchi Shrine, forming Mukō Shrine and becoming the area's guardian shrine. The current main hall was rebuilt in 1422 (29th year of {{ill|Ōei|ja|応永|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōei|qq=}}) with the cooperation of seven surrounding villages. During the Muromachi period, Mukō Shrine became a rallying point for local {{ill|Tsuchi Ikki|ja|土一揆|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchi Ikki|qq=}} peasant uprisings before they marched on Kyoto. In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine. In 1961 (36th year of {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa|qq=}}), the surrounding hill was partially excavated for the construction of the {{ill|Meishin Expressway|ja|名神高速道路|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meishin Expressway|qq=}}. Today, the shrine and Moto-Inari Kofun remain as a small hill. In recent years, the shrine has been surrounded by a cherry blossom park, partially restoring the area’s former beauty. The main hall of Tokyo's {{ill|Meiji Shrine|ja|明治神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Shrine|qq=}} is modeled after Mukō Shrine's main hall, scaled up by 1.5 times. == Grounds == [[File:Mukō-jinja-Cherry Blossoms of a11roach to a shrine.jpg|thumb|Path lined with cherry blossoms leading to the shrine]] From {{ill|Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|ja|京都府道・大阪府道67号西京高槻線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|qq=}} (Astro Street), visitors enter a long, stone-paved path lined with cherry, azalea, and maple trees. The incline is scenic but shared with vehicles, requiring caution. * Main Hall (Important Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1422 during the Muromachi period. Representative of nagare-zukuri architecture. The model for the Meiji Shrine. ''' Main Hall Shelter (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842 (13th year of {{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpō|qq=}}). * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842. * North and South Gates (Registered Tangible Cultural Properties) – Built in 1842. * Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1842. * Kagura Hall – Rebuilt in 2003 after the original structure (built in 1625) was lost in a 2002 fire. * Guest Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Relocated from a youth training facility in Osaka around 1965. * Purification Pavilion (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Late 18th-century reconstruction. === Auxiliary Shrines === * Ancestral Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1688 (1st year of {{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genroku|qq=}}). * Five Shrines (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Dedicated to various deities. * Other shrines include {{ill|Goryō Shrine|ja|御霊神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goryō Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Tenmangū|ja|天満宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmangū|qq=}}, and Katsuyama Inari Shrine. == Festivals == * festival – Second Sunday in May * Sōmen Festival == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall and five ridge plaques. * Paper manuscript of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Shoki|qq=}} (Lower Chapter, Age of the Gods) – A 14th-century transcription. === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Multiple structures, including the Main Hall Shelter, North and South Gates, and various auxiliary shrines. == Access == * Nearest station: [[Hankyu Kyoto Line]] [[Nishimukō Station]], 500 meters northwest. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Mutsume Koreka|ja|六人部是香|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mutsume Koreka|qq=}} – Late Edo-period scholar and former shrine priest. == External Links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20190101000000/http://www.geocities.jp/mukojinjahp/index.htm Mukō Shrine Official Website (archived)] * {{Commonscat-inline}} {{shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|向日神社|version=99695516|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Tangible Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Historic sites in Mukō]] [[Category:Cherry blossom spots in Japan]] [[Category:Mukō Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] }} [[sn:Muko Shrine]] [[ja:向日神社]] [[en:Muko Shrine]]

revision.comment: Bot: fix redirect [[Gotsukahara Kofun]] → [[Itsukahara Kofun]]
revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Mukō Shrine | location = Mukōchō Kitayama 65, [[Mukō]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]], Japan | Latitude degrees = 34 | Latitude minutes = 56 | Latitude seconds = 38.8 | N/S = N | Longitude degrees = 135 | Longitude minutes = 41 | Longitude seconds = 49.6 | E/W = E | Map country code = JP | Map = Japan Kyoto | Main deity = {{ill|Toshigami|ja|年神|1=Toshigami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshigami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshigami|qq=}}, {{ill|Honoikazuchi no Ōkami|ja|火雷大神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honoikazuchi no Ōkami|qq=}}, {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamayori-hime|qq=}}, {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Jimmu|qq=}} | Shrine Rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|qq=}} and small shrine), former {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|府社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|qq=}} | Main hall = Three-bay nagare-zukuri (Important Cultural Property) | festival = Second Sunday in May | Religious rituals = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Muko-jinja, shaden.jpg|280px]]
Dance Hall (right) and Worship Hall (left background) }} {{osm box|w|48434848}} [[File:Muko-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Mukō Shrine''' (向日神社, Mukō Jinja), also known as '''Mukō Myōjin''', is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto shrine|qq=}} located in Mukōchō Kitayama, [[Mukō]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]]. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}} (Myōjin Taisha and small shrine) and a former prefectural shrine. == Deities == * Main deities: {{ill|Toshigami|ja|年神|1=Toshigami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshigami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshigami|qq=}}, {{ill|Yakusanoikazuchi|ja|火雷大神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakusanoikazuchi|qq=}}, {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamayori-hime|qq=}}, {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Jimmu|qq=}} == History == Originally, Mukō Shrine consisted of two separate shrines on Mukō Mountain: {{ill|Muka Shrine|ja|向神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muka Shrine|qq=}} (U11er Shrine) and {{ill|Honoikazuchi Shrine|ja|火雷神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honoikazuchi Shrine|qq=}} (Lower Shrine). Both are ancient shrines mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, with Honoikazuchi Shrine being one of the candidates for the {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|qq=}} "Otokuni-zazuchi Shrine" (other candidates include {{ill|Sumiyagashira Shrine|ja|角宮神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyagashira Shrine|qq=}} in {{ill|Nagaokakyō|ja|長岡京市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagaokakyō|qq=}}). Muka Shrine is believed to have originated when Toshigami (Mukō Kami) stayed on Mukō Mountain and promoted rice cultivation. Initially, the shrine was located atop the {{ill|Itsukahara Kofun|lt=Itsukahara Kofun|lt=Gotsukahara Kofun|ja|五塚原古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gotsukahara Kofun|qq=}}, 500 meters north of its current location. In 718 (2nd year of {{ill|Yōrō|ja|養老|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō|qq=}}), the shrine was relocated to its current position south of the {{ill|Moto-Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Inari Kofun|qq=}}. Honoikazuchi Shrine is said to have been established when Emperor Jimmu enshrined the deity Honoikazuchi during his journey from {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国|qq=Yamato Province|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Province|qq=}} to {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|山背国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashiro Province|qq=}}. It is also believed to have been founded around 518 (12th year of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|継体天皇|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Keitai|qq=}}), when Keitai built the {{ill|Otokuni Palace|ja|乙訓宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otokuni Palace|qq=}}. In 718, Honoikazuchi Shrine incorporated Tamayorihime and Emperor Jimmu as deities. In 784 (3rd year of {{ill|Enryaku|ja|延暦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enryaku|qq=}}), the establishment of {{ill|Nagaoka-kyō|ja|長岡京|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagaoka-kyō|qq=}} brought the imperial palace, Nagaoka-no-miya, to the eastern foot of Muka Shrine. The chief priest of Honoikazuchi Shrine joined Emperor Go-Toba's forces during the {{ill|Jōkyū Rebellion|ja|承久の乱|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōkyū Rebellion|qq=}} in 1221 but later fled to the {{ill|Rokuto-no-shō|ja|六人部荘|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokuto-no-shō|qq=}} estate in present-day {{ill|Fukuchiyama|ja|福知山市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukuchiyama|qq=}}. After returning, the shrine's influence waned, and in 1275 (1st year of {{ill|Kenji|ja|建治|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenji|qq=}}), Muka Shrine merged with Honoikazuchi Shrine, forming Mukō Shrine and becoming the area's guardian shrine. The current main hall was rebuilt in 1422 (29th year of {{ill|Ōei|ja|応永|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōei|qq=}}) with the cooperation of seven surrounding villages. During the Muromachi period, Mukō Shrine became a rallying point for local {{ill|Tsuchi Ikki|ja|土一揆|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchi Ikki|qq=}} peasant uprisings before they marched on Kyoto. In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine. In 1961 (36th year of {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa|qq=}}), the surrounding hill was partially excavated for the construction of the {{ill|Meishin Expressway|ja|名神高速道路|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meishin Expressway|qq=}}. Today, the shrine and Moto-Inari Kofun remain as a small hill. In recent years, the shrine has been surrounded by a cherry blossom park, partially restoring the area’s former beauty. The main hall of Tokyo's {{ill|Meiji Shrine|ja|明治神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Shrine|qq=}} is modeled after Mukō Shrine's main hall, scaled up by 1.5 times. == Grounds == [[File:Mukō-jinja-Cherry Blossoms of a11roach to a shrine.jpg|thumb|Path lined with cherry blossoms leading to the shrine]] From {{ill|Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|ja|京都府道・大阪府道67号西京高槻線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|qq=}} (Astro Street), visitors enter a long, stone-paved path lined with cherry, azalea, and maple trees. The incline is scenic but shared with vehicles, requiring caution. * Main Hall (Important Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1422 during the Muromachi period. Representative of nagare-zukuri architecture. The model for the Meiji Shrine. ''' Main Hall Shelter (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842 (13th year of {{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpō|qq=}}). * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842. * North and South Gates (Registered Tangible Cultural Properties) – Built in 1842. * Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1842. * Kagura Hall – Rebuilt in 2003 after the original structure (built in 1625) was lost in a 2002 fire. * Guest Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Relocated from a youth training facility in Osaka around 1965. * Purification Pavilion (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Late 18th-century reconstruction. === Auxiliary Shrines === * Ancestral Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1688 (1st year of {{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genroku|qq=}}). * Five Shrines (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Dedicated to various deities. * Other shrines include {{ill|Goryo Shrine|lt=Goryo Shrine|lt=Goryō Shrine|ja|御霊神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goryō Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Tenmangū|ja|天満宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmangū|qq=}}, and Katsuyama Inari Shrine. == Festivals == * festival – Second Sunday in May * Sōmen Festival == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall and five ridge plaques. * Paper manuscript of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Shoki|qq=}} (Lower Chapter, Age of the Gods) – A 14th-century transcription. === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Multiple structures, including the Main Hall Shelter, North and South Gates, and various auxiliary shrines. == Access == * Nearest station: [[Hankyu Kyoto Line]] [[Nishimukō Station]], 500 meters northwest. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Mutsume Koreka|ja|六人部是香|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mutsume Koreka|qq=}} – Late Edo-period scholar and former shrine priest. == External Links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20190101000000/http://www.geocities.jp/mukojinjahp/index.htm Mukō Shrine Official Website (archived)] * {{Commonscat-inline}} {{shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|向日神社|version=99695516|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Tangible Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Historic sites in Mukō]] [[Category:Cherry blossom spots in Japan]] [[Category:Mukō Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] }} [[sn:Muko Shrine]] [[ja:向日神社]] [[en:Muko Shrine]]

revision.comment: Bot: fix redirect [[Meiji Shrine]] → [[Meiji Jingu]]
revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Mukō Shrine | location = Mukōchō Kitayama 65, [[Mukō]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]], Japan | Latitude degrees = 34 | Latitude minutes = 56 | Latitude seconds = 38.8 | N/S = N | Longitude degrees = 135 | Longitude minutes = 41 | Longitude seconds = 49.6 | E/W = E | Map country code = JP | Map = Japan Kyoto | Main deity = {{ill|Toshigami|ja|年神|1=Toshigami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshigami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshigami|qq=}}, {{ill|Honoikazuchi no Ōkami|ja|火雷大神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honoikazuchi no Ōkami|qq=}}, {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamayori-hime|qq=}}, {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Jimmu|qq=}} | Shrine Rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|qq=}} and small shrine), former {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|府社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|qq=}} | Main hall = Three-bay nagare-zukuri (Important Cultural Property) | festival = Second Sunday in May | Religious rituals = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Muko-jinja, shaden.jpg|280px]]
Dance Hall (right) and Worship Hall (left background) }} {{osm box|w|48434848}} [[File:Muko-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Mukō Shrine''' (向日神社, Mukō Jinja), also known as '''Mukō Myōjin''', is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto shrine|qq=}} located in Mukōchō Kitayama, [[Mukō]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]]. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}} (Myōjin Taisha and small shrine) and a former prefectural shrine. == Deities == * Main deities: {{ill|Toshigami|ja|年神|1=Toshigami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshigami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshigami|qq=}}, {{ill|Yakusanoikazuchi|ja|火雷大神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakusanoikazuchi|qq=}}, {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamayori-hime|qq=}}, {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Jimmu|qq=}} == History == Originally, Mukō Shrine consisted of two separate shrines on Mukō Mountain: {{ill|Muka Shrine|ja|向神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muka Shrine|qq=}} (U11er Shrine) and {{ill|Honoikazuchi Shrine|ja|火雷神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honoikazuchi Shrine|qq=}} (Lower Shrine). Both are ancient shrines mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, with Honoikazuchi Shrine being one of the candidates for the {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|qq=}} "Otokuni-zazuchi Shrine" (other candidates include {{ill|Sumiyagashira Shrine|ja|角宮神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyagashira Shrine|qq=}} in {{ill|Nagaokakyō|ja|長岡京市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagaokakyō|qq=}}). Muka Shrine is believed to have originated when Toshigami (Mukō Kami) stayed on Mukō Mountain and promoted rice cultivation. Initially, the shrine was located atop the {{ill|Itsukahara Kofun|lt=Itsukahara Kofun|lt=Gotsukahara Kofun|ja|五塚原古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gotsukahara Kofun|qq=}}, 500 meters north of its current location. In 718 (2nd year of {{ill|Yōrō|ja|養老|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō|qq=}}), the shrine was relocated to its current position south of the {{ill|Moto-Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Inari Kofun|qq=}}. Honoikazuchi Shrine is said to have been established when Emperor Jimmu enshrined the deity Honoikazuchi during his journey from {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国|qq=Yamato Province|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Province|qq=}} to {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|山背国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashiro Province|qq=}}. It is also believed to have been founded around 518 (12th year of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|継体天皇|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Keitai|qq=}}), when Keitai built the {{ill|Otokuni Palace|ja|乙訓宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otokuni Palace|qq=}}. In 718, Honoikazuchi Shrine incorporated Tamayorihime and Emperor Jimmu as deities. In 784 (3rd year of {{ill|Enryaku|ja|延暦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enryaku|qq=}}), the establishment of {{ill|Nagaoka-kyō|ja|長岡京|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagaoka-kyō|qq=}} brought the imperial palace, Nagaoka-no-miya, to the eastern foot of Muka Shrine. The chief priest of Honoikazuchi Shrine joined Emperor Go-Toba's forces during the {{ill|Jōkyū Rebellion|ja|承久の乱|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōkyū Rebellion|qq=}} in 1221 but later fled to the {{ill|Rokuto-no-shō|ja|六人部荘|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokuto-no-shō|qq=}} estate in present-day {{ill|Fukuchiyama|ja|福知山市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukuchiyama|qq=}}. After returning, the shrine's influence waned, and in 1275 (1st year of {{ill|Kenji|ja|建治|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenji|qq=}}), Muka Shrine merged with Honoikazuchi Shrine, forming Mukō Shrine and becoming the area's guardian shrine. The current main hall was rebuilt in 1422 (29th year of {{ill|Ōei|ja|応永|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōei|qq=}}) with the cooperation of seven surrounding villages. During the Muromachi period, Mukō Shrine became a rallying point for local {{ill|Tsuchi Ikki|ja|土一揆|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchi Ikki|qq=}} peasant uprisings before they marched on Kyoto. In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine. In 1961 (36th year of {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa|qq=}}), the surrounding hill was partially excavated for the construction of the {{ill|Meishin Expressway|ja|名神高速道路|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meishin Expressway|qq=}}. Today, the shrine and Moto-Inari Kofun remain as a small hill. In recent years, the shrine has been surrounded by a cherry blossom park, partially restoring the area’s former beauty. The main hall of Tokyo's {{ill|Meiji Jingu|lt=Meiji Jingu|lt=Meiji Shrine|ja|明治神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Shrine|qq=}} is modeled after Mukō Shrine's main hall, scaled up by 1.5 times. == Grounds == [[File:Mukō-jinja-Cherry Blossoms of a11roach to a shrine.jpg|thumb|Path lined with cherry blossoms leading to the shrine]] From {{ill|Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|ja|京都府道・大阪府道67号西京高槻線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|qq=}} (Astro Street), visitors enter a long, stone-paved path lined with cherry, azalea, and maple trees. The incline is scenic but shared with vehicles, requiring caution. * Main Hall (Important Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1422 during the Muromachi period. Representative of nagare-zukuri architecture. The model for the Meiji Shrine. ''' Main Hall Shelter (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842 (13th year of {{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpō|qq=}}). * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842. * North and South Gates (Registered Tangible Cultural Properties) – Built in 1842. * Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1842. * Kagura Hall – Rebuilt in 2003 after the original structure (built in 1625) was lost in a 2002 fire. * Guest Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Relocated from a youth training facility in Osaka around 1965. * Purification Pavilion (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Late 18th-century reconstruction. === Auxiliary Shrines === * Ancestral Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1688 (1st year of {{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genroku|qq=}}). * Five Shrines (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Dedicated to various deities. * Other shrines include {{ill|Goryo Shrine|lt=Goryo Shrine|lt=Goryō Shrine|ja|御霊神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goryō Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Tenmangū|ja|天満宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmangū|qq=}}, and Katsuyama Inari Shrine. == Festivals == * festival – Second Sunday in May * Sōmen Festival == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall and five ridge plaques. * Paper manuscript of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Shoki|qq=}} (Lower Chapter, Age of the Gods) – A 14th-century transcription. === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Multiple structures, including the Main Hall Shelter, North and South Gates, and various auxiliary shrines. == Access == * Nearest station: [[Hankyu Kyoto Line]] [[Nishimukō Station]], 500 meters northwest. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Mutsume Koreka|ja|六人部是香|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mutsume Koreka|qq=}} – Late Edo-period scholar and former shrine priest. == External Links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20190101000000/http://www.geocities.jp/mukojinjahp/index.htm Mukō Shrine Official Website (archived)] * {{Commonscat-inline}} {{shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|向日神社|version=99695516|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Tangible Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Historic sites in Mukō]] [[Category:Cherry blossom spots in Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] }} [[sn:Muko Shrine]] [[ja:向日神社]] [[en:Muko Shrine]]

revision.comment: Bot: fix redirect [[Moto-Inari Kofun]] → [[Motoinari Kofun]]
revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Mukō Shrine | location = Mukōchō Kitayama 65, [[Mukō]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]], Japan | Latitude degrees = 34 | Latitude minutes = 56 | Latitude seconds = 38.8 | N/S = N | Longitude degrees = 135 | Longitude minutes = 41 | Longitude seconds = 49.6 | E/W = E | Map country code = JP | Map = Japan Kyoto | Main deity = {{ill|Toshigami|ja|年神|1=Toshigami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshigami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshigami|qq=}}, {{ill|Honoikazuchi no Ōkami|ja|火雷大神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honoikazuchi no Ōkami|qq=}}, {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamayori-hime|qq=}}, {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Jimmu|qq=}} | Shrine Rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|qq=}} and small shrine), former {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|府社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|qq=}} | Main hall = Three-bay nagare-zukuri (Important Cultural Property) | festival = Second Sunday in May | Religious rituals = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Muko-jinja, shaden.jpg|280px]]
Dance Hall (right) and Worship Hall (left background) }} {{osm box|w|48434848}} [[File:Muko-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Mukō Shrine''' (向日神社, Mukō Jinja), also known as '''Mukō Myōjin''', is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto shrine|qq=}} located in Mukōchō Kitayama, [[Mukō]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]]. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}} (Myōjin Taisha and small shrine) and a former prefectural shrine. == Deities == * Main deities: {{ill|Toshigami|ja|年神|1=Toshigami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshigami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshigami|qq=}}, {{ill|Yakusanoikazuchi|ja|火雷大神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakusanoikazuchi|qq=}}, {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamayori-hime|qq=}}, {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Jimmu|qq=}} == History == Originally, Mukō Shrine consisted of two separate shrines on Mukō Mountain: {{ill|Muka Shrine|ja|向神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muka Shrine|qq=}} (U11er Shrine) and {{ill|Honoikazuchi Shrine|ja|火雷神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honoikazuchi Shrine|qq=}} (Lower Shrine). Both are ancient shrines mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, with Honoikazuchi Shrine being one of the candidates for the {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|qq=}} "Otokuni-zazuchi Shrine" (other candidates include {{ill|Sumiyagashira Shrine|ja|角宮神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyagashira Shrine|qq=}} in {{ill|Nagaokakyō|ja|長岡京市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagaokakyō|qq=}}). Muka Shrine is believed to have originated when Toshigami (Mukō Kami) stayed on Mukō Mountain and promoted rice cultivation. Initially, the shrine was located atop the {{ill|Itsukahara Kofun|lt=Itsukahara Kofun|lt=Gotsukahara Kofun|ja|五塚原古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gotsukahara Kofun|qq=}}, 500 meters north of its current location. In 718 (2nd year of {{ill|Yōrō|ja|養老|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō|qq=}}), the shrine was relocated to its current position south of the {{ill|Motoinari Kofun|lt=Motoinari Kofun|lt=Moto-Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Inari Kofun|qq=}}. Honoikazuchi Shrine is said to have been established when Emperor Jimmu enshrined the deity Honoikazuchi during his journey from {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国|qq=Yamato Province|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Province|qq=}} to {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|山背国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashiro Province|qq=}}. It is also believed to have been founded around 518 (12th year of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|継体天皇|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Keitai|qq=}}), when Keitai built the {{ill|Otokuni Palace|ja|乙訓宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otokuni Palace|qq=}}. In 718, Honoikazuchi Shrine incorporated Tamayorihime and Emperor Jimmu as deities. In 784 (3rd year of {{ill|Enryaku|ja|延暦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enryaku|qq=}}), the establishment of {{ill|Nagaoka-kyō|ja|長岡京|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagaoka-kyō|qq=}} brought the imperial palace, Nagaoka-no-miya, to the eastern foot of Muka Shrine. The chief priest of Honoikazuchi Shrine joined Emperor Go-Toba's forces during the {{ill|Jōkyū Rebellion|ja|承久の乱|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōkyū Rebellion|qq=}} in 1221 but later fled to the {{ill|Rokuto-no-shō|ja|六人部荘|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokuto-no-shō|qq=}} estate in present-day {{ill|Fukuchiyama|ja|福知山市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukuchiyama|qq=}}. After returning, the shrine's influence waned, and in 1275 (1st year of {{ill|Kenji|ja|建治|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenji|qq=}}), Muka Shrine merged with Honoikazuchi Shrine, forming Mukō Shrine and becoming the area's guardian shrine. The current main hall was rebuilt in 1422 (29th year of {{ill|Ōei|ja|応永|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōei|qq=}}) with the cooperation of seven surrounding villages. During the Muromachi period, Mukō Shrine became a rallying point for local {{ill|Tsuchi Ikki|ja|土一揆|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchi Ikki|qq=}} peasant uprisings before they marched on Kyoto. In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine. In 1961 (36th year of {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa|qq=}}), the surrounding hill was partially excavated for the construction of the {{ill|Meishin Expressway|ja|名神高速道路|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meishin Expressway|qq=}}. Today, the shrine and Moto-Inari Kofun remain as a small hill. In recent years, the shrine has been surrounded by a cherry blossom park, partially restoring the area’s former beauty. The main hall of Tokyo's {{ill|Meiji Jingu|lt=Meiji Jingu|lt=Meiji Shrine|ja|明治神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Shrine|qq=}} is modeled after Mukō Shrine's main hall, scaled up by 1.5 times. == Grounds == [[File:Mukō-jinja-Cherry Blossoms of a11roach to a shrine.jpg|thumb|Path lined with cherry blossoms leading to the shrine]] From {{ill|Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|ja|京都府道・大阪府道67号西京高槻線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|qq=}} (Astro Street), visitors enter a long, stone-paved path lined with cherry, azalea, and maple trees. The incline is scenic but shared with vehicles, requiring caution. * Main Hall (Important Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1422 during the Muromachi period. Representative of nagare-zukuri architecture. The model for the Meiji Shrine. ''' Main Hall Shelter (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842 (13th year of {{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpō|qq=}}). * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842. * North and South Gates (Registered Tangible Cultural Properties) – Built in 1842. * Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1842. * Kagura Hall – Rebuilt in 2003 after the original structure (built in 1625) was lost in a 2002 fire. * Guest Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Relocated from a youth training facility in Osaka around 1965. * Purification Pavilion (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Late 18th-century reconstruction. === Auxiliary Shrines === * Ancestral Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1688 (1st year of {{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genroku|qq=}}). * Five Shrines (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Dedicated to various deities. * Other shrines include {{ill|Goryo Shrine|lt=Goryo Shrine|lt=Goryō Shrine|ja|御霊神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goryō Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Tenmangū|ja|天満宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmangū|qq=}}, and Katsuyama Inari Shrine. == Festivals == * festival – Second Sunday in May * Sōmen Festival == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall and five ridge plaques. * Paper manuscript of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Shoki|qq=}} (Lower Chapter, Age of the Gods) – A 14th-century transcription. === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Multiple structures, including the Main Hall Shelter, North and South Gates, and various auxiliary shrines. == Access == * Nearest station: [[Hankyu Kyoto Line]] [[Nishimukō Station]], 500 meters northwest. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Mutsume Koreka|ja|六人部是香|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mutsume Koreka|qq=}} – Late Edo-period scholar and former shrine priest. == External Links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20190101000000/http://www.geocities.jp/mukojinjahp/index.htm Mukō Shrine Official Website (archived)] * {{Commonscat-inline}} {{shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|向日神社|version=99695516|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Tangible Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Historic sites in Mukō]] [[Category:Cherry blossom spots in Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] }} [[sn:Muko Shrine]] [[ja:向日神社]] [[en:Muko Shrine]]

revision.comment: Bot: fix redirect [[Honoikazuchi Shrine]] → [[Suminomiya Shrine]]
revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Mukō Shrine | location = Mukōchō Kitayama 65, [[Mukō]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]], Japan | Latitude degrees = 34 | Latitude minutes = 56 | Latitude seconds = 38.8 | N/S = N | Longitude degrees = 135 | Longitude minutes = 41 | Longitude seconds = 49.6 | E/W = E | Map country code = JP | Map = Japan Kyoto | Main deity = {{ill|Toshigami|ja|年神|1=Toshigami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshigami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshigami|qq=}}, {{ill|Honoikazuchi no Ōkami|ja|火雷大神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honoikazuchi no Ōkami|qq=}}, {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamayori-hime|qq=}}, {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Jimmu|qq=}} | Shrine Rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|qq=}} and small shrine), former {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|府社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|qq=}} | Main hall = Three-bay nagare-zukuri (Important Cultural Property) | festival = Second Sunday in May | Religious rituals = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Muko-jinja, shaden.jpg|280px]]
Dance Hall (right) and Worship Hall (left background) }} {{osm box|w|48434848}} [[File:Muko-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Mukō Shrine''' (向日神社, Mukō Jinja), also known as '''Mukō Myōjin''', is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto shrine|qq=}} located in Mukōchō Kitayama, [[Mukō]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]]. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}} (Myōjin Taisha and small shrine) and a former prefectural shrine. == Deities == * Main deities: {{ill|Toshigami|ja|年神|1=Toshigami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshigami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshigami|qq=}}, {{ill|Yakusanoikazuchi|ja|火雷大神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakusanoikazuchi|qq=}}, {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamayori-hime|qq=}}, {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Jimmu|qq=}} == History == Originally, Mukō Shrine consisted of two separate shrines on Mukō Mountain: {{ill|Muka Shrine|ja|向神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muka Shrine|qq=}} (U11er Shrine) and {{ill|Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Honoikazuchi Shrine|ja|火雷神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honoikazuchi Shrine|qq=}} (Lower Shrine). Both are ancient shrines mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, with Honoikazuchi Shrine being one of the candidates for the {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|qq=}} "Otokuni-zazuchi Shrine" (other candidates include {{ill|Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Sumiyagashira Shrine|ja|角宮神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyagashira Shrine|qq=}} in {{ill|Nagaokakyō|ja|長岡京市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagaokakyō|qq=}}). Muka Shrine is believed to have originated when Toshigami (Mukō Kami) stayed on Mukō Mountain and promoted rice cultivation. Initially, the shrine was located atop the {{ill|Itsukahara Kofun|lt=Itsukahara Kofun|lt=Gotsukahara Kofun|ja|五塚原古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gotsukahara Kofun|qq=}}, 500 meters north of its current location. In 718 (2nd year of {{ill|Yōrō|ja|養老|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō|qq=}}), the shrine was relocated to its current position south of the {{ill|Motoinari Kofun|lt=Motoinari Kofun|lt=Moto-Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Inari Kofun|qq=}}. Honoikazuchi Shrine is said to have been established when Emperor Jimmu enshrined the deity Honoikazuchi during his journey from {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国|qq=Yamato Province|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Province|qq=}} to {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|山背国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashiro Province|qq=}}. It is also believed to have been founded around 518 (12th year of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|継体天皇|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Keitai|qq=}}), when Keitai built the {{ill|Otokuni Palace|ja|乙訓宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otokuni Palace|qq=}}. In 718, Honoikazuchi Shrine incorporated Tamayorihime and Emperor Jimmu as deities. In 784 (3rd year of {{ill|Enryaku|ja|延暦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enryaku|qq=}}), the establishment of {{ill|Nagaoka-kyō|ja|長岡京|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagaoka-kyō|qq=}} brought the imperial palace, Nagaoka-no-miya, to the eastern foot of Muka Shrine. The chief priest of Honoikazuchi Shrine joined Emperor Go-Toba's forces during the {{ill|Jōkyū Rebellion|ja|承久の乱|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōkyū Rebellion|qq=}} in 1221 but later fled to the {{ill|Rokuto-no-shō|ja|六人部荘|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokuto-no-shō|qq=}} estate in present-day {{ill|Fukuchiyama|ja|福知山市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukuchiyama|qq=}}. After returning, the shrine's influence waned, and in 1275 (1st year of {{ill|Kenji|ja|建治|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenji|qq=}}), Muka Shrine merged with Honoikazuchi Shrine, forming Mukō Shrine and becoming the area's guardian shrine. The current main hall was rebuilt in 1422 (29th year of {{ill|Ōei|ja|応永|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōei|qq=}}) with the cooperation of seven surrounding villages. During the Muromachi period, Mukō Shrine became a rallying point for local {{ill|Tsuchi Ikki|ja|土一揆|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchi Ikki|qq=}} peasant uprisings before they marched on Kyoto. In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine. In 1961 (36th year of {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa|qq=}}), the surrounding hill was partially excavated for the construction of the {{ill|Meishin Expressway|ja|名神高速道路|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meishin Expressway|qq=}}. Today, the shrine and Moto-Inari Kofun remain as a small hill. In recent years, the shrine has been surrounded by a cherry blossom park, partially restoring the area’s former beauty. The main hall of Tokyo's {{ill|Meiji Jingu|lt=Meiji Jingu|lt=Meiji Shrine|ja|明治神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Shrine|qq=}} is modeled after Mukō Shrine's main hall, scaled up by 1.5 times. == Grounds == [[File:Mukō-jinja-Cherry Blossoms of a11roach to a shrine.jpg|thumb|Path lined with cherry blossoms leading to the shrine]] From {{ill|Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|ja|京都府道・大阪府道67号西京高槻線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|qq=}} (Astro Street), visitors enter a long, stone-paved path lined with cherry, azalea, and maple trees. The incline is scenic but shared with vehicles, requiring caution. * Main Hall (Important Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1422 during the Muromachi period. Representative of nagare-zukuri architecture. The model for the Meiji Shrine. ''' Main Hall Shelter (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842 (13th year of {{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpō|qq=}}). * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842. * North and South Gates (Registered Tangible Cultural Properties) – Built in 1842. * Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1842. * Kagura Hall – Rebuilt in 2003 after the original structure (built in 1625) was lost in a 2002 fire. * Guest Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Relocated from a youth training facility in Osaka around 1965. * Purification Pavilion (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Late 18th-century reconstruction. === Auxiliary Shrines === * Ancestral Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1688 (1st year of {{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genroku|qq=}}). * Five Shrines (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Dedicated to various deities. * Other shrines include {{ill|Goryo Shrine|lt=Goryo Shrine|lt=Goryō Shrine|ja|御霊神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goryō Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Tenmangū|ja|天満宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmangū|qq=}}, and Katsuyama Inari Shrine. == Festivals == * festival – Second Sunday in May * Sōmen Festival == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall and five ridge plaques. * Paper manuscript of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Shoki|qq=}} (Lower Chapter, Age of the Gods) – A 14th-century transcription. === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Multiple structures, including the Main Hall Shelter, North and South Gates, and various auxiliary shrines. == Access == * Nearest station: [[Hankyu Kyoto Line]] [[Nishimukō Station]], 500 meters northwest. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Mutsume Koreka|ja|六人部是香|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mutsume Koreka|qq=}} – Late Edo-period scholar and former shrine priest. == External Links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20190101000000/http://www.geocities.jp/mukojinjahp/index.htm Mukō Shrine Official Website (archived)] * {{Commonscat-inline}} {{shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|向日神社|version=99695516|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Tangible Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Historic sites in Mukō]] [[Category:Cherry blossom spots in Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] }} [[sn:Muko Shrine]] [[ja:向日神社]] [[en:Muko Shrine]]
