{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Mukō Shrine | location = Mukōchō Kitayama 65, [[Mukō]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]], Japan | Latitude degrees = 34 | Latitude minutes = 56 | Latitude seconds = 38.8 | N/S = N | Longitude degrees = 135 | Longitude minutes = 41 | Longitude seconds = 49.6 | E/W = E | Map country code = JP | Map = Japan Kyoto | Main deity = [[:en:Toshigami|Toshigami]], [[:en:Yakusanoikazuchi|Honoikazuchi no Ōkami]], {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamayori-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タマヨリビメ}}, [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] | Shrine Rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} and small shrine), former {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|府社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}} | Main hall = Three-bay nagare-zukuri (Important Cultural Property) | festival = Second Sunday in May | Religious rituals = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Muko-jinja, shaden.jpg|280px]]
Dance Hall (right) and Worship Hall (left background) }} {{osm box|w|48434848}} [[File:Muko-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Mukō Shrine''' (向日神社, Mukō Jinja), also known as '''Mukō Myōjin''', is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in Mukōchō Kitayama, [[Mukō]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]]. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}} (Myōjin Taisha and small shrine) and a former prefectural shrine. == Deities == * Main deities: [[:en:Toshigami|Toshigami]], [[:en:Yakusanoikazuchi|Yakusanoikazuchi]], {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamayori-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タマヨリビメ}}, [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] == History == Originally, Mukō Shrine consisted of two separate shrines on Mukō Mountain: {{ill|Muka Shrine|ja|向神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muka Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (U11er Shrine) and {{ill|Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Honoikazuchi Shrine|ja|火雷神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honoikazuchi Shrine|qq=}} (Lower Shrine). Both are ancient shrines mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, with Honoikazuchi Shrine being one of the candidates for the {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} "Otokuni-zazuchi Shrine" (other candidates include {{ill|Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Sumiyagashira Shrine|ja|角宮神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyagashira Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Nagaokakyō, Kyoto|Nagaokakyō]]). Muka Shrine is believed to have originated when Toshigami (Mukō Kami) stayed on Mukō Mountain and promoted rice cultivation. Initially, the shrine was located atop the {{ill|Itsukahara Kofun|lt=Itsukahara Kofun|lt=Gotsukahara Kofun|ja|五塚原古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gotsukahara Kofun|qq=}}, 500 meters north of its current location. In 718 (2nd year of [[:en:Yōrō|Yōrō]]), the shrine was relocated to its current position south of the {{ill|Motoinari Kofun|lt=Motoinari Kofun|lt=Moto-Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Inari Kofun|qq=}}. Honoikazuchi Shrine is said to have been established when Emperor Jimmu enshrined the deity Honoikazuchi during his journey from [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] to {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|山背国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashiro Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 山城国}}. It is also believed to have been founded around 518 (12th year of [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]]), when Keitai built the {{ill|Otokuni Palace|ja|乙訓宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otokuni Palace|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In 718, Honoikazuchi Shrine incorporated Tamayorihime and Emperor Jimmu as deities. In 784 (3rd year of [[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]]), the establishment of [[:en:Nagaoka-kyō|Nagaoka-kyō]] brought the imperial palace, Nagaoka-no-miya, to the eastern foot of Muka Shrine. The chief priest of Honoikazuchi Shrine joined Emperor Go-Toba's forces during the [[:en:Jōkyū War|Jōkyū Rebellion]] in 1221 but later fled to the {{ill|Rokuto-no-shō|ja|六人部荘|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokuto-no-shō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} estate in present-day [[:en:Fukuchiyama, Kyoto|Fukuchiyama]]. After returning, the shrine's influence waned, and in 1275 (1st year of [[:en:Kenji (era)|Kenji]]), Muka Shrine merged with Honoikazuchi Shrine, forming Mukō Shrine and becoming the area's guardian shrine. The current main hall was rebuilt in 1422 (29th year of [[:en:Ōei|Ōei]]) with the cooperation of seven surrounding villages. During the Muromachi period, Mukō Shrine became a rallying point for local {{ill|Tsuchi Ikki|ja|土一揆|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchi Ikki|qq=}} peasant uprisings before they marched on Kyoto. In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine. In 1961 (36th year of [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]]), the surrounding hill was partially excavated for the construction of the [[:en:Meishin Expressway|Meishin Expressway]]. Today, the shrine and Moto-Inari Kofun remain as a small hill. In recent years, the shrine has been surrounded by a cherry blossom park, partially restoring the area’s former beauty. The main hall of Tokyo's [[:en:Meiji Shrine|Meiji Shrine]] is modeled after Mukō Shrine's main hall, scaled up by 1.5 times. == Grounds == [[File:Mukō-jinja-Cherry Blossoms of a11roach to a shrine.jpg|thumb|Path lined with cherry blossoms leading to the shrine]] From {{ill|Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|ja|京都府道・大阪府道67号西京高槻線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|qq=}} (Astro Street), visitors enter a long, stone-paved path lined with cherry, azalea, and maple trees. The incline is scenic but shared with vehicles, requiring caution. * Main Hall (Important Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1422 during the Muromachi period. Representative of nagare-zukuri architecture. The model for the Meiji Shrine. ''' Main Hall Shelter (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842 (13th year of [[:en:Tenpō|Tenpō]]). * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842. * North and South Gates (Registered Tangible Cultural Properties) – Built in 1842. * Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1842. * Kagura Hall – Rebuilt in 2003 after the original structure (built in 1625) was lost in a 2002 fire. * Guest Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Relocated from a youth training facility in Osaka around 1965. * Purification Pavilion (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Late 18th-century reconstruction. === Auxiliary Shrines === * Ancestral Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1688 (1st year of [[:en:Genroku|Genroku]]). * Five Shrines (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Dedicated to various deities. * Other shrines include {{ill|Goryo Shrine|lt=Goryo Shrine|lt=Goryō Shrine|ja|御霊神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goryō Shrine|qq=}}, [[:en:Tenmangū|Tenmangū]], and Katsuyama Inari Shrine. == Festivals == * festival – Second Sunday in May * Sōmen Festival == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall and five ridge plaques. * Paper manuscript of the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] (Lower Chapter, Age of the Gods) – A 14th-century transcription. === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Multiple structures, including the Main Hall Shelter, North and South Gates, and various auxiliary shrines. == Access == * Nearest station: [[Hankyu Kyoto Line]] [[Nishimukō Station]], 500 meters northwest. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Mutsume Koreka|ja|六人部是香|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mutsume Koreka|qq=}} – Late Edo-period scholar and former shrine priest. == External Links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20190101000000/http://www.geocities.jp/mukojinjahp/index.htm Mukō Shrine Official Website (archived)] * {{Commonscat-inline}} {{shinto-stub}} {{translated revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Mukō Shrine | location = Mukōchō Kitayama 65, [[Mukō]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]], Japan | Latitude degrees = 34 | Latitude minutes = 56 | Latitude seconds = 38.8 | N/S = N | Longitude degrees = 135 | Longitude minutes = 41 | Longitude seconds = 49.6 | E/W = E | Map country code = JP | Map = Japan Kyoto | Main deity = [[:en:Toshigami|Toshigami]], [[:en:Yakusanoikazuchi|Honoikazuchi no Ōkami]], {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamayori-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タマヨリビメ}}, [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] | Shrine Rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} and small shrine), former {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|府社|lt=Fu-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#Metropolitan_and_Prefectural_Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}} | Main hall = Three-bay nagare-zukuri (Important Cultural Property) | festival = Second Sunday in May | Religious rituals = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Muko-jinja, shaden.jpg|280px]]
Dance Hall (right) and Worship Hall (left background) }} {{osm box|w|48434848}} [[File:Muko-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Mukō Shrine''' (向日神社, Mukō Jinja), also known as '''Mukō Myōjin''', is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in Mukōchō Kitayama, [[Mukō]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]]. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}} (Myōjin Taisha and small shrine) and a former prefectural shrine. == Deities == * Main deities: [[:en:Toshigami|Toshigami]], [[:en:Yakusanoikazuchi|Yakusanoikazuchi]], {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamayori-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タマヨリビメ}}, [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] == History == Originally, Mukō Shrine consisted of two separate shrines on Mukō Mountain: {{ill|Muka Shrine|ja|向神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muka Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (U11er Shrine) and {{ill|Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Honoikazuchi Shrine|ja|火雷神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honoikazuchi Shrine|qq=}} (Lower Shrine). Both are ancient shrines mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, with Honoikazuchi Shrine being one of the candidates for the {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} "Otokuni-zazuchi Shrine" (other candidates include {{ill|Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Sumiyagashira Shrine|ja|角宮神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyagashira Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Nagaokakyō, Kyoto|Nagaokakyō]]). Muka Shrine is believed to have originated when Toshigami (Mukō Kami) stayed on Mukō Mountain and promoted rice cultivation. Initially, the shrine was located atop the {{ill|Itsukahara Kofun|lt=Itsukahara Kofun|lt=Gotsukahara Kofun|ja|五塚原古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gotsukahara Kofun|qq=}}, 500 meters north of its current location. In 718 (2nd year of [[:en:Yōrō|Yōrō]]), the shrine was relocated to its current position south of the {{ill|Motoinari Kofun|lt=Motoinari Kofun|lt=Moto-Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Inari Kofun|qq=}}. Honoikazuchi Shrine is said to have been established when Emperor Jimmu enshrined the deity Honoikazuchi during his journey from [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] to {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|山背国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashiro Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 山城国}}. It is also believed to have been founded around 518 (12th year of [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]]), when Keitai built the {{ill|Otokuni Palace|ja|乙訓宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otokuni Palace|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In 718, Honoikazuchi Shrine incorporated Tamayorihime and Emperor Jimmu as deities. In 784 (3rd year of [[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]]), the establishment of [[:en:Nagaoka-kyō|Nagaoka-kyō]] brought the imperial palace, Nagaoka-no-miya, to the eastern foot of Muka Shrine. The chief priest of Honoikazuchi Shrine joined Emperor Go-Toba's forces during the [[:en:Jōkyū War|Jōkyū Rebellion]] in 1221 but later fled to the {{ill|Rokuto-no-shō|ja|六人部荘|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokuto-no-shō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} estate in present-day [[:en:Fukuchiyama, Kyoto|Fukuchiyama]]. After returning, the shrine's influence waned, and in 1275 (1st year of [[:en:Kenji (era)|Kenji]]), Muka Shrine merged with Honoikazuchi Shrine, forming Mukō Shrine and becoming the area's guardian shrine. The current main hall was rebuilt in 1422 (29th year of [[:en:Ōei|Ōei]]) with the cooperation of seven surrounding villages. During the Muromachi period, Mukō Shrine became a rallying point for local {{ill|Tsuchi Ikki|ja|土一揆|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchi Ikki|qq=}} peasant uprisings before they marched on Kyoto. In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine. In 1961 (36th year of [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]]), the surrounding hill was partially excavated for the construction of the [[:en:Meishin Expressway|Meishin Expressway]]. Today, the shrine and Moto-Inari Kofun remain as a small hill. In recent years, the shrine has been surrounded by a cherry blossom park, partially restoring the area’s former beauty. The main hall of Tokyo's [[:en:Meiji Shrine|Meiji Shrine]] is modeled after Mukō Shrine's main hall, scaled up by 1.5 times. == Grounds == [[File:Mukō-jinja-Cherry Blossoms of a11roach to a shrine.jpg|thumb|Path lined with cherry blossoms leading to the shrine]] From {{ill|Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|ja|京都府道・大阪府道67号西京高槻線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|qq=}} (Astro Street), visitors enter a long, stone-paved path lined with cherry, azalea, and maple trees. The incline is scenic but shared with vehicles, requiring caution. * Main Hall (Important Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1422 during the Muromachi period. Representative of nagare-zukuri architecture. The model for the Meiji Shrine. ''' Main Hall Shelter (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842 (13th year of [[:en:Tenpō|Tenpō]]). * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842. * North and South Gates (Registered Tangible Cultural Properties) – Built in 1842. * Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1842. * Kagura Hall – Rebuilt in 2003 after the original structure (built in 1625) was lost in a 2002 fire. * Guest Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Relocated from a youth training facility in Osaka around 1965. * Purification Pavilion (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Late 18th-century reconstruction. === Auxiliary Shrines === * Ancestral Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1688 (1st year of [[:en:Genroku|Genroku]]). * Five Shrines (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Dedicated to various deities. * Other shrines include {{ill|Goryo Shrine|lt=Goryo Shrine|lt=Goryō Shrine|ja|御霊神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goryō Shrine|qq=}}, [[:en:Tenmangū|Tenmangū]], and Katsuyama Inari Shrine. == Festivals == * festival – Second Sunday in May * Sōmen Festival == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall and five ridge plaques. * Paper manuscript of the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] (Lower Chapter, Age of the Gods) – A 14th-century transcription. === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Multiple structures, including the Main Hall Shelter, North and South Gates, and various auxiliary shrines. == Access == * Nearest station: [[:en:Hankyu Kyoto Line|Hankyu Kyoto Line]] [[Nishimukō Station]], 500 meters northwest. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Mutsume Koreka|ja|六人部是香|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mutsume Koreka|qq=}} – Late Edo-period scholar and former shrine priest. == External Links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20190101000000/http://www.geocities.jp/mukojinjahp/index.htm Mukō Shrine Official Website (archived)] * {{Commonscat-inline}} {{shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|向日神社|version=99695516|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] }} [[sn:Muko Shrine]] [[ja:向日神社]] [[Category:Registered Tangible Cultural Property of Japan in Kyoto prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Kyoto prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings in Muko, Kyoto]] [[Category:History of Muko, Kyoto]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Yamashiro Province]] [[Category:Hanami spots of Japan]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] {{Commons category|Mukō-jinja}}

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Mukō Shrine | location = Mukōchō Kitayama 65, [[:en:Mukō|Mukō]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]], Japan | Latitude degrees = 34 | Latitude minutes = 56 | Latitude seconds = 38.8 | N/S = N | Longitude degrees = 135 | Longitude minutes = 41 | Longitude seconds = 49.6 | E/W = E | Map country code = JP | Map = Japan Kyoto | Main deity = [[:en:Toshigami|Toshigami]], [[:en:Yakusanoikazuchi|Honoikazuchi no Ōkami]], {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamayori-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タマヨリビメ}}, [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] | Shrine Rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} and small shrine), former {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|府社|lt=Fu-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#Metropolitan_and_Prefectural_Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}} | Main hall = Three-bay nagare-zukuri (Important Cultural Property) | festival = Second Sunday in May | Religious rituals = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Muko-jinja, shaden.jpg|280px]]
Dance Hall (right) and Worship Hall (left background) }} {{osm box|w|48434848}} [[File:Muko-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Mukō Shrine''' (向日神社, Mukō Jinja), also known as '''Mukō Myōjin''', is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in Mukōchō Kitayama, [[:en:Mukō|Mukō]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]]. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}} (Myōjin Taisha and small shrine) and a former prefectural shrine. == Deities == * Main deities: [[:en:Toshigami|Toshigami]], [[:en:Yakusanoikazuchi|Yakusanoikazuchi]], {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamayori-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タマヨリビメ}}, [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] == History == Originally, Mukō Shrine consisted of two separate shrines on Mukō Mountain: {{ill|Muka Shrine|ja|向神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muka Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (U11er Shrine) and {{ill|Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Honoikazuchi Shrine|ja|火雷神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honoikazuchi Shrine|qq=}} (Lower Shrine). Both are ancient shrines mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, with Honoikazuchi Shrine being one of the candidates for the {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} "Otokuni-zazuchi Shrine" (other candidates include {{ill|Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Sumiyagashira Shrine|ja|角宮神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyagashira Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Nagaokakyō, Kyoto|Nagaokakyō]]). Muka Shrine is believed to have originated when Toshigami (Mukō Kami) stayed on Mukō Mountain and promoted rice cultivation. Initially, the shrine was located atop the {{ill|Itsukahara Kofun|lt=Itsukahara Kofun|lt=Gotsukahara Kofun|ja|五塚原古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gotsukahara Kofun|qq=}}, 500 meters north of its current location. In 718 (2nd year of [[:en:Yōrō|Yōrō]]), the shrine was relocated to its current position south of the {{ill|Motoinari Kofun|lt=Motoinari Kofun|lt=Moto-Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Inari Kofun|qq=}}. Honoikazuchi Shrine is said to have been established when Emperor Jimmu enshrined the deity Honoikazuchi during his journey from [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] to {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|山背国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashiro Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 山城国}}. It is also believed to have been founded around 518 (12th year of [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]]), when Keitai built the {{ill|Otokuni Palace|ja|乙訓宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otokuni Palace|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In 718, Honoikazuchi Shrine incorporated Tamayorihime and Emperor Jimmu as deities. In 784 (3rd year of [[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]]), the establishment of [[:en:Nagaoka-kyō|Nagaoka-kyō]] brought the imperial palace, Nagaoka-no-miya, to the eastern foot of Muka Shrine. The chief priest of Honoikazuchi Shrine joined Emperor Go-Toba's forces during the [[:en:Jōkyū War|Jōkyū Rebellion]] in 1221 but later fled to the {{ill|Rokuto-no-shō|ja|六人部荘|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokuto-no-shō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} estate in present-day [[:en:Fukuchiyama, Kyoto|Fukuchiyama]]. After returning, the shrine's influence waned, and in 1275 (1st year of [[:en:Kenji (era)|Kenji]]), Muka Shrine merged with Honoikazuchi Shrine, forming Mukō Shrine and becoming the area's guardian shrine. The current main hall was rebuilt in 1422 (29th year of [[:en:Ōei|Ōei]]) with the cooperation of seven surrounding villages. During the Muromachi period, Mukō Shrine became a rallying point for local {{ill|Tsuchi Ikki|ja|土一揆|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchi Ikki|qq=}} peasant uprisings before they marched on Kyoto. In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine. In 1961 (36th year of [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]]), the surrounding hill was partially excavated for the construction of the [[:en:Meishin Expressway|Meishin Expressway]]. Today, the shrine and Moto-Inari Kofun remain as a small hill. In recent years, the shrine has been surrounded by a cherry blossom park, partially restoring the area’s former beauty. The main hall of Tokyo's [[:en:Meiji Shrine|Meiji Shrine]] is modeled after Mukō Shrine's main hall, scaled up by 1.5 times. == Grounds == [[File:Mukō-jinja-Cherry Blossoms of a11roach to a shrine.jpg|thumb|Path lined with cherry blossoms leading to the shrine]] From {{ill|Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|ja|京都府道・大阪府道67号西京高槻線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|qq=}} (Astro Street), visitors enter a long, stone-paved path lined with cherry, azalea, and maple trees. The incline is scenic but shared with vehicles, requiring caution. * Main Hall (Important Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1422 during the Muromachi period. Representative of nagare-zukuri architecture. The model for the Meiji Shrine. ''' Main Hall Shelter (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842 (13th year of [[:en:Tenpō|Tenpō]]). * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842. * North and South Gates (Registered Tangible Cultural Properties) – Built in 1842. * Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1842. * Kagura Hall – Rebuilt in 2003 after the original structure (built in 1625) was lost in a 2002 fire. * Guest Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Relocated from a youth training facility in Osaka around 1965. * Purification Pavilion (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Late 18th-century reconstruction. === Auxiliary Shrines === * Ancestral Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1688 (1st year of [[:en:Genroku|Genroku]]). * Five Shrines (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Dedicated to various deities. * Other shrines include {{ill|Goryo Shrine|lt=Goryo Shrine|lt=Goryō Shrine|ja|御霊神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goryō Shrine|qq=}}, [[:en:Tenmangū|Tenmangū]], and Katsuyama Inari Shrine. == Festivals == * festival – Second Sunday in May * Sōmen Festival == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall and five ridge plaques. * Paper manuscript of the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] (Lower Chapter, Age of the Gods) – A 14th-century transcription. === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Multiple structures, including the Main Hall Shelter, North and South Gates, and various auxiliary shrines. == Access == * Nearest station: [[:en:Hankyu Kyoto Line|Hankyu Kyoto Line]] [[Nishimukō Station]], 500 meters northwest. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Mutsume Koreka|ja|六人部是香|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mutsume Koreka|qq=}} – Late Edo-period scholar and former shrine priest. == External Links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20190101000000/http://www.geocities.jp/mukojinjahp/index.htm Mukō Shrine Official Website (archived)] * {{Commonscat-inline}} {{shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|向日神社|version=99695516|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] }} [[sn:Muko Shrine]] [[ja:向日神社]] [[Category:Registered Tangible Cultural Property of Japan in Kyoto prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Kyoto prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings in Muko, Kyoto]] [[Category:History of Muko, Kyoto]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Yamashiro Province]] [[Category:Hanami spots of Japan]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] {{Commons category|Mukō-jinja}}

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11415986]] Wikidata name: Mukō-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:20, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Mukō Shrine | location = Mukōchō Kitayama 65, [[:en:Mukō|Mukō]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]], Japan | Latitude degrees = 34 | Latitude minutes = 56 | Latitude seconds = 38.8 | N/S = N | Longitude degrees = 135 | Longitude minutes = 41 | Longitude seconds = 49.6 | E/W = E | Map country code = JP | Map = Japan Kyoto | Main deity = [[:en:Toshigami|Toshigami]], [[:en:Yakusanoikazuchi|Honoikazuchi no Ōkami]], {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamayori-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タマヨリビメ}}, [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] | Shrine Rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} and small shrine), former {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|府社|lt=Fu-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#Metropolitan_and_Prefectural_Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}} | Main hall = Three-bay nagare-zukuri (Important Cultural Property) | festival = Second Sunday in May | Religious rituals = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Muko-jinja, shaden.jpg|280px]]
Dance Hall (right) and Worship Hall (left background) }} {{osm box|w|48434848}} [[File:Muko-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Mukō Shrine''' (向日神社, Mukō Jinja), also known as '''Mukō Myōjin''', is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in Mukōchō Kitayama, [[:en:Mukō|Mukō]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]]. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} (Myōjin Taisha and small shrine) and a former prefectural shrine. == Deities == * Main deities: [[:en:Toshigami|Toshigami]], [[:en:Yakusanoikazuchi|Yakusanoikazuchi]], {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamayori-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タマヨリビメ}}, [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] == History == Originally, Mukō Shrine consisted of two separate shrines on Mukō Mountain: {{ill|Muka Shrine|ja|向神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muka Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (U11er Shrine) and {{ill|Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Honoikazuchi Shrine|ja|火雷神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honoikazuchi Shrine|qq=}} (Lower Shrine). Both are ancient shrines mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, with Honoikazuchi Shrine being one of the candidates for the {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} "Otokuni-zazuchi Shrine" (other candidates include {{ill|Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Sumiyagashira Shrine|ja|角宮神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyagashira Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Nagaokakyō, Kyoto|Nagaokakyō]]). Muka Shrine is believed to have originated when Toshigami (Mukō Kami) stayed on Mukō Mountain and promoted rice cultivation. Initially, the shrine was located atop the {{ill|Itsukahara Kofun|lt=Itsukahara Kofun|lt=Gotsukahara Kofun|ja|五塚原古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gotsukahara Kofun|qq=}}, 500 meters north of its current location. In 718 (2nd year of [[:en:Yōrō|Yōrō]]), the shrine was relocated to its current position south of the {{ill|Motoinari Kofun|lt=Motoinari Kofun|lt=Moto-Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Inari Kofun|qq=}}. Honoikazuchi Shrine is said to have been established when Emperor Jimmu enshrined the deity Honoikazuchi during his journey from [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] to {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|山背国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashiro Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 山城国}}. It is also believed to have been founded around 518 (12th year of [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]]), when Keitai built the {{ill|Otokuni Palace|ja|乙訓宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otokuni Palace|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In 718, Honoikazuchi Shrine incorporated Tamayorihime and Emperor Jimmu as deities. In 784 (3rd year of [[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]]), the establishment of [[:en:Nagaoka-kyō|Nagaoka-kyō]] brought the imperial palace, Nagaoka-no-miya, to the eastern foot of Muka Shrine. The chief priest of Honoikazuchi Shrine joined Emperor Go-Toba's forces during the [[:en:Jōkyū War|Jōkyū Rebellion]] in 1221 but later fled to the {{ill|Rokuto-no-shō|ja|六人部荘|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokuto-no-shō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} estate in present-day [[:en:Fukuchiyama, Kyoto|Fukuchiyama]]. After returning, the shrine's influence waned, and in 1275 (1st year of [[:en:Kenji (era)|Kenji]]), Muka Shrine merged with Honoikazuchi Shrine, forming Mukō Shrine and becoming the area's guardian shrine. The current main hall was rebuilt in 1422 (29th year of [[:en:Ōei|Ōei]]) with the cooperation of seven surrounding villages. During the Muromachi period, Mukō Shrine became a rallying point for local {{ill|Tsuchi Ikki|ja|土一揆|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchi Ikki|qq=}} peasant uprisings before they marched on Kyoto. In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine. In 1961 (36th year of [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]]), the surrounding hill was partially excavated for the construction of the [[:en:Meishin Expressway|Meishin Expressway]]. Today, the shrine and Moto-Inari Kofun remain as a small hill. In recent years, the shrine has been surrounded by a cherry blossom park, partially restoring the area’s former beauty. The main hall of Tokyo's [[:en:Meiji Shrine|Meiji Shrine]] is modeled after Mukō Shrine's main hall, scaled up by 1.5 times. == Grounds == [[File:Mukō-jinja-Cherry Blossoms of a11roach to a shrine.jpg|thumb|Path lined with cherry blossoms leading to the shrine]] From {{ill|Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|ja|京都府道・大阪府道67号西京高槻線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|qq=}} (Astro Street), visitors enter a long, stone-paved path lined with cherry, azalea, and maple trees. The incline is scenic but shared with vehicles, requiring caution. * Main Hall (Important Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1422 during the Muromachi period. Representative of nagare-zukuri architecture. The model for the Meiji Shrine. ''' Main Hall Shelter (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842 (13th year of [[:en:Tenpō|Tenpō]]). * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842. * North and South Gates (Registered Tangible Cultural Properties) – Built in 1842. * Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1842. * Kagura Hall – Rebuilt in 2003 after the original structure (built in 1625) was lost in a 2002 fire. * Guest Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Relocated from a youth training facility in Osaka around 1965. * Purification Pavilion (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Late 18th-century reconstruction. === Auxiliary Shrines === * Ancestral Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1688 (1st year of [[:en:Genroku|Genroku]]). * Five Shrines (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Dedicated to various deities. * Other shrines include {{ill|Goryo Shrine|lt=Goryo Shrine|lt=Goryō Shrine|ja|御霊神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goryō Shrine|qq=}}, [[:en:Tenmangū|Tenmangū]], and Katsuyama Inari Shrine. == Festivals == * festival – Second Sunday in May * Sōmen Festival == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall and five ridge plaques. * Paper manuscript of the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] (Lower Chapter, Age of the Gods) – A 14th-century transcription. === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Multiple structures, including the Main Hall Shelter, North and South Gates, and various auxiliary shrines. == Access == * Nearest station: [[:en:Hankyu Kyoto Line|Hankyu Kyoto Line]] [[Nishimukō Station]], 500 meters northwest. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Mutsume Koreka|ja|六人部是香|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mutsume Koreka|qq=}} – Late Edo-period scholar and former shrine priest. == External Links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20190101000000/http://www.geocities.jp/mukojinjahp/index.htm Mukō Shrine Official Website (archived)] * {{Commonscat-inline}} {{shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|向日神社|version=99695516|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] }} [[sn:Muko Shrine]] [[ja:向日神社]] [[Category:Registered Tangible Cultural Property of Japan in Kyoto prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in box|w|48434848}} [[File:Muko-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Mukō Shrine''' (向日神社, Mukō Jinja), also known as '''Mukō Myōjin''', is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in Mukōchō Kitayama, [[:en:Mukō|Mukō]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]]. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} (Myōjin Taisha and small shrine) and a former prefectural shrine.
== Deities ==
* Main deities: [[:en:Toshigami|Toshigami]], [[:en:Yakusanoikazuchi|Yakusanoikazuchi]], {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamayori-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タマヨリビメ}}, [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]]
== History ==
Originally, Mukō Shrine consisted of two separate shrines on Mukō Mountain: {{ill|Muka Shrine|ja|向神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muka Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (U11er Shrine) and {{ill|Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Honoikazuchi Shrine|ja|火雷神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honoikazuchi Shrine|qq=}} (Lower Shrine). Both are ancient shrines mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, with Honoikazuchi Shrine being one of the candidates for the {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} "Otokuni-zazuchi Shrine" (other candidates include {{ill|Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Sumiyagashira Shrine|ja|角宮神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyagashira Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Nagaokakyō, Kyoto|Nagaokakyō]]).
Muka Shrine is believed to have originated when Toshigami (Mukō Kami) stayed on Mukō Mountain and promoted rice cultivation. Initially, the shrine was located atop the {{ill|Itsukahara Kofun|lt=Itsukahara Kofun|lt=Gotsukahara Kofun|ja|五塚原古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gotsukahara Kofun|qq=}}, 500 meters north of its current location. In 718 (2nd year of [[:en:Yōrō|Yōrō]]), the shrine was relocated to its current position south of the {{ill|Motoinari Kofun|lt=Motoinari Kofun|lt=Moto-Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Inari Kofun|qq=}}.
Honoikazuchi Shrine is said to have been established when Emperor Jimmu enshrined the deity Honoikazuchi during his journey from [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] to {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|山背国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashiro Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 山城国}}. It is also believed to have been founded around 518 (12th year of [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]]), when Keitai built the {{ill|Otokuni Palace|ja|乙訓宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otokuni Palace|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In 718, Honoikazuchi Shrine incorporated Tamayorihime and Emperor Jimmu as deities.
In 784 (3rd year of [[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]]), the establishment of [[:en:Nagaoka-kyō|Nagaoka-kyō]] brought the imperial palace, Nagaoka-no-miya, to the eastern foot of Muka Shrine.
The chief priest of Honoikazuchi Shrine joined Emperor Go-Toba's forces during the [[:en:Jōkyū War|Jōkyū Rebellion]] in 1221 but later fled to the {{ill|Rokuto-no-shō|ja|六人部荘|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokuto-no-shō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} estate in present-day [[:en:Fukuchiyama, Kyoto|Fukuchiyama]]. After returning, the shrine's influence waned, and in 1275 (1st year of [[:en:Kenji (era)|Kenji]]), Muka Shrine merged with Honoikazuchi Shrine, forming Mukō Shrine and becoming the area's guardian shrine.
The current main hall was rebuilt in 1422 (29th year of [[:en:Ōei|Ōei]]) with the cooperation of seven surrounding villages. During the Muromachi period, Mukō Shrine became a rallying point for local {{ill|Tsuchi Ikki|ja|土一揆|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchi Ikki|qq=}} peasant uprisings before they marched on Kyoto.
In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine.
In 1961 (36th year of [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]]), the surrounding hill was partially excavated for the construction of the [[:en:Meishin Expressway|Meishin Expressway]]. Today, the shrine and Moto-Inari Kofun remain as a small hill. In recent years, the shrine has been surrounded by a cherry blossom park, partially restoring the area’s former beauty.
The main hall of Tokyo's [[:en:Meiji Shrine|Meiji Shrine]] is modeled after Mukō Shrine's main hall, scaled up by 1.5 times.
== Grounds ==
[[File:Mukō-jinja-Cherry Blossoms of a11roach to a shrine.jpg|thumb|Path lined with cherry blossoms leading to the shrine]] From {{ill|Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|ja|京都府道・大阪府道67号西京高槻線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|qq=}} (Astro Street), visitors enter a long, stone-paved path lined with cherry, azalea, and maple trees. The incline is scenic but shared with vehicles, requiring caution.
* Main Hall (Important Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1422 during the Muromachi period. Representative of nagare-zukuri architecture. The model for the Meiji Shrine. ''' Main Hall Shelter (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842 (13th year of [[:en:Tenpō|Tenpō]]). * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842. * North and South Gates (Registered Tangible Cultural Properties) – Built in 1842. * Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1842. * Kagura Hall – Rebuilt in 2003 after the original structure (built in 1625) was lost in a 2002 fire. * Guest Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Relocated from a youth training facility in Osaka around 1965. * Purification Pavilion (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Late 18th-century reconstruction.
=== Auxiliary Shrines === * Ancestral Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1688 (1st year of [[:en:Genroku|Genroku]]). * Five Shrines (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Dedicated to various deities. * Other shrines include {{ill|Goryo Shrine|lt=Goryo Shrine|lt=Goryō Shrine|ja|御霊神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goryō Shrine|qq=}}, [[:en:Tenmangū|Tenmangū]], and Katsuyama Inari Shrine.
== Festivals ==
* festival – Second Sunday in May * Sōmen Festival
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall and five ridge plaques. * Paper manuscript of the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] (Lower Chapter, Age of the Gods) – A 14th-century transcription.
=== Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Multiple structures, including the Main Hall Shelter, North and South Gates, and various auxiliary shrines.
== Access ==
* Nearest station: [[:en:Hankyu Kyoto Line|Hankyu Kyoto Line]] [[Nishimukō Station]], 500 meters northwest.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Mutsume Koreka|ja|六人部是香|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mutsume Koreka|qq=}} – Late Edo-period scholar and former shrine priest.
== External Links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20190101000000/http://www.geocities.jp/mukojinjahp/index.htm Mukō Shrine Official Website (archived)] * {{Commonscat-inline}}

Taisha|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} and small shrine), former {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|府社|lt=Fu-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#Metropolitan_and_Prefectural_Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}} | Main hall = Three-bay nagare-zukuri (Important Cultural Property) | festival = Second Sunday in May | Religious rituals = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Muko-jinja, shaden.jpg | location = {{ill|Muko|qid=Q850640}} | coordinates = {{Coord|34.944111|N|135.697111|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Toshigami|qid=Q3028663}} }} {{osm box|w|48434848}} [[File:Muko-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Mukō Shrine''' (向日神社, Mukō Jinja), also known as '''Mukō Myōjin''', is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in Mukōchō Kitayama, [[:en:Mukō|Mukō]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]]. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} (Myōjin Taisha and small shrine) and a former prefectural shrine.
== Deities ==
* Main deities: [[:en:Toshigami|Toshigami]], [[:en:Yakusanoikazuchi|Yakusanoikazuchi]], {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamayori-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タマヨリビメ}}, [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]]
== History ==
Originally, Mukō Shrine consisted of two separate shrines on Mukō Mountain: {{ill|Muka Shrine|ja|向神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muka Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (U11er Shrine) and {{ill|Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Honoikazuchi Shrine|ja|火雷神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honoikazuchi Shrine|qq=}} (Lower Shrine). Both are ancient shrines mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, with Honoikazuchi Shrine being one of the candidates for the {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} "Otokuni-zazuchi Shrine" (other candidates include {{ill|Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Sumiyagashira Shrine|ja|角宮神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyagashira Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Nagaokakyō, Kyoto|Nagaokakyō]]).
Muka Shrine is believed to have originated when Toshigami (Mukō Kami) stayed on Mukō Mountain and promoted rice cultivation. Initially, the shrine was located atop the {{ill|Itsukahara Kofun|lt=Itsukahara Kofun|lt=Gotsukahara Kofun|ja|五塚原古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gotsukahara Kofun|qq=}}, 500 meters north of its current location. In 718 (2nd year of [[:en:Yōrō|Yōrō]]), the shrine was relocated to its current position south of the {{ill|Motoinari Kofun|lt=Motoinari Kofun|lt=Moto-Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Inari Kofun|qq=}}.
Honoikazuchi Shrine is said to have been established when Emperor Jimmu enshrined the deity Honoikazuchi during his journey from [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] to {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|山背国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashiro Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 山城国}}. It is also believed to have been founded around 518 (12th year of [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]]), when Keitai built the {{ill|Otokuni Palace|ja|乙訓宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otokuni Palace|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In 718, Honoikazuchi Shrine incorporated Tamayorihime and Emperor Jimmu as deities.
In 784 (3rd year of [[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]]), the establishment of [[:en:Nagaoka-kyō|Nagaoka-kyō]] brought the imperial palace, Nagaoka-no-miya, to the eastern foot of Muka Shrine.
The chief priest of Honoikazuchi Shrine joined Emperor Go-Toba's forces during the [[:en:Jōkyū War|Jōkyū Rebellion]] in 1221 but later fled to the {{ill|Rokuto-no-shō|ja|六人部荘|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokuto-no-shō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} estate in present-day [[:en:Fukuchiyama, Kyoto|Fukuchiyama]]. After returning, the shrine's influence waned, and in 1275 (1st year of [[:en:Kenji (era)|Kenji]]), Muka Shrine merged with Honoikazuchi Shrine, forming Mukō Shrine and becoming the area's guardian shrine.
The current main hall was rebuilt in 1422 (29th year of [[:en:Ōei|Ōei]]) with the cooperation of seven surrounding villages. During the Muromachi period, Mukō Shrine became a rallying point for local {{ill|Tsuchi Ikki|ja|土一揆|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchi Ikki|qq=}} peasant uprisings before they marched on Kyoto.
In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine.
In 1961 (36th year of [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]]), the surrounding hill was partially excavated for the construction of the [[:en:Meishin Expressway|Meishin Expressway]]. Today, the shrine and Moto-Inari Kofun remain as a small hill. In recent years, the shrine has been surrounded by a cherry blossom park, partially restoring the area’s former beauty.
The main hall of Tokyo's [[:en:Meiji Shrine|Meiji Shrine]] is modeled after Mukō Shrine's main hall, scaled up by 1.5 times.
== Grounds ==
[[File:Mukō-jinja-Cherry Blossoms of a11roach to a shrine.jpg|thumb|Path lined with cherry blossoms leading to the shrine]] From {{ill|Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|ja|京都府道・大阪府道67号西京高槻線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|qq=}} (Astro Street), visitors enter a long, stone-paved path lined with cherry, azalea, and maple trees. The incline is scenic but shared with vehicles, requiring caution.
* Main Hall (Important Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1422 during the Muromachi period. Representative of nagare-zukuri architecture. The model for the Meiji Shrine. ''' Main Hall Shelter (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842 (13th year of [[:en:Tenpō|Tenpō]]). * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842. * North and South Gates (Registered Tangible Cultural Properties) – Built in 1842. * Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1842. * Kagura Hall – Rebuilt in 2003 after the original structure (built in 1625) was lost in a 2002 fire. * Guest Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Relocated from a youth training facility in Osaka around 1965. * Purification Pavilion (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Late 18th-century reconstruction.
=== Auxiliary Shrines === * Ancestral Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1688 (1st year of [[:en:Genroku|Genroku]]). * Five Shrines (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Dedicated to various deities. * Other shrines include {{ill|Goryo Shrine|lt=Goryo Shrine|lt=Goryō Shrine|ja|御霊神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goryō Shrine|qq=}}, [[:en:Tenmangū|Tenmangū]], and Katsuyama Inari Shrine.
== Festivals ==
* festival – Second Sunday in May * Sōmen Festival
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall and five ridge plaques. * Paper manuscript of the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] (Lower Chapter, Age of the Gods) – A 14th-century transcription.
=== Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Multiple structures, including the Main Hall Shelter, North and South Gates, and various auxiliary shrines.
== Access ==
* Nearest station: [[:en:Hankyu Kyoto Line|Hankyu Kyoto Line]] [[Nishimukō Station]], 500 meters northwest.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Mutsume Koreka|ja|六人部是香|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mutsume Koreka|qq=}} – Late Edo-period scholar and former shrine priest.
== External Links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20190101000000/http://www.geocities.jp/mukojinjahp/index.htm Mukō Shrine Official Website (archived)] * {{Commonscat-inline}}

{{ill|Toshigami|qid=Q3028663}} }} {{osm box|w|48434848}} [[File:Muko-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Mukō Shrine''' (向日神社, Mukō Jinja), also known as '''Mukō Myōjin''', is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in Mukōchō Kitayama, [[:en:Mukō|Mukō]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]]. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} (Myōjin Taisha and small shrine) and a former prefectural shrine.
== Deities ==
* Main deities: [[:en:Toshigami|Toshigami]], [[:en:Yakusanoikazuchi|Yakusanoikazuchi]], {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamayori-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タマヨリビメ|1=Tamayori-hime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56344982}}, [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]]
== History ==
Originally, Mukō Shrine consisted of two separate shrines on Mukō Mountain: {{ill|Muka Shrine|ja|向神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muka Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (U11er Shrine) and {{ill|Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Honoikazuchi Shrine|ja|火雷神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honoikazuchi Shrine|qq=}} (Lower Shrine). Both are ancient shrines mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, with Honoikazuchi Shrine being one of the candidates for the {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} "Otokuni-zazuchi Shrine" (other candidates include {{ill|Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Sumiyagashira Shrine|ja|角宮神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyagashira Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Nagaokakyō, Kyoto|Nagaokakyō]]).
Muka Shrine is believed to have originated when Toshigami (Mukō Kami) stayed on Mukō Mountain and promoted rice cultivation. Initially, the shrine was located atop the {{ill|Itsukahara Kofun|lt=Itsukahara Kofun|lt=Gotsukahara Kofun|ja|五塚原古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gotsukahara Kofun|qq=}}, 500 meters north of its current location. In 718 (2nd year of [[:en:Yōrō|Yōrō]]), the shrine was relocated to its current position south of the {{ill|Motoinari Kofun|lt=Motoinari Kofun|lt=Moto-Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Inari Kofun|qq=}}.
Honoikazuchi Shrine is said to have been established when Emperor Jimmu enshrined the deity Honoikazuchi during his journey from [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] to {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|山背国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashiro Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 山城国|1=Yamashiro Province|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q749276}}. It is also believed to have been founded around 518 (12th year of [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]]), when Keitai built the {{ill|Otokuni Palace|ja|乙訓宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otokuni Palace|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In 718, Honoikazuchi Shrine incorporated Tamayorihime and Emperor Jimmu as deities.
In 784 (3rd year of [[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]]), the establishment of [[:en:Nagaoka-kyō|Nagaoka-kyō]] brought the imperial palace, Nagaoka-no-miya, to the eastern foot of Muka Shrine.
The chief priest of Honoikazuchi Shrine joined Emperor Go-Toba's forces during the [[:en:Jōkyū War|Jōkyū Rebellion]] in 1221 but later fled to the {{ill|Rokuto-no-shō|ja|六人部荘|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokuto-no-shō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} estate in present-day [[:en:Fukuchiyama, Kyoto|Fukuchiyama]]. After returning, the shrine's influence waned, and in 1275 (1st year of [[:en:Kenji (era)|Kenji]]), Muka Shrine merged with Honoikazuchi Shrine, forming Mukō Shrine and becoming the area's guardian shrine.
The current main hall was rebuilt in 1422 (29th year of [[:en:Ōei|Ōei]]) with the cooperation of seven surrounding villages. During the Muromachi period, Mukō Shrine became a rallying point for local {{ill|Tsuchi Ikki|ja|土一揆|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchi Ikki|qq=}} peasant uprisings before they marched on Kyoto.
In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine.
In 1961 (36th year of [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]]), the surrounding hill was partially excavated for the construction of the [[:en:Meishin Expressway|Meishin Expressway]]. Today, the shrine and Moto-Inari Kofun remain as a small hill. In recent years, the shrine has been surrounded by a cherry blossom park, partially restoring the area’s former beauty.
The main hall of Tokyo's [[:en:Meiji Shrine|Meiji Shrine]] is modeled after Mukō Shrine's main hall, scaled up by 1.5 times.
== Grounds ==
[[File:Mukō-jinja-Cherry Blossoms of a11roach to a shrine.jpg|thumb|Path lined with cherry blossoms leading to the shrine]] From {{ill|Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|ja|京都府道・大阪府道67号西京高槻線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|qq=|1=Kyoto Prefectural Road and Osaka Prefectural Road Route 67|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11375557}} (Astro Street), visitors enter a long, stone-paved path lined with cherry, azalea, and maple trees. The incline is scenic but shared with vehicles, requiring caution.
* Main Hall (Important Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1422 during the Muromachi period. Representative of nagare-zukuri architecture. The model for the Meiji Shrine. ''' Main Hall Shelter (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842 (13th year of [[:en:Tenpō|Tenpō]]). * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842. * North and South Gates (Registered Tangible Cultural Properties) – Built in 1842. * Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1842. * Kagura Hall – Rebuilt in 2003 after the original structure (built in 1625) was lost in a 2002 fire. * Guest Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Relocated from a youth training facility in Osaka around 1965. * Purification Pavilion (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Late 18th-century reconstruction.
=== Auxiliary Shrines === * Ancestral Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1688 (1st year of [[:en:Genroku|Genroku]]). * Five Shrines (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Dedicated to various deities. * Other shrines include {{ill|Goryo Shrine|lt=Goryo Shrine|lt=Goryō Shrine|ja|御霊神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goryō Shrine|qq=}}, [[:en:Tenmangū|Tenmangū]], and Katsuyama Inari Shrine.
== Festivals ==
* festival – Second Sunday in May * Sōmen Festival
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall and five ridge plaques. * Paper manuscript of the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] (Lower Chapter, Age of the Gods) – A 14th-century transcription.
=== Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Multiple structures, including the Main Hall Shelter, North and South Gates, and various auxiliary shrines.
== Access ==
* Nearest station: [[:en:Hankyu Kyoto Line|Hankyu Kyoto Line]] [[Nishimukō Station]], 500 meters northwest.

File:Muko-jinja, shaden.jpg | location = {{ill|Muko|qid=Q850640}} | coordinates = {{Coord|34.944111|N|135.697111|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Toshigami|qid=Q3028663}} }} {{osm box|w|48434848}} [[File:Muko-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Mukō Shrine''' (向日神社, Mukō Jinja), also known as '''Mukō Myōjin''', is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in Mukōchō Kitayama, [[:en:Mukō|Mukō]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]]. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} (Myōjin Taisha and small shrine) and a former prefectural shrine.
== Deities ==
* Main deities: [[:en:Toshigami|Toshigami]], [[:en:Yakusanoikazuchi|Yakusanoikazuchi]], {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamayori-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タマヨリビメ|1=Tamayori-hime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56344982}}, [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]]
== History ==
Originally, Mukō Shrine consisted of two separate shrines on Mukō Mountain: {{ill|Muka Shrine|ja|向神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muka Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (U11er Shrine) and {{ill|Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Honoikazuchi Shrine|ja|火雷神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honoikazuchi Shrine|qq=}} (Lower Shrine). Both are ancient shrines mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, with Honoikazuchi Shrine being one of the candidates for the {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} "Otokuni-zazuchi Shrine" (other candidates include {{ill|Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Sumiyagashira Shrine|ja|角宮神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyagashira Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Nagaokakyō, Kyoto|Nagaokakyō]]).
Muka Shrine is believed to have originated when Toshigami (Mukō Kami) stayed on Mukō Mountain and promoted rice cultivation. Initially, the shrine was located atop the {{ill|Itsukahara Kofun|lt=Itsukahara Kofun|lt=Gotsukahara Kofun|ja|五塚原古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gotsukahara Kofun|qq=}}, 500 meters north of its current location. In 718 (2nd year of [[:en:Yōrō|Yōrō]]), the shrine was relocated to its current position south of the {{ill|Motoinari Kofun|lt=Motoinari Kofun|lt=Moto-Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Inari Kofun|qq=}}.
Honoikazuchi Shrine is said to have been established when Emperor Jimmu enshrined the deity Honoikazuchi during his journey from [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] to {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|山背国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashiro Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 山城国|1=Yamashiro Province|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q749276}}. It is also believed to have been founded around 518 (12th year of [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]]), when Keitai built the {{ill|Otokuni Palace|ja|乙訓宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otokuni Palace|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In 718, Honoikazuchi Shrine incorporated Tamayorihime and Emperor Jimmu as deities.
In 784 (3rd year of [[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]]), the establishment of [[:en:Nagaoka-kyō|Nagaoka-kyō]] brought the imperial palace, Nagaoka-no-miya, to the eastern foot of Muka Shrine.
The chief priest of Honoikazuchi Shrine joined Emperor Go-Toba's forces during the [[:en:Jōkyū War|Jōkyū Rebellion]] in 1221 but later fled to the {{ill|Rokuto-no-shō|ja|六人部荘|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokuto-no-shō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} estate in present-day [[:en:Fukuchiyama, Kyoto|Fukuchiyama]]. After returning, the shrine's influence waned, and in 1275 (1st year of [[:en:Kenji (era)|Kenji]]), Muka Shrine merged with Honoikazuchi Shrine, forming Mukō Shrine and becoming the area's guardian shrine.
The current main hall was rebuilt in 1422 (29th year of [[:en:Ōei|Ōei]]) with the cooperation of seven surrounding villages. During the Muromachi period, Mukō Shrine became a rallying point for local {{ill|Tsuchi Ikki|ja|土一揆|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchi Ikki|qq=}} peasant uprisings before they marched on Kyoto.
In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine.
In 1961 (36th year of [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]]), the surrounding hill was partially excavated for the construction of the [[:en:Meishin Expressway|Meishin Expressway]]. Today, the shrine and Moto-Inari Kofun remain as a small hill. In recent years, the shrine has been surrounded by a cherry blossom park, partially restoring the area’s former beauty.
The main hall of Tokyo's [[:en:Meiji Shrine|Meiji Shrine]] is modeled after Mukō Shrine's main hall, scaled up by 1.5 times.
== Grounds ==
[[File:Mukō-jinja-Cherry Blossoms of a11roach to a shrine.jpg|thumb|Path lined with cherry blossoms leading to the shrine]] From {{ill|Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|ja|京都府道・大阪府道67号西京高槻線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|qq=|1=Kyoto Prefectural Road and Osaka Prefectural Road Route 67|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11375557}} (Astro Street), visitors enter a long, stone-paved path lined with cherry, azalea, and maple trees. The incline is scenic but shared with vehicles, requiring caution.
* Main Hall (Important Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1422 during the Muromachi period. Representative of nagare-zukuri architecture. The model for the Meiji Shrine. ''' Main Hall Shelter (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842 (13th year of [[:en:Tenpō|Tenpō]]). * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842. * North and South Gates (Registered Tangible Cultural Properties) – Built in 1842. * Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1842. * Kagura Hall – Rebuilt in 2003 after the original structure (built in 1625) was lost in a 2002 fire. * Guest Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Relocated from a youth training facility in Osaka around 1965. * Purification Pavilion (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Late 18th-century reconstruction.
=== Auxiliary Shrines === * Ancestral Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1688 (1st year of [[:en:Genroku|Genroku]]). * Five Shrines (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Dedicated to various deities. * Other shrines include {{ill|Goryo Shrine|lt=Goryo Shrine|lt=Goryō Shrine|ja|御霊神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goryō Shrine|qq=}}, [[:en:Tenmangū|Tenmangū]], and Katsuyama Inari Shrine.
== Festivals ==
* festival – Second Sunday in May * Sōmen Festival
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall and five ridge plaques. * Paper manuscript of the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] (Lower Chapter, Age of the Gods) – A 14th-century transcription.
=== Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Multiple structures, including the Main Hall Shelter, North and South Gates, and various auxiliary shrines.
== Access ==
* Nearest station: [[:en:Hankyu Kyoto Line|Hankyu Kyoto Line]] [[Nishimukō Station]], 500 meters northwest.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Mutsume Koreka|ja|六人部是香|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mutsume Koreka|qq=|1=Yoshika Mutobe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6537708}} – Late Edo-period scholar and former shrine priest.
== External Links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20190101000000/http://www.geocities.jp/mukojinjahp/index.htm Mukō Shrine Official Website (archived)] * {{Commonscat-inline}}

== History ==
Originally, Mukō Shrine consisted of two separate shrines on Mukō Mountain: {{ill|Muka Shrine|ja|向神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muka Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (U11er Shrine) and {{ill|Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Honoikazuchi Shrine|ja|火雷神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honoikazuchi Shrine|qq=}} (Lower Shrine). Both are ancient shrines mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, with Honoikazuchi Shrine being one of the candidates for the {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} "Otokuni-zazuchi Shrine" (other candidates include {{ill|Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Sumiyagashira Shrine|ja|角宮神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyagashira Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Nagaokakyō, Kyoto|Nagaokakyō]]).
Muka Shrine is believed to have originated when Toshigami (Mukō Kami) stayed on Mukō Mountain and promoted rice cultivation. Initially, the shrine was located atop the {{ill|Itsukahara Kofun|lt=Itsukahara Kofun|lt=Gotsukahara Kofun|ja|五塚原古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gotsukahara Kofun|qq=}}, 500 meters north of its current location. In 718 (2nd year of [[:en:Yōrō|Yōrō]]), the shrine was relocated to its current position south of the {{ill|Motoinari Kofun|lt=Motoinari Kofun|lt=Moto-Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Inari Kofun|qq=}}.
Honoikazuchi Shrine is said to have been established when Emperor Jimmu enshrined the deity Honoikazuchi during his journey from [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] to {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|山背国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashiro Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 山城国|1=Yamashiro Province|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q749276}}. It is also believed to have been founded around 518 (12th year of [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]]), when Keitai built the {{ill|Otokuni Palace|ja|乙訓宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otokuni Palace|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In 718, Honoikazuchi Shrine incorporated Tamayorihime and Emperor Jimmu as deities.
In 784 (3rd year of [[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]]), the establishment of [[:en:Nagaoka-kyō|Nagaoka-kyō]] brought the imperial palace, Nagaoka-no-miya, to the eastern foot of Muka Shrine.
The chief priest of Honoikazuchi Shrine joined Emperor Go-Toba's forces during the [[:en:Jōkyū War|Jōkyū Rebellion]] in 1221 but later fled to the {{ill|Rokuto-no-shō|ja|六人部荘|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokuto-no-shō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} estate in present-day [[:en:Fukuchiyama, Kyoto|Fukuchiyama]]. After returning, the shrine's influence waned, and in 1275 (1st year of [[:en:Kenji (era)|Kenji]]), Muka Shrine merged with Honoikazuchi Shrine, forming Mukō Shrine and becoming the area's guardian shrine.
The current main hall was rebuilt in 1422 (29th year of [[:en:Ōei|Ōei]]) with the cooperation of seven surrounding villages. During the Muromachi period, Mukō Shrine became a rallying point for local {{ill|Tsuchi Ikki|ja|土一揆|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchi Ikki|qq=}} peasant uprisings before they marched on Kyoto.
In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine.
In 1961 (36th year of [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]]), the surrounding hill was partially excavated for the construction of the [[:en:Meishin Expressway|Meishin Expressway]]. Today, the shrine and Moto-Inari Kofun remain as a small hill. In recent years, the shrine has been surrounded by a cherry blossom park, partially restoring the area’s former beauty.
The main hall of Tokyo's [[:en:Meiji Shrine|Meiji Shrine]] is modeled after Mukō Shrine's main hall, scaled up by 1.5 times.
== Grounds ==
[[File:Mukō-jinja-Cherry Blossoms of a11roach to a shrine.jpg|thumb|Path lined with cherry blossoms leading to the shrine]] From {{ill|Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|ja|京都府道・大阪府道67号西京高槻線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|qq=|1=Kyoto Prefectural Road and Osaka Prefectural Road Route 67|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11375557}} (Astro Street), visitors enter a long, stone-paved path lined with cherry, azalea, and maple trees. The incline is scenic but shared with vehicles, requiring caution.
* Main Hall (Important Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1422 during the Muromachi period. Representative of nagare-zukuri architecture. The model for the Meiji Shrine. ''' Main Hall Shelter (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842 (13th year of [[:en:Tenpō|Tenpō]]). * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842. * North and South Gates (Registered Tangible Cultural Properties) – Built in 1842. * Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1842. * Kagura Hall – Rebuilt in 2003 after the original structure (built in 1625) was lost in a 2002 fire. * Guest Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Relocated from a youth training facility in Osaka around 1965. * Purification Pavilion (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Late 18th-century reconstruction.
=== Auxiliary Shrines === * Ancestral Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1688 (1st year of [[:en:Genroku|Genroku]]). * Five Shrines (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Dedicated to various deities. * Other shrines include {{ill|Goryo Shrine|lt=Goryo Shrine|lt=Goryō Shrine|ja|御霊神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goryō Shrine|qq=}}, [[:en:Tenmangū|Tenmangū]], and Katsuyama Inari Shrine.
