Shrine|WD=Q134917326}}{{efn|lt=Fu-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} | Main hall = Three-bay nagare-zukuri (Important Cultural Property) | festival = Second Sunday in May | Religious rituals = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Muko-jinja, shaden.jpg | location = {{ill|Muko|WD=Q850640}} | coordinates = {{Coord|34.944111|N|135.697111|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Toshigami|WD=Q3028663}} }} {{osm box|w|48434848}} [[File:Muko-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Mukō Shrine''' (向日神社, Mukō Jinja), also known as '''Mukō Myōjin''', is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in Mukōchō Kitayama, [[:en:Mukō|Mukō]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]]. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} (Myōjin Taisha and small shrine) and a former prefectural shrine.
== Deities ==
* Main deities: [[:en:Toshigami|Toshigami]], [[:en:Yakusanoikazuchi|Yakusanoikazuchi]], {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamayori-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タマヨリビメ|1=Tamayori-hime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56344982}}, [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]]
== History ==
Originally, Mukō Shrine consisted of two separate shrines on Mukō Mountain: {{ill|Muka Shrine|ja|向神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muka Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (U11er Shrine) and {{ill|Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Honoikazuchi Shrine|ja|火雷神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honoikazuchi Shrine|qq=}} (Lower Shrine). Both are ancient shrines mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, with Honoikazuchi Shrine being one of the candidates for the {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} "Otokuni-zazuchi Shrine" (other candidates include {{ill|Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Sumiyagashira Shrine|ja|角宮神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyagashira Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Nagaokakyō, Kyoto|Nagaokakyō]]).
Muka Shrine is believed to have originated when Toshigami (Mukō Kami) stayed on Mukō Mountain and promoted rice cultivation. Initially, the shrine was located atop the {{ill|Itsukahara Kofun|lt=Itsukahara Kofun|lt=Gotsukahara Kofun|ja|五塚原古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gotsukahara Kofun|qq=}}, 500 meters north of its current location. In 718 (2nd year of [[:en:Yōrō|Yōrō]]), the shrine was relocated to its current position south of the {{ill|Motoinari Kofun|lt=Motoinari Kofun|lt=Moto-Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Inari Kofun|qq=}}.
Honoikazuchi Shrine is said to have been established when Emperor Jimmu enshrined the deity Honoikazuchi during his journey from [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] to {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|山背国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashiro Province|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 山城国|1=Yamashiro Province|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q749276}}. It is also believed to have been founded around 518 (12th year of [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]]), when Keitai built the {{ill|Otokuni Palace|ja|乙訓宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otokuni Palace|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In 718, Honoikazuchi Shrine incorporated Tamayorihime and Emperor Jimmu as deities.
In 784 (3rd year of [[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]]), the establishment of [[:en:Nagaoka-kyō|Nagaoka-kyō]] brought the imperial palace, Nagaoka-no-miya, to the eastern foot of Muka Shrine.
The chief priest of Honoikazuchi Shrine joined Emperor Go-Toba's forces during the [[:en:Jōkyū War|Jōkyū Rebellion]] in 1221 but later fled to the {{ill|Rokuto-no-shō|ja|六人部荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokuto-no-shō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} estate in present-day [[:en:Fukuchiyama, Kyoto|Fukuchiyama]]. After returning, the shrine's influence waned, and in 1275 (1st year of [[:en:Kenji (era)|Kenji]]), Muka Shrine merged with Honoikazuchi Shrine, forming Mukō Shrine and becoming the area's guardian shrine.
The current main hall was rebuilt in 1422 (29th year of [[:en:Ōei|Ōei]]) with the cooperation of seven surrounding villages. During the Muromachi period, Mukō Shrine became a rallying point for local {{ill|Tsuchi Ikki|ja|土一揆|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchi Ikki|qq=}} peasant uprisings before they marched on Kyoto.
In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine.
In 1961 (36th year of [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]]), the surrounding hill was partially excavated for the construction of the [[:en:Meishin Expressway|Meishin Expressway]]. Today, the shrine and Moto-Inari Kofun remain as a small hill. In recent years, the shrine has been surrounded by a cherry blossom park, partially restoring the area’s former beauty.
The main hall of Tokyo's [[:en:Meiji Shrine|Meiji Shrine]] is modeled after Mukō Shrine's main hall, scaled up by 1.5 times.
== Grounds ==
[[File:Mukō-jinja-Cherry Blossoms of a11roach to a shrine.jpg|thumb|Path lined with cherry blossoms leading to the shrine]] From {{ill|Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|ja|京都府道・大阪府道67号西京高槻線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|1=Kyoto Prefectural Road and Osaka Prefectural Road Route 67|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11375557}} (Astro Street), visitors enter a long, stone-paved path lined with cherry, azalea, and maple trees. The incline is scenic but shared with vehicles, requiring caution.
* Main Hall (Important Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1422 during the Muromachi period. Representative of nagare-zukuri architecture. The model for the Meiji Shrine. ''' Main Hall Shelter (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842 (13th year of [[:en:Tenpō|Tenpō]]). * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842. * North and South Gates (Registered Tangible Cultural Properties) – Built in 1842. * Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1842. * Kagura Hall – Rebuilt in 2003 after the original structure (built in 1625) was lost in a 2002 fire. * Guest Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Relocated from a youth training facility in Osaka around 1965. * Purification Pavilion (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Late 18th-century reconstruction.
=== Auxiliary Shrines === * Ancestral Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1688 (1st year of [[:en:Genroku|Genroku]]). * Five Shrines (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Dedicated to various deities. * Other shrines include {{ill|Goryo Shrine|lt=Goryo Shrine|lt=Goryō Shrine|ja|御霊神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goryō Shrine|qq=}}, [[:en:Tenmangū|Tenmangū]], and Katsuyama Inari Shrine.
== Festivals ==
* festival – Second Sunday in May * Sōmen Festival
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall and five ridge plaques. * Paper manuscript of the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] (Lower Chapter, Age of the Gods) – A 14th-century transcription.
=== Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Multiple structures, including the Main Hall Shelter, North and South Gates, and various auxiliary shrines.
== Access ==
* Nearest station: [[:en:Hankyu Kyoto Line|Hankyu Kyoto Line]] [[Nishimukō Station]], 500 meters northwest.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Mutsume Koreka|ja|六人部是香|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mutsume Koreka|1=Yoshika Mutobe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6537708}} – Late Edo-period scholar and former shrine priest.
== External Links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20190101000000/http://www.geocities.jp/mukojinjahp/index.htm Mukō Shrine Official Website (archived)] * {{Commonscat-inline}}

In 718, Honoikazuchi Shrine incorporated Tamayorihime and Emperor Jimmu as deities.

In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine.
In 1961 (36th year of [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]]), the surrounding hill was partially excavated for the construction of the [[:en:Meishin Expressway|Meishin Expressway]]. [[File:Mukō-jinja-Cherry Blossoms of a11roach to a shrine.jpg|thumb|Path lined with cherry blossoms leading to the shrine]] From {{ill|Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|ja|京都府道・大阪府道67号西京高槻線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|1=Kyoto Prefectural Road and Osaka Prefectural Road Route 67|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11375557}} (Astro Street), visitors enter a long, stone-paved path lined with cherry, azalea, and maple trees. The incline is scenic but shared with vehicles, requiring caution.

''' Main Hall Shelter (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842 (13th year of [[:en:Tenpō|Tenpō]]). * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842. * North and South Gates (Registered Tangible Cultural Properties) – Built in 1842. * Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1842. * Kagura Hall – Rebuilt in 2003 after the original structure (built in 1625) was lost in a 2002 fire. * Guest Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Relocated from a youth training facility in Osaka around 1965. * Purification Pavilion (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Late 18th-century reconstruction.

{{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamayori-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タマヨリビメ|1=Tamayori-hime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56344982}}, [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] | Shrine Rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} and small shrine), former {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|府社|1=Metropolitan Shrine|WD=Q134917326}}{{efn|lt=Fu-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} | Main hall = Three-bay nagare-zukuri (Important Cultural Property) | festival = Second Sunday in May | Religious rituals = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Muko-jinja, shaden.jpg | location = {{ill|Muko|WD=Q850640}} | coordinates = {{Coord|34.944111|N|135.697111|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Toshigami|WD=Q3028663}} }} {{osm box|w|48434848}} [[File:Muko-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Mukō Shrine''' (向日神社, Mukō Jinja), also known as '''Mukō Myōjin''', is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in Mukōchō Kitayama, [[:en:Mukō|Mukō]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]]. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} (Myōjin Taisha and small shrine) and a former prefectural shrine.
== Deities ==
* Main deities: [[:en:Toshigami|Toshigami]], [[:en:Yakusanoikazuchi|Yakusanoikazuchi]], {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamayori-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タマヨリビメ|1=Tamayori-hime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56344982}}, [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]]
== History ==
Originally, Mukō Shrine consisted of two separate shrines on Mukō Mountain: {{ill|Muka Shrine|ja|向神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muka Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (U11er Shrine) and {{ill|Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Honoikazuchi Shrine|ja|火雷神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honoikazuchi Shrine|qq=}} (Lower Shrine). Both are ancient shrines mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, with Honoikazuchi Shrine being one of the candidates for the {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} "Otokuni-zazuchi Shrine" (other candidates include {{ill|Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Sumiyagashira Shrine|ja|角宮神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyagashira Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Nagaokakyō, Kyoto|Nagaokakyō]]).
Muka Shrine is believed to have originated when Toshigami (Mukō Kami) stayed on Mukō Mountain and promoted rice cultivation. Initially, the shrine was located atop the {{ill|Itsukahara Kofun|lt=Itsukahara Kofun|lt=Gotsukahara Kofun|ja|五塚原古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gotsukahara Kofun|qq=}}, 500 meters north of its current location. In 718 (2nd year of [[:en:Yōrō|Yōrō]]), the shrine was relocated to its current position south of the {{ill|Motoinari Kofun|lt=Motoinari Kofun|lt=Moto-Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Inari Kofun|qq=}}.
Honoikazuchi Shrine is said to have been established when Emperor Jimmu enshrined the deity Honoikazuchi during his journey from [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] to {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|山背国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashiro Province|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 山城国|1=Yamashiro Province|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q749276}}. It is also believed to have been founded around 518 (12th year of [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]]), when Keitai built the {{ill|Otokuni Palace|ja|乙訓宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otokuni Palace|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In 718, Honoikazuchi Shrine incorporated Tamayorihime and Emperor Jimmu as deities.
In 784 (3rd year of [[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]]), the establishment of [[:en:Nagaoka-kyō|Nagaoka-kyō]] brought the imperial palace, Nagaoka-no-miya, to the eastern foot of Muka Shrine.
The chief priest of Honoikazuchi Shrine joined Emperor Go-Toba's forces during the [[:en:Jōkyū War|Jōkyū Rebellion]] in 1221 but later fled to the {{ill|Rokuto-no-shō|ja|六人部荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokuto-no-shō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} estate in present-day [[:en:Fukuchiyama, Kyoto|Fukuchiyama]]. After returning, the shrine's influence waned, and in 1275 (1st year of [[:en:Kenji (era)|Kenji]]), Muka Shrine merged with Honoikazuchi Shrine, forming Mukō Shrine and becoming the area's guardian shrine.
The current main hall was rebuilt in 1422 (29th year of [[:en:Ōei|Ōei]]) with the cooperation of seven surrounding villages. During the Muromachi period, Mukō Shrine became a rallying point for local {{ill|Tsuchi Ikki|ja|土一揆|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchi Ikki|qq=}} peasant uprisings before they marched on Kyoto.
In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine.
In 1961 (36th year of [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]]), the surrounding hill was partially excavated for the construction of the [[:en:Meishin Expressway|Meishin Expressway]]. Today, the shrine and Moto-Inari Kofun remain as a small hill. In recent years, the shrine has been surrounded by a cherry blossom park, partially restoring the area’s former beauty.
The main hall of Tokyo's [[:en:Meiji Shrine|Meiji Shrine]] is modeled after Mukō Shrine's main hall, scaled up by 1.5 times.
== Grounds ==
[[File:Mukō-jinja-Cherry Blossoms of a11roach to a shrine.jpg|thumb|Path lined with cherry blossoms leading to the shrine]] From {{ill|Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|ja|京都府道・大阪府道67号西京高槻線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|1=Kyoto Prefectural Road and Osaka Prefectural Road Route 67|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11375557}} (Astro Street), visitors enter a long, stone-paved path lined with cherry, azalea, and maple trees. The incline is scenic but shared with vehicles, requiring caution.
* Main Hall (Important Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1422 during the Muromachi period. Representative of nagare-zukuri architecture. The model for the Meiji Shrine. ''' Main Hall Shelter (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842 (13th year of [[:en:Tenpō|Tenpō]]). * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842. * North and South Gates (Registered Tangible Cultural Properties) – Built in 1842. * Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1842. * Kagura Hall – Rebuilt in 2003 after the original structure (built in 1625) was lost in a 2002 fire. * Guest Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Relocated from a youth training facility in Osaka around 1965. * Purification Pavilion (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Late 18th-century reconstruction.
=== Auxiliary Shrines === * Ancestral Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1688 (1st year of [[:en:Genroku|Genroku]]). * Five Shrines (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Dedicated to various deities. * Other shrines include {{ill|Goryo Shrine|lt=Goryo Shrine|lt=Goryō Shrine|ja|御霊神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goryō Shrine|qq=}}, [[:en:Tenmangū|Tenmangū]], and Katsuyama Inari Shrine.
== Festivals ==
* festival – Second Sunday in May * Sōmen Festival
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall and five ridge plaques. * Paper manuscript of the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] (Lower Chapter, Age of the Gods) – A 14th-century transcription.
=== Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Multiple structures, including the Main Hall Shelter, North and South Gates, and various auxiliary shrines.
== Access ==
* Nearest station: [[:en:Hankyu Kyoto Line|Hankyu Kyoto Line]] [[Nishimukō Station]], 500 meters northwest.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Mutsume Koreka|ja|六人部是香|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mutsume Koreka|1=Yoshika Mutobe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6537708}} – Late Edo-period scholar and former shrine priest.
== External Links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20190101000000/http://www.geocities.jp/mukojinjahp/index.htm Mukō Shrine Official Website (archived)] * {{Commonscat-inline}}

* Main deities: [[:en:Toshigami|Toshigami]], [[:en:Yakusanoikazuchi|Yakusanoikazuchi]], {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamayori-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タマヨリビメ|1=Tamayori-hime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56344982}}, [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]]

Both are ancient shrines mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, with Honoikazuchi Shrine being one of the candidates for the {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} "Otokuni-zazuchi Shrine" (other candidates include {{ill|Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Sumiyagashira Shrine|ja|角宮神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyagashira Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Nagaokakyō, Kyoto|Nagaokakyō]]).
Muka Shrine is believed to have originated when Toshigami (Mukō Kami) stayed on Mukō Mountain and promoted rice cultivation. Initially, the shrine was located atop the {{ill|Itsukahara Kofun|lt=Itsukahara Kofun|lt=Gotsukahara Kofun|ja|五塚原古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gotsukahara Kofun|qq=}}, 500 meters north of its current location. In 718 (2nd year of [[:en:Yōrō|Yōrō]]), the shrine was relocated to its current position south of the {{ill|Motoinari Kofun|lt=Motoinari Kofun|lt=Moto-Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Inari Kofun|qq=}}.
Honoikazuchi Shrine is said to have been established when Emperor Jimmu enshrined the deity Honoikazuchi during his journey from [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] to {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|山背国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashiro Province|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 山城国|1=Yamashiro Province|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q749276}}. It is also believed to have been founded around 518 (12th year of [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]]), when Keitai built the {{ill|Otokuni Palace|ja|乙訓宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otokuni Palace|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In 718, Honoikazuchi Shrine incorporated Tamayorihime and Emperor Jimmu as deities.
In 784 (3rd year of [[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]]), the establishment of [[:en:Nagaoka-kyō|Nagaoka-kyō]] brought the imperial palace, Nagaoka-no-miya, to the eastern foot of Muka Shrine.
The chief priest of Honoikazuchi Shrine joined Emperor Go-Toba's forces during the [[:en:Jōkyū War|Jōkyū Rebellion]] in 1221 but later fled to the {{ill|Rokuto-no-shō|ja|六人部荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokuto-no-shō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} estate in present-day [[:en:Fukuchiyama, Kyoto|Fukuchiyama]]. After returning, the shrine's influence waned, and in 1275 (1st year of [[:en:Kenji (era)|Kenji]]), Muka Shrine merged with Honoikazuchi Shrine, forming Mukō Shrine and becoming the area's guardian shrine.
The current main hall was rebuilt in 1422 (29th year of [[:en:Ōei|Ōei]]) with the cooperation of seven surrounding villages. During the Muromachi period, Mukō Shrine became a rallying point for local {{ill|Tsuchi Ikki|ja|土一揆|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchi Ikki|qq=}} peasant uprisings before they marched on Kyoto.
In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine.
In 1961 (36th year of [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]]), the surrounding hill was partially excavated for the construction of the [[:en:Meishin Expressway|Meishin Expressway]]. Today, the shrine and Moto-Inari Kofun remain as a small hill. In recent years, the shrine has been surrounded by a cherry blossom park, partially restoring the area’s former beauty.
The main hall of Tokyo's [[:en:Meiji Shrine|Meiji Shrine]] is modeled after Mukō Shrine's main hall, scaled up by 1.5 times.

[[File:Mukō-jinja-Cherry Blossoms of a11roach to a shrine.jpg|thumb|Path lined with cherry blossoms leading to the shrine]] From {{ill|Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|ja|京都府道・大阪府道67号西京高槻線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|1=Kyoto Prefectural Road and Osaka Prefectural Road Route 67|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11375557}} (Astro Street), visitors enter a long, stone-paved path lined with cherry, azalea, and maple trees. The incline is scenic but shared with vehicles, requiring caution.
* Main Hall (Important Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1422 during the Muromachi period. Representative of nagare-zukuri architecture. The model for the Meiji Shrine. ''' Main Hall Shelter (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842 (13th year of [[:en:Tenpō|Tenpō]]). * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842. * North and South Gates (Registered Tangible Cultural Properties) – Built in 1842. * Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1842. * Kagura Hall – Rebuilt in 2003 after the original structure (built in 1625) was lost in a 2002 fire. * Guest Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Relocated from a youth training facility in Osaka around 1965. * Purification Pavilion (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Late 18th-century reconstruction.
=== Auxiliary Shrines === * Ancestral Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1688 (1st year of [[:en:Genroku|Genroku]]). * Five Shrines (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Dedicated to various deities. * Other shrines include {{ill|Goryo Shrine|lt=Goryo Shrine|lt=Goryō Shrine|ja|御霊神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goryō Shrine|qq=}}, [[:en:Tenmangū|Tenmangū]], and Katsuyama Inari Shrine.

[[:en:Yakusanoikazuchi|Honoikazuchi no Ōkami]], {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamayori-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タマヨリビメ|1=Tamayori-hime|comment=changed title based {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} and small shrine), former {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|府社|1=Metropolitan Shrine|WD=Q134917326}}{{efn|lt=Fu-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} | Main hall = Three-bay nagare-zukuri (Important Cultural Property) | festival = Second Sunday in May | Religious rituals = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Muko-jinja, shaden.jpg | location = {{ill|Muko|WD=Q850640}} | coordinates = {{Coord|34.944111|N|135.697111|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Toshigami|WD=Q3028663}} }} {{osm box|w|48434848}} [[File:Muko-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Mukō Shrine''' (向日神社, Mukō Jinja), also known as '''Mukō Myōjin''', is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in Mukōchō Kitayama, [[:en:Mukō|Mukō]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]]. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} (Myōjin Taisha and small shrine) and a former prefectural shrine.
== Deities ==
* Main deities: [[:en:Toshigami|Toshigami]], [[:en:Yakusanoikazuchi|Yakusanoikazuchi]], {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamayori-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タマヨリビメ|1=Tamayori-hime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56344982}}, [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]]
== History ==
Originally, Mukō Shrine consisted of two separate shrines on Mukō Mountain: {{ill|Muka Shrine|ja|向神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muka Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (U11er Shrine) and {{ill|Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Honoikazuchi Shrine|ja|火雷神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honoikazuchi Shrine|qq=}} (Lower Shrine). Both are ancient shrines mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, with Honoikazuchi Shrine being one of the candidates for the {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} "Otokuni-zazuchi Shrine" (other candidates include {{ill|Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Sumiyagashira Shrine|ja|角宮神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyagashira Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Nagaokakyō, Kyoto|Nagaokakyō]]).
Muka Shrine is believed to have originated when Toshigami (Mukō Kami) stayed on Mukō Mountain and promoted rice cultivation. Initially, the shrine was located atop the {{ill|Itsukahara Kofun|lt=Itsukahara Kofun|lt=Gotsukahara Kofun|ja|五塚原古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gotsukahara Kofun|qq=}}, 500 meters north of its current location. In 718 (2nd year of [[:en:Yōrō|Yōrō]]), the shrine was relocated to its current position south of the {{ill|Motoinari Kofun|lt=Motoinari Kofun|lt=Moto-Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Inari Kofun|qq=}}.
Honoikazuchi Shrine is said to have been established when Emperor Jimmu enshrined the deity Honoikazuchi during his journey from [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] to {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|山背国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashiro Province|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 山城国|1=Yamashiro Province|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q749276}}. It is also believed to have been founded around 518 (12th year of [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]]), when Keitai built the {{ill|Otokuni Palace|ja|乙訓宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otokuni Palace|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In 718, Honoikazuchi Shrine incorporated Tamayorihime and Emperor Jimmu as deities.
In 784 (3rd year of [[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]]), the establishment of [[:en:Nagaoka-kyō|Nagaoka-kyō]] brought the imperial palace, Nagaoka-no-miya, to the eastern foot of Muka Shrine.
The chief priest of Honoikazuchi Shrine joined Emperor Go-Toba's forces during the [[:en:Jōkyū War|Jōkyū Rebellion]] in 1221 but later fled to the {{ill|Rokuto-no-shō|ja|六人部荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokuto-no-shō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} estate in present-day [[:en:Fukuchiyama, Kyoto|Fukuchiyama]]. After returning, the shrine's influence waned, and in 1275 (1st year of [[:en:Kenji (era)|Kenji]]), Muka Shrine merged with Honoikazuchi Shrine, forming Mukō Shrine and becoming the area's guardian shrine.
The current main hall was rebuilt in 1422 (29th year of [[:en:Ōei|Ōei]]) with the cooperation of seven surrounding villages. During the Muromachi period, Mukō Shrine became a rallying point for local {{ill|Tsuchi Ikki|ja|土一揆|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchi Ikki|qq=}} peasant uprisings before they marched on Kyoto.
In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine.
In 1961 (36th year of [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]]), the surrounding hill was partially excavated for the construction of the [[:en:Meishin Expressway|Meishin Expressway]]. Today, the shrine and Moto-Inari Kofun remain as a small hill. In recent years, the shrine has been surrounded by a cherry blossom park, partially restoring the area’s former beauty.
The main hall of Tokyo's [[:en:Meiji Shrine|Meiji Shrine]] is modeled after Mukō Shrine's main hall, scaled up by 1.5 times.
== Grounds ==
[[File:Mukō-jinja-Cherry Blossoms of a11roach to a shrine.jpg|thumb|Path lined with cherry blossoms leading to the shrine]] From {{ill|Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|ja|京都府道・大阪府道67号西京高槻線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|1=Kyoto Prefectural Road and Osaka Prefectural Road Route 67|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11375557}} (Astro Street), visitors enter a long, stone-paved path lined with cherry, azalea, and maple trees. The incline is scenic but shared with vehicles, requiring caution.
* Main Hall (Important Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1422 during the Muromachi period. Representative of nagare-zukuri architecture. The model for the Meiji Shrine. ''' Main Hall Shelter (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842 (13th year of [[:en:Tenpō|Tenpō]]). * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842. * North and South Gates (Registered Tangible Cultural Properties) – Built in 1842. * Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1842. * Kagura Hall – Rebuilt in 2003 after the original structure (built in 1625) was lost in a 2002 fire. * Guest Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Relocated from a youth training facility in Osaka around 1965. * Purification Pavilion (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Late 18th-century reconstruction.
=== Auxiliary Shrines === * Ancestral Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1688 (1st year of [[:en:Genroku|Genroku]]). * Five Shrines (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Dedicated to various deities. * Other shrines include {{ill|Goryo Shrine|lt=Goryo Shrine|lt=Goryō Shrine|ja|御霊神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goryō Shrine|qq=}}, [[:en:Tenmangū|Tenmangū]], and Katsuyama Inari Shrine.
== Festivals ==
* festival – Second Sunday in May * Sōmen Festival
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall and five ridge plaques. * Paper manuscript of the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] (Lower Chapter, Age of the Gods) – A 14th-century transcription.
=== Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Multiple structures, including the Main Hall Shelter, North and South Gates, and various auxiliary shrines.
== Access ==
* Nearest station: [[:en:Hankyu Kyoto Line|Hankyu Kyoto Line]] [[Nishimukō Station]], 500 meters northwest.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Mutsume Koreka|ja|六人部是香|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mutsume Koreka|1=Yoshika Mutobe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6537708}} – Late Edo-period scholar and former shrine priest.
== External Links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20190101000000/http://www.geocities.jp/mukojinjahp/index.htm Mukō Shrine Official Website (archived)] * {{Commonscat-inline}}

Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] | Shrine Rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} and small shrine), former {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|府社|1=Metropolitan Shrine|WD=Q134917326}}{{efn|lt=Fu-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} | Main hall = Three-bay nagare-zukuri (Important Cultural Property) | festival = Second Sunday in May | Religious rituals = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Muko-jinja, shaden.jpg | location = {{ill|Muko|WD=Q850640}} | coordinates = {{Coord|34.944111|N|135.697111|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Toshigami|WD=Q3028663}} }} {{osm box|w|48434848}} [[File:Muko-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Mukō Shrine''' (向日神社, Mukō Jinja), also known as '''Mukō Myōjin''', is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in Mukōchō Kitayama, [[:en:Mukō|Mukō]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]]. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} (Myōjin Taisha and small shrine) and a former prefectural shrine.
== Deities ==
* Main deities: [[:en:Toshigami|Toshigami]], [[:en:Yakusanoikazuchi|Yakusanoikazuchi]], {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamayori-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タマヨリビメ|1=Tamayori-hime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56344982}}, [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]]
== History ==
Originally, Mukō Shrine consisted of two separate shrines on Mukō Mountain: {{ill|Muka Shrine|ja|向神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muka Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (U11er Shrine) and {{ill|Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Honoikazuchi Shrine|ja|火雷神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honoikazuchi Shrine|qq=}} (Lower Shrine). Both are ancient shrines mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, with Honoikazuchi Shrine being one of the candidates for the {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} "Otokuni-zazuchi Shrine" (other candidates include {{ill|Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Sumiyagashira Shrine|ja|角宮神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyagashira Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Nagaokakyō, Kyoto|Nagaokakyō]]).
Muka Shrine is believed to have originated when Toshigami (Mukō Kami) stayed on Mukō Mountain and promoted rice cultivation. Initially, the shrine was located atop the {{ill|Itsukahara Kofun|lt=Itsukahara Kofun|lt=Gotsukahara Kofun|ja|五塚原古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gotsukahara Kofun|qq=}}, 500 meters north of its current location. In 718 (2nd year of [[:en:Yōrō|Yōrō]]), the shrine was relocated to its current position south of the {{ill|Motoinari Kofun|lt=Motoinari Kofun|lt=Moto-Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Inari Kofun|qq=}}.
Honoikazuchi Shrine is said to have been established when Emperor Jimmu enshrined the deity Honoikazuchi during his journey from [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] to {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|山背国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashiro Province|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 山城国|1=Yamashiro Province|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q749276}}. It is also believed to have been founded around 518 (12th year of [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]]), when Keitai built the {{ill|Otokuni Palace|ja|乙訓宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otokuni Palace|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In 718, Honoikazuchi Shrine incorporated Tamayorihime and Emperor Jimmu as deities.
In 784 (3rd year of [[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]]), the establishment of [[:en:Nagaoka-kyō|Nagaoka-kyō]] brought the imperial palace, Nagaoka-no-miya, to the eastern foot of Muka Shrine.
The chief priest of Honoikazuchi Shrine joined Emperor Go-Toba's forces during the [[:en:Jōkyū War|Jōkyū Rebellion]] in 1221 but later fled to the {{ill|Rokuto-no-shō|ja|六人部荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokuto-no-shō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} estate in present-day [[:en:Fukuchiyama, Kyoto|Fukuchiyama]]. After returning, the shrine's influence waned, and in 1275 (1st year of [[:en:Kenji (era)|Kenji]]), Muka Shrine merged with Honoikazuchi Shrine, forming Mukō Shrine and becoming the area's guardian shrine.
The current main hall was rebuilt in 1422 (29th year of [[:en:Ōei|Ōei]]) with the cooperation of seven surrounding villages. During the Muromachi period, Mukō Shrine became a rallying point for local {{ill|Tsuchi Ikki|ja|土一揆|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchi Ikki|qq=}} peasant uprisings before they marched on Kyoto.
In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine.
In 1961 (36th year of [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]]), the surrounding hill was partially excavated for the construction of the [[:en:Meishin Expressway|Meishin Expressway]]. Today, the shrine and Moto-Inari Kofun remain as a small hill. In recent years, the shrine has been surrounded by a cherry blossom park, partially restoring the area’s former beauty.
The main hall of Tokyo's [[:en:Meiji Shrine|Meiji Shrine]] is modeled after Mukō Shrine's main hall, scaled up by 1.5 times.
== Grounds ==
[[File:Mukō-jinja-Cherry Blossoms of a11roach to a shrine.jpg|thumb|Path lined with cherry blossoms leading to the shrine]] From {{ill|Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|ja|京都府道・大阪府道67号西京高槻線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|1=Kyoto Prefectural Road and Osaka Prefectural Road Route 67|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11375557}} (Astro Street), visitors enter a long, stone-paved path lined with cherry, azalea, and maple trees. The incline is scenic but shared with vehicles, requiring caution.
* Main Hall (Important Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1422 during the Muromachi period. Representative of nagare-zukuri architecture. The model for the Meiji Shrine. ''' Main Hall Shelter (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842 (13th year of [[:en:Tenpō|Tenpō]]). * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842. * North and South Gates (Registered Tangible Cultural Properties) – Built in 1842. * Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1842. * Kagura Hall – Rebuilt in 2003 after the original structure (built in 1625) was lost in a 2002 fire. * Guest Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Relocated from a youth training facility in Osaka around 1965. * Purification Pavilion (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Late 18th-century reconstruction.
=== Auxiliary Shrines === * Ancestral Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1688 (1st year of [[:en:Genroku|Genroku]]). * Five Shrines (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Dedicated to various deities. * Other shrines include {{ill|Goryo Shrine|lt=Goryo Shrine|lt=Goryō Shrine|ja|御霊神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goryō Shrine|qq=}}, [[:en:Tenmangū|Tenmangū]], and Katsuyama Inari Shrine.
== Festivals ==
* festival – Second Sunday in May * Sōmen Festival
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall and five ridge plaques. * Paper manuscript of the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] (Lower Chapter, Age of the Gods) – A 14th-century transcription.
=== Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Multiple structures, including the Main Hall Shelter, North and South Gates, and various auxiliary shrines.
== Access ==
* Nearest station: [[:en:Hankyu Kyoto Line|Hankyu Kyoto Line]] [[Nishimukō Station]], 500 meters northwest.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Mutsume Koreka|ja|六人部是香|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mutsume Koreka|1=Yoshika Mutobe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6537708}} – Late Edo-period scholar and former shrine priest.
== External Links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20190101000000/http://www.geocities.jp/mukojinjahp/index.htm Mukō Shrine Official Website (archived)] * {{Commonscat-inline}}

}} {{osm box|w|48434848}} [[File:Muko-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Mukō Shrine''' (向日神社, Mukō Jinja), also known as '''Mukō Myōjin''', is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in Mukōchō Kitayama, [[:en:Mukō|Mukō]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]]. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} (Myōjin Taisha and small shrine) and a former prefectural shrine.
== Deities ==
* Main deities: [[:en:Toshigami|Toshigami]], [[:en:Yakusanoikazuchi|Yakusanoikazuchi]], {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamayori-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タマヨリビメ|1=Tamayori-hime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56344982}}, [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]]
== History ==
Originally, Mukō Shrine consisted of two separate shrines on Mukō Mountain: {{ill|Muka Shrine|ja|向神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muka Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (U11er Shrine) and {{ill|Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Honoikazuchi Shrine|ja|火雷神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honoikazuchi Shrine|qq=}} (Lower Shrine). Both are ancient shrines mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, with Honoikazuchi Shrine being one of the candidates for the {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} "Otokuni-zazuchi Shrine" (other candidates include {{ill|Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Sumiyagashira Shrine|ja|角宮神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyagashira Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Nagaokakyō, Kyoto|Nagaokakyō]]).
Muka Shrine is believed to have originated when Toshigami (Mukō Kami) stayed on Mukō Mountain and promoted rice cultivation. Initially, the shrine was located atop the {{ill|Itsukahara Kofun|lt=Itsukahara Kofun|lt=Gotsukahara Kofun|ja|五塚原古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gotsukahara Kofun|qq=}}, 500 meters north of its current location. In 718 (2nd year of [[:en:Yōrō|Yōrō]]), the shrine was relocated to its current position south of the {{ill|Motoinari Kofun|lt=Motoinari Kofun|lt=Moto-Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Inari Kofun|qq=}}.
Honoikazuchi Shrine is said to have been established when Emperor Jimmu enshrined the deity Honoikazuchi during his journey from [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] to {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|山背国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashiro Province|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 山城国|1=Yamashiro Province|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q749276}}. It is also believed to have been founded around 518 (12th year of [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]]), when Keitai built the {{ill|Otokuni Palace|ja|乙訓宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otokuni Palace|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In 718, Honoikazuchi Shrine incorporated Tamayorihime and Emperor Jimmu as deities.
In 784 (3rd year of [[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]]), the establishment of [[:en:Nagaoka-kyō|Nagaoka-kyō]] brought the imperial palace, Nagaoka-no-miya, to the eastern foot of Muka Shrine.
The chief priest of Honoikazuchi Shrine joined Emperor Go-Toba's forces during the [[:en:Jōkyū War|Jōkyū Rebellion]] in 1221 but later fled to the {{ill|Rokuto-no-shō|ja|六人部荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokuto-no-shō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} estate in present-day [[:en:Fukuchiyama, Kyoto|Fukuchiyama]]. After returning, the shrine's influence waned, and in 1275 (1st year of [[:en:Kenji (era)|Kenji]]), Muka Shrine merged with Honoikazuchi Shrine, forming Mukō Shrine and becoming the area's guardian shrine.
The current main hall was rebuilt in 1422 (29th year of [[:en:Ōei|Ōei]]) with the cooperation of seven surrounding villages. During the Muromachi period, Mukō Shrine became a rallying point for local {{ill|Tsuchi Ikki|ja|土一揆|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchi Ikki|qq=}} peasant uprisings before they marched on Kyoto.
In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine.
In 1961 (36th year of [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]]), the surrounding hill was partially excavated for the construction of the [[:en:Meishin Expressway|Meishin Expressway]]. Today, the shrine and Moto-Inari Kofun remain as a small hill. In recent years, the shrine has been surrounded by a cherry blossom park, partially restoring the area’s former beauty.
The main hall of Tokyo's [[:en:Meiji Shrine|Meiji Shrine]] is modeled after Mukō Shrine's main hall, scaled up by 1.5 times.
== Grounds ==
[[File:Mukō-jinja-Cherry Blossoms of a11roach to a shrine.jpg|thumb|Path lined with cherry blossoms leading to the shrine]] From {{ill|Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|ja|京都府道・大阪府道67号西京高槻線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|1=Kyoto Prefectural Road and Osaka Prefectural Road Route 67|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11375557}} (Astro Street), visitors enter a long, stone-paved path lined with cherry, azalea, and maple trees. The incline is scenic but shared with vehicles, requiring caution.
* Main Hall (Important Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1422 during the Muromachi period. Representative of nagare-zukuri architecture. The model for the Meiji Shrine. ''' Main Hall Shelter (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842 (13th year of [[:en:Tenpō|Tenpō]]). * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842. * North and South Gates (Registered Tangible Cultural Properties) – Built in 1842. * Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1842. * Kagura Hall – Rebuilt in 2003 after the original structure (built in 1625) was lost in a 2002 fire. * Guest Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Relocated from a youth training facility in Osaka around 1965. * Purification Pavilion (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Late 18th-century reconstruction.
=== Auxiliary Shrines === * Ancestral Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1688 (1st year of [[:en:Genroku|Genroku]]). * Five Shrines (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Dedicated to various deities. * Other shrines include {{ill|Goryo Shrine|lt=Goryo Shrine|lt=Goryō Shrine|ja|御霊神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goryō Shrine|qq=}}, [[:en:Tenmangū|Tenmangū]], and Katsuyama Inari Shrine.

* festival – Second Sunday in May * Sōmen Festival
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall and five ridge plaques. * Paper manuscript of the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] (Lower Chapter, Age of the Gods) – A 14th-century transcription.
=== Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Multiple structures, including the Main Hall Shelter, North and South Gates, and various auxiliary shrines.

* Nearest station: [[:en:Hankyu Kyoto Line|Hankyu Kyoto Line]] [[Nishimukō Station]], 500 meters northwest.

Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} and small shrine), former {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|府社|1=Metropolitan Shrine|WD=Q134917326}}{{efn|lt=Fu-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} | Main hall = Three-bay nagare-zukuri (Important Cultural Property) | festival = Second Sunday in May | Religious rituals = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Muko-jinja, shaden.jpg | location = {{ill|Muko|WD=Q850640}} | coordinates = {{Coord|34.944111|N|135.697111|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Toshigami|WD=Q3028663}} }} {{osm box|w|48434848}} [[File:Muko-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Mukō Shrine''' (向日神社, Mukō Jinja), also known as '''Mukō Myōjin''', is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in Mukōchō Kitayama, [[:en:Mukō|Mukō]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]]. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} (Myōjin Taisha and small shrine) and a former prefectural shrine.
== Deities ==
* Main deities: [[:en:Toshigami|Toshigami]], [[:en:Yakusanoikazuchi|Yakusanoikazuchi]], {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamayori-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タマヨリビメ|1=Tamayori-hime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56344982}}, [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]]
== History ==
Originally, Mukō Shrine consisted of two separate shrines on Mukō Mountain: {{ill|Muka Shrine|ja|向神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muka Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (U11er Shrine) and {{ill|Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Honoikazuchi Shrine|ja|火雷神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honoikazuchi Shrine|qq=}} (Lower Shrine). Both are ancient shrines mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, with Honoikazuchi Shrine being one of the candidates for the {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} "Otokuni-zazuchi Shrine" (other candidates include {{ill|Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Sumiyagashira Shrine|ja|角宮神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyagashira Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Nagaokakyō, Kyoto|Nagaokakyō]]).
Muka Shrine is believed to have originated when Toshigami (Mukō Kami) stayed on Mukō Mountain and promoted rice cultivation. Initially, the shrine was located atop the {{ill|Itsukahara Kofun|lt=Itsukahara Kofun|lt=Gotsukahara Kofun|ja|五塚原古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gotsukahara Kofun|qq=}}, 500 meters north of its current location. In 718 (2nd year of [[:en:Yōrō|Yōrō]]), the shrine was relocated to its current position south of the {{ill|Motoinari Kofun|lt=Motoinari Kofun|lt=Moto-Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Inari Kofun|qq=}}.
Honoikazuchi Shrine is said to have been established when Emperor Jimmu enshrined the deity Honoikazuchi during his journey from [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] to {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|山背国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashiro Province|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 山城国|1=Yamashiro Province|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q749276}}. It is also believed to have been founded around 518 (12th year of [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]]), when Keitai built the {{ill|Otokuni Palace|ja|乙訓宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otokuni Palace|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In 718, Honoikazuchi Shrine incorporated Tamayorihime and Emperor Jimmu as deities.
In 784 (3rd year of [[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]]), the establishment of [[:en:Nagaoka-kyō|Nagaoka-kyō]] brought the imperial palace, Nagaoka-no-miya, to the eastern foot of Muka Shrine.
The chief priest of Honoikazuchi Shrine joined Emperor Go-Toba's forces during the [[:en:Jōkyū War|Jōkyū Rebellion]] in 1221 but later fled to the {{ill|Rokuto-no-shō|ja|六人部荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokuto-no-shō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} estate in present-day [[:en:Fukuchiyama, Kyoto|Fukuchiyama]]. After returning, the shrine's influence waned, and in 1275 (1st year of [[:en:Kenji (era)|Kenji]]), Muka Shrine merged with Honoikazuchi Shrine, forming Mukō Shrine and becoming the area's guardian shrine.
The current main hall was rebuilt in 1422 (29th year of [[:en:Ōei|Ōei]]) with the cooperation of seven surrounding villages. During the Muromachi period, Mukō Shrine became a rallying point for local {{ill|Tsuchi Ikki|ja|土一揆|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchi Ikki|qq=}} peasant uprisings before they marched on Kyoto.
In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine.
In 1961 (36th year of [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]]), the surrounding hill was partially excavated for the construction of the [[:en:Meishin Expressway|Meishin Expressway]]. Today, the shrine and Moto-Inari Kofun remain as a small hill. In recent years, the shrine has been surrounded by a cherry blossom park, partially restoring the area’s former beauty.
The main hall of Tokyo's [[:en:Meiji Shrine|Meiji Shrine]] is modeled after Mukō Shrine's main hall, scaled up by 1.5 times.
== Grounds ==
[[File:Mukō-jinja-Cherry Blossoms of a11roach to a shrine.jpg|thumb|Path lined with cherry blossoms leading to the shrine]] From {{ill|Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|ja|京都府道・大阪府道67号西京高槻線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|1=Kyoto Prefectural Road and Osaka Prefectural Road Route 67|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11375557}} (Astro Street), visitors enter a long, stone-paved path lined with cherry, azalea, and maple trees. The incline is scenic but shared with vehicles, requiring caution.
* Main Hall (Important Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1422 during the Muromachi period. Representative of nagare-zukuri architecture. The model for the Meiji Shrine. ''' Main Hall Shelter (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842 (13th year of [[:en:Tenpō|Tenpō]]). * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842. * North and South Gates (Registered Tangible Cultural Properties) – Built in 1842. * Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1842. * Kagura Hall – Rebuilt in 2003 after the original structure (built in 1625) was lost in a 2002 fire. * Guest Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Relocated from a youth training facility in Osaka around 1965. * Purification Pavilion (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Late 18th-century reconstruction.
=== Auxiliary Shrines === * Ancestral Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1688 (1st year of [[:en:Genroku|Genroku]]). * Five Shrines (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Dedicated to various deities. * Other shrines include {{ill|Goryo Shrine|lt=Goryo Shrine|lt=Goryō Shrine|ja|御霊神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goryō Shrine|qq=}}, [[:en:Tenmangū|Tenmangū]], and Katsuyama Inari Shrine.
== Festivals ==
* festival – Second Sunday in May * Sōmen Festival
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall and five ridge plaques. * Paper manuscript of the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] (Lower Chapter, Age of the Gods) – A 14th-century transcription.
=== Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Multiple structures, including the Main Hall Shelter, North and South Gates, and various auxiliary shrines.
== Access ==
* Nearest station: [[:en:Hankyu Kyoto Line|Hankyu Kyoto Line]] [[Nishimukō Station]], 500 meters northwest.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Mutsume Koreka|ja|六人部是香|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mutsume Koreka|1=Yoshika Mutobe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6537708}} – Late Edo-period scholar and former shrine priest.
== External Links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20190101000000/http://www.geocities.jp/mukojinjahp/index.htm Mukō Shrine Official Website (archived)] * {{Commonscat-inline}}

Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} and small shrine), former {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|府社|1=Metropolitan Shrine|WD=Q134917326}}{{efn|lt=Fu-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} | Main hall = Three-bay nagare-zukuri (Important Cultural Property) | festival = Second Sunday in May | Religious rituals = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Muko-jinja, shaden.jpg | location = {{ill|Muko|WD=Q850640}} | coordinates = {{Coord|34.944111|N|135.697111|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Toshigami|WD=Q3028663}} }} {{osm box|w|48434848}} [[File:Muko-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Mukō Shrine''' (向日神社, Mukō Jinja), also known as '''Mukō Myōjin''', is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in Mukōchō Kitayama, [[:en:Mukō|Mukō]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]]. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} (Myōjin Taisha and small shrine) and a former prefectural shrine.
== Deities ==
* Main deities: [[:en:Toshigami|Toshigami]], [[:en:Yakusanoikazuchi|Yakusanoikazuchi]], {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamayori-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タマヨリビメ|1=Tamayori-hime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56344982}}, [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]]
== History ==
Originally, Mukō Shrine consisted of two separate shrines on Mukō Mountain: {{ill|Muka Shrine|ja|向神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muka Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (U11er Shrine) and {{ill|Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Honoikazuchi Shrine|ja|火雷神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honoikazuchi Shrine|qq=}} (Lower Shrine). Both are ancient shrines mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, with Honoikazuchi Shrine being one of the candidates for the {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} "Otokuni-zazuchi Shrine" (other candidates include {{ill|Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Sumiyagashira Shrine|ja|角宮神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyagashira Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Nagaokakyō, Kyoto|Nagaokakyō]]).
Muka Shrine is believed to have originated when Toshigami (Mukō Kami) stayed on Mukō Mountain and promoted rice cultivation. Initially, the shrine was located atop the {{ill|Itsukahara Kofun|lt=Itsukahara Kofun|lt=Gotsukahara Kofun|ja|五塚原古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gotsukahara Kofun|qq=}}, 500 meters north of its current location. In 718 (2nd year of [[:en:Yōrō|Yōrō]]), the shrine was relocated to its current position south of the {{ill|Motoinari Kofun|lt=Motoinari Kofun|lt=Moto-Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Inari Kofun|qq=}}.
Honoikazuchi Shrine is said to have been established when Emperor Jimmu enshrined the deity Honoikazuchi during his journey from [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] to {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|山背国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashiro Province|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 山城国|1=Yamashiro Province|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q749276}}. It is also believed to have been founded around 518 (12th year of [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]]), when Keitai built the {{ill|Otokuni Palace|ja|乙訓宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otokuni Palace|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In 718, Honoikazuchi Shrine incorporated Tamayorihime and Emperor Jimmu as deities.
In 784 (3rd year of [[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]]), the establishment of [[:en:Nagaoka-kyō|Nagaoka-kyō]] brought the imperial palace, Nagaoka-no-miya, to the eastern foot of Muka Shrine.
The chief priest of Honoikazuchi Shrine joined Emperor Go-Toba's forces during the [[:en:Jōkyū War|Jōkyū Rebellion]] in 1221 but later fled to the {{ill|Rokuto-no-shō|ja|六人部荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokuto-no-shō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} estate in present-day [[:en:Fukuchiyama, Kyoto|Fukuchiyama]]. After returning, the shrine's influence waned, and in 1275 (1st year of [[:en:Kenji (era)|Kenji]]), Muka Shrine merged with Honoikazuchi Shrine, forming Mukō Shrine and becoming the area's guardian shrine.
The current main hall was rebuilt in 1422 (29th year of [[:en:Ōei|Ōei]]) with the cooperation of seven surrounding villages. During the Muromachi period, Mukō Shrine became a rallying point for local {{ill|Tsuchi Ikki|ja|土一揆|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchi Ikki|qq=}} peasant uprisings before they marched on Kyoto.
In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine.
In 1961 (36th year of [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]]), the surrounding hill was partially excavated for the construction of the [[:en:Meishin Expressway|Meishin Expressway]]. Today, the shrine and Moto-Inari Kofun remain as a small hill. In recent years, the shrine has been surrounded by a cherry blossom park, partially restoring the area’s former beauty.
The main hall of Tokyo's [[:en:Meiji Shrine|Meiji Shrine]] is modeled after Mukō Shrine's main hall, scaled up by 1.5 times.
== Grounds ==
[[File:Mukō-jinja-Cherry Blossoms of a11roach to a shrine.jpg|thumb|Path lined with cherry blossoms leading to the shrine]] From {{ill|Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|ja|京都府道・大阪府道67号西京高槻線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|1=Kyoto Prefectural Road and Osaka Prefectural Road Route 67|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11375557}} (Astro Street), visitors enter a long, stone-paved path lined with cherry, azalea, and maple trees. The incline is scenic but shared with vehicles, requiring caution.
* Main Hall (Important Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1422 during the Muromachi period. Representative of nagare-zukuri architecture. The model for the Meiji Shrine. ''' Main Hall Shelter (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842 (13th year of [[:en:Tenpō|Tenpō]]). * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842. * North and South Gates (Registered Tangible Cultural Properties) – Built in 1842. * Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1842. * Kagura Hall – Rebuilt in 2003 after the original structure (built in 1625) was lost in a 2002 fire. * Guest Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Relocated from a youth training facility in Osaka around 1965. * Purification Pavilion (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Late 18th-century reconstruction.
=== Auxiliary Shrines === * Ancestral Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1688 (1st year of [[:en:Genroku|Genroku]]). * Five Shrines (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Dedicated to various deities. * Other shrines include {{ill|Goryo Shrine|lt=Goryo Shrine|lt=Goryō Shrine|ja|御霊神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goryō Shrine|qq=}}, [[:en:Tenmangū|Tenmangū]], and Katsuyama Inari Shrine.
== Festivals ==
* festival – Second Sunday in May * Sōmen Festival
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall and five ridge plaques. * Paper manuscript of the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] (Lower Chapter, Age of the Gods) – A 14th-century transcription.
=== Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Multiple structures, including the Main Hall Shelter, North and South Gates, and various auxiliary shrines.
== Access ==
* Nearest station: [[:en:Hankyu Kyoto Line|Hankyu Kyoto Line]] [[Nishimukō Station]], 500 meters northwest.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Mutsume Koreka|ja|六人部是香|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mutsume Koreka|1=Yoshika Mutobe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6537708}} – Late Edo-period scholar and former shrine priest.
== External Links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20190101000000/http://www.geocities.jp/mukojinjahp/index.htm Mukō Shrine Official Website (archived)] * {{Commonscat-inline}}

torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Mukō Shrine''' (向日神社, Mukō Jinja), also known as '''Mukō Myōjin''', is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in Mukōchō Kitayama, {{ill|Mukō|en|Mukō|WD=Q850640|lt=Mukō}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} (Myōjin Taisha and small shrine) and a former prefectural shrine.
== Deities ==
* Main deities: {{ill|Toshigami|en|Toshigami|WD=Q3028663|lt=Toshigami}}, {{ill|Yakusanoikazuchi|en|Yakusanoikazuchi|WD=Q10892534|lt=Yakusanoikazuchi}}, {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamayori-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タマヨリビメ|1=Tamayori-hime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56344982}}, {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu}}
== History ==
Originally, Mukō Shrine consisted of two separate shrines on Mukō Mountain: {{ill|Muka Shrine|ja|向神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muka Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (U11er Shrine) and {{ill|Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Honoikazuchi Shrine|ja|火雷神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honoikazuchi Shrine|qq=}} (Lower Shrine). Both are ancient shrines mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, with Honoikazuchi Shrine being one of the candidates for the {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} "Otokuni-zazuchi Shrine" (other candidates include {{ill|Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Suminomiya Shrine|lt=Sumiyagashira Shrine|ja|角宮神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyagashira Shrine|qq=}} in {{ill|Nagaokakyō, Kyoto|en|Nagaokakyō, Kyoto|WD=Q861246|lt=Nagaokakyō}}).
Muka Shrine is believed to have originated when Toshigami (Mukō Kami) stayed on Mukō Mountain and promoted rice cultivation. Initially, the shrine was located atop the {{ill|Itsukahara Kofun|lt=Itsukahara Kofun|lt=Gotsukahara Kofun|ja|五塚原古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gotsukahara Kofun|qq=}}, 500 meters north of its current location. In 718 (2nd year of {{ill|Yōrō|en|Yōrō|WD=Q1373061|lt=Yōrō}}), the shrine was relocated to its current position south of the {{ill|Motoinari Kofun|lt=Motoinari Kofun|lt=Moto-Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Inari Kofun|qq=}}.
Honoikazuchi Shrine is said to have been established when Emperor Jimmu enshrined the deity Honoikazuchi during his journey from {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province}} to {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|山背国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashiro Province|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 山城国|1=Yamashiro Province|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q749276}}. It is also believed to have been founded around 518 (12th year of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|en|Emperor Keitai|WD=Q349277|lt=Emperor Keitai}}), when Keitai built the {{ill|Otokuni Palace|ja|乙訓宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otokuni Palace|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In 718, Honoikazuchi Shrine incorporated Tamayorihime and Emperor Jimmu as deities.
In 784 (3rd year of {{ill|Enryaku|en|Enryaku|WD=Q1373176|lt=Enryaku}}), the establishment of {{ill|Nagaoka-kyō|en|Nagaoka-kyō|WD=Q1052803|lt=Nagaoka-kyō}} brought the imperial palace, Nagaoka-no-miya, to the eastern foot of Muka Shrine.
The chief priest of Honoikazuchi Shrine joined Emperor Go-Toba's forces during the {{ill|Jōkyū War|en|Jōkyū War|WD=Q1372308|lt=Jōkyū Rebellion}} in 1221 but later fled to the {{ill|Rokuto-no-shō|ja|六人部荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokuto-no-shō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} estate in present-day {{ill|Fukuchiyama, Kyoto|en|Fukuchiyama, Kyoto|WD=Q3355306|lt=Fukuchiyama}}. After returning, the shrine's influence waned, and in 1275 (1st year of {{ill|Kenji (era)|en|Kenji (era)|WD=Q1327993|lt=Kenji}}), Muka Shrine merged with Honoikazuchi Shrine, forming Mukō Shrine and becoming the area's guardian shrine.
The current main hall was rebuilt in 1422 (29th year of {{ill|Ōei|en|Ōei|WD=Q1193078|lt=Ōei}}) with the cooperation of seven surrounding villages. During the Muromachi period, Mukō Shrine became a rallying point for local {{ill|Tsuchi Ikki|ja|土一揆|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchi Ikki|qq=}} peasant uprisings before they marched on Kyoto.
In the Meiji period, the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine.
In 1961 (36th year of {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa}}), the surrounding hill was partially excavated for the construction of the {{ill|Meishin Expressway|en|Meishin Expressway|WD=Q904481|lt=Meishin Expressway}}. Today, the shrine and Moto-Inari Kofun remain as a small hill. In recent years, the shrine has been surrounded by a cherry blossom park, partially restoring the area’s former beauty.
The main hall of Tokyo's {{ill|Meiji Shrine|en|Meiji Shrine|WD=Q287165|lt=Meiji Shrine}} is modeled after Mukō Shrine's main hall, scaled up by 1.5 times.
== Grounds ==
[[File:Mukō-jinja-Cherry Blossoms of a11roach to a shrine.jpg|thumb|Path lined with cherry blossoms leading to the shrine]] From {{ill|Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|ja|京都府道・大阪府道67号西京高槻線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Prefectural Route 67|1=Kyoto Prefectural Road and Osaka Prefectural Road Route 67|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11375557}} (Astro Street), visitors enter a long, stone-paved path lined with cherry, azalea, and maple trees. The incline is scenic but shared with vehicles, requiring caution.
* Main Hall (Important Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1422 during the Muromachi period. Representative of nagare-zukuri architecture. The model for the Meiji Shrine. ''' Main Hall Shelter (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842 (13th year of {{ill|Tenpō|en|Tenpō|WD=Q1369045|lt=Tenpō}}). * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Rebuilt in 1842. * North and South Gates (Registered Tangible Cultural Properties) – Built in 1842. * Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1842. * Kagura Hall – Rebuilt in 2003 after the original structure (built in 1625) was lost in a 2002 fire. * Guest Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Relocated from a youth training facility in Osaka around 1965. * Purification Pavilion (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Late 18th-century reconstruction.
=== Auxiliary Shrines === * Ancestral Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Built in 1688 (1st year of {{ill|Genroku|en|Genroku|WD=Q1325001|lt=Genroku}}). * Five Shrines (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) – Dedicated to various deities. * Other shrines include {{ill|Goryo Shrine|lt=Goryo Shrine|lt=Goryō Shrine|ja|御霊神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goryō Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Tenmangū|en|Tenmangū|WD=Q798838|lt=Tenmangū}}, and Katsuyama Inari Shrine.
== Festivals ==
* festival – Second Sunday in May * Sōmen Festival
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall and five ridge plaques. * Paper manuscript of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}} (Lower Chapter, Age of the Gods) – A 14th-century transcription.
=== Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Multiple structures, including the Main Hall Shelter, North and South Gates, and various auxiliary shrines.
== Access ==
* Nearest station: [[:en:Hankyu Kyoto Line|Hankyu Kyoto Line]] [[Nishimukō Station]], 500 meters northwest.

{{ill|Yakusanoikazuchi|en|Yakusanoikazuchi|WD=Q10892534|lt=Honoikazuchi no Ōkami}}, {{ill|Tamayori-hime|ja|玉依姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamayori-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} and small shrine), former {{ill|Modern system
